title: @node: error messages point to construct_from_module call site, not @node decoration site
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness).

## Problem

When `construct_from_module` raises `ConstructError` for an invalid @node function (unknown parameter, self-dependency, cycle, fan-in type mismatch from neograph-8pt), the error's source location — rendered by `_source_location` in `_construct_validation.py` — points to the `construct_from_module(mod)` CALL SITE, not to the offending `@node`-decorated function.

This makes debugging painful in a module with dozens of @node functions:

    ConstructError: @node 'summarize' parameter 'max_items' does not match any @node in module 'my_pipeline'.
      available @nodes: [...]
      at my_pipeline.py:287    ← this is the `pipeline = construct_from_module(...)` line,
                                  NOT where `summarize` is defined

The user has to grep for `def summarize` to find the actual offender.

## Proposed fix

At `@node` decoration time, capture the function's source location via `fn.__code__.co_filename` and `fn.__code__.co_firstlineno`, and stash them in the sidecar alongside `fn` and `param_names`:

    _node_sidecar[id(n)] = (fn, param_names, fn.__code__.co_filename, fn.__code__.co_firstlineno)

When `construct_from_module` raises an error involving a specific @node, include that location in the error message:

    ConstructError: @node 'summarize' parameter 'max_items' does not match any @node ...
      available @nodes: [...]
      @node defined at my_pipeline.py:42        ← the actual function definition
      construct_from_module called at my_pipeline.py:287

Format: use `os.path.basename(filename)` for consistency with the existing `_source_location` output.

Apply to all construct_from_module errors that name a specific @node:
- Unknown parameter name
- Self-dependency
- Cycle detection (name the first-visited node in the cycle)
- (After neograph-8pt) Fan-in type mismatch
- (After neograph-q54) Any decoration-time error from the @node decorator itself already has a clean traceback since it raises during class loading — no change needed there

## Acceptance criteria

- Extend `_register_sidecar` signature: `_register_sidecar(n, f, param_names, source_file, source_line)`.
- Extend the sidecar tuple to include source location.
- New helper `_get_source_location(n: Node) -> tuple[str, int] | None` returning the stashed location.
- All `ConstructError` raises in `construct_from_module` that name a specific node include `at <basename>:<line>` for that node's definition.
- Update the existing `test_node_decorator_unknown_param_raises` to assert the error message contains both the function's source filename and a line number.
- New test `test_node_decorator_cycle_error_includes_source_location`: construct a two-@node cycle and assert both source locations appear.
- New test `test_node_decorator_error_filename_is_basename`: verify we emit `module.py:N` not an absolute path.
- Full suite stays green.

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/decorators.py:65-78` — sidecar registration
- `src/neograph/_construct_validation.py:_source_location, _location_suffix` — existing location-formatting conventions
- `inspect` / `fn.__code__` attributes reference

## Non-goals

- Do NOT edit `_construct_validation.py` to change its walker (it still uses `sys._getframe` for construct.__init__ errors)
- Do NOT try to capture the source of inline-defined functions (test helpers, lambdas) — falling back to `<string>` is fine
close_reason: Wave 6: three parallel agents. 3hk: error source location via fn.__code__ (no sidecar changes), threaded into 4 ConstructError raises in construct_from_module + _validate_fan_in_types, 3 tests. jtz: collision detection in construct_from_module walker loop + cross-module composition test, 3 tests. lkn: example 06 + TestRawNode migrated to @node(mode='raw'), @raw_node deleted from node.py + __init__.py (zero remaining API-level hits). 159/159 passing.

title: Mutable default dict on Node.llm_config and Tool.config
description: Node(llm_config: dict = {}) and Tool(config: dict = {}) use mutable defaults. Should use Field(default_factory=dict). Pydantic v2 handles this safely but it's bad practice. Source: code review MED-05, PP-01.

title: Send Pydantic ValidationError details back to LLM on retry
description:
When _parse_json_response fails and we retry, the retry message is generic: 'could not be parsed as valid JSON.' Instructor sends the exact Pydantic field errors back to the LLM ('age: value is not a valid integer, got twenty-five'), which lets the LLM fix the specific problem.

## Current behavior
_invoke_json_with_retry catches ExecutionError (which wraps ValidationError) and sends:
'Your previous response could not be parsed as valid JSON. Respond with ONLY the JSON object.'

## Desired behavior
Catch ValidationError separately in _parse_json_response (or propagate it through ExecutionError). Format the field-level errors into the retry message:
'Your response failed validation for EmailDraft:
- score: value is not a valid float, got "high"
- email_number: field required
Respond with a corrected JSON object.'

## Implementation
1. In _parse_json_response: catch pydantic.ValidationError separately from other exceptions. Include the formatted error string in the ExecutionError (or a new ParseError subclass).
2. In _invoke_json_with_retry: extract the validation details from the caught error and include them in the retry message instead of the generic text.

## Files
- src/neograph/_llm.py: _parse_json_response, _invoke_json_with_retry, _RETRY_MSG
- tests/test_pipeline_modes.py: test that the retry message includes field-level errors
close_reason: ValidationError field details sent to LLM on retry

title: forward: symbolic-proxy tracer for straight-line forward()
description:
Implement symbolic-proxy tracing for `ForwardConstruct.forward()` so the nodes list is populated in **call order** (as observed during a traced run of `forward()`) rather than class-declaration order. This is the core of the torch.fx-style strategy chosen in the spike.

## Context

- Depends on the previous subtask (ForwardConstruct base + discovery).
- Strategy and rationale: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md` — "Chosen strategy: Symbolic Proxy" and "How the Proxy type works".
- Working reference: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/spike.py` — `Tracer`, `Proxy`, `_NodeCall`, `_ForwardSelf`.

## Files

- `src/neograph/forward.py` — add tracing machinery alongside `ForwardConstruct`.
- `tests/test_forward_construct_tracing.py` — new test file.

## Signatures

```python
# src/neograph/forward.py
class Proxy:
    """Stand-in value during tracing. v1: provenance only (source node + name).
    Comparison / attr-access dunders are intentionally NOT added in this
    subtask — they land with the conditional-branch subtask.
    """
    _neo_source: Node | None
    _neo_name: str

class _NodeCall:
    """Callable wrapper: recording forward() sees when it does self.<node>(...)."""
    def __call__(self, *args, **kwargs) -> Proxy: ...

class Tracer:
    def record(self, node: Node) -> None: ...
    @property
    def nodes(self) -> list[Node]: ...

class _ForwardSelf:
    """Replacement `self` during tracing. __getattr__ returns _NodeCall for
    known node names, falls through to the real instance otherwise."""
```

`ForwardConstruct.__init__` now:
1. Calls `_discover_node_attrs()` to get `dict[str, Node]`.
2. Builds a `Tracer` and a `_ForwardSelf` shim.
3. Invokes `type(self).forward(shim, Proxy(source_node=None, name='forward_input'))` — unbound call so the shim plays `self`.
4. Passes `tracer.nodes` as the nodes list to `super().__init__(...)`.
5. If forward() is not overridden, raise as before.

## Acceptance criteria

1. A `Pipeline` whose `forward()` calls `self.a(x)` then `self.b(...)` produces `nodes == [a, b]` where `nodes[0] is Pipeline.a`, `nodes[1] is Pipeline.b`.
2. Calling `self.a(x)` where `a` does NOT exist on the class raises `AttributeError` with a clear message naming `a` and listing the available nodes.
3. A node attribute that isn't used in `forward()` is NOT included (tracing is call-driven, not declaration-driven — this is the whole point over subtask 1).
4. Repeated calls to the same Node instance in `forward()` collapse into one entry (document this in the module docstring and the `Tracer.record` comment; loop unrolling is a later subtask).
5. Non-Node attributes (plain helpers on the subclass) are still reachable from forward() via the shim's fallthrough `__getattr__`.
6. `forward()` receiving multiple positional args still works — each arg gets its own seed `Proxy`.
7. Tests cover: call-order vs declaration-order divergence, unused node exclusion, unknown-attr error, multi-arg forward, fallthrough for helper methods.

## Out of scope

- `Proxy.__bool__`, `__lt__`, `__getattr__` for attribute chains, `__iter__` — all branching/looping subtasks.
- Argument-to-producer wiring. `_NodeCall` IGNORES its args for this subtask (documented in spike open question #1). Fan-in DAGs are a follow-up.
- Any compiler changes.
close_reason: Straight-line ForwardConstruct MVP complete. di0: base class with node discovery via __mro__ walk + Pydantic ignored_types fix. 3us: symbolic proxy tracer (_Proxy + _Tracer + _ForwardSelf + _NodeCall) wired into ForwardConstruct.__init__. vxv: zero compiler changes — tracer output is a plain nodes list that compile()/state-builder/factory consume unchanged. 10 new tests across 3 classes. 169/169 passing.

title: Subgraph state model loses type info for context=[...] fields, breaking msgpack allowlist
description:
## Symptom

User of NeoGraph (piarch derive_ensemble) hits checkpoint deserialization failure when running a Construct that uses subconstructs with cross-graph context dependencies:

    Blocked deserialization of derive_ensemble.constructs._shared.GraphContextBundle
    - not in allowed_msgpack_modules. Add to allowed_msgpack_modules to allow:
    [('derive_ensemble.constructs._shared', 'GraphContextBundle')]

Reproducer (in piarch context):
- rw_ingestion construct has node 'build_catalog' with @node(outputs=GraphContextBundle)
- verify_claim subconstruct has nodes that use context=['build_catalog']
- verify_claim is composed via .map(...) into rw_ingestion
- Parent construct's state model correctly includes 'build_catalog: GraphContextBundle | None'
- Parent allowlist correctly contains ('derive_ensemble.constructs._shared', 'GraphContextBundle')

But:
- Subconstruct's compiled state model has 'build_catalog: typing.Any'  ← type info lost
- build_serde_allowlist(schemas=[subgraph_state_model]) cannot find GraphContextBundle
- Subgraph's checkpointer rejects the GraphContextBundle on read

## Root cause

In compile_state_model for a subconstruct, fields named in nodes' context=[...] arguments are typed as Any (a sensible default since the subconstruct doesn't know the parent's types in isolation). But that erasure breaks the msgpack allowlist build.

## Fix options

(a) When a Construct is used INSIDE another via .map() or similar, propagate the parent's field types into the subconstruct's state model — replace 'typing.Any' with the concrete type from the outer schema.

(b) When _register_msgpack_types is called for a subgraph, walk the parent construct hierarchy and merge their allowlists into the subgraph's serde.

(c) Add an explicit context_types parameter on @node decorator: context=['build_catalog'], context_types={'build_catalog': GraphContextBundle}. Manual but reliable.

Recommendation: (a) is best (no API change for users); (b) is acceptable as a stopgap.

## Workaround for piarch user
Delete the checkpoint DB before re-running. Forces fresh start. Loses --redo-from savings.

## Acceptance
Test: a Construct with a subconstruct that uses context=[<parent_node>] roundtrips a checkpoint cleanly. The parent_node's output type is in the subgraph's serde allowlist.
close_reason: Fixed: compile_state_model takes context_types from parent, subconstruct context fields get concrete types instead of Any. Fixes msgpack allowlist.

title: UX: mixing @node + Construct sub-pipelines + construct_from_functions is too many surfaces
description:
Real-world attempt to use dict-output nodes with tool_log inside a sub-Construct mapped over a collection required:

1. @node for most pipeline nodes (good)
2. Node() declarative for sub-Construct internals (because @node param names don't resolve inside sub-Constructs — the input port is 'neo_subgraph_input', not a named @node)
3. Construct() for the sub-pipeline boundary (input/output ports)
4. .map() on the Construct for Each fan-out
5. construct_from_functions() for the top-level assembly
6. Manual node list splicing to insert the Construct into construct_from_functions output (because construct_from_functions rejects non-@node items)

That's SIX different API surfaces in one pipeline file. The user went from 'write Python functions, framework wires them' to 'juggle three node definition styles, two assembly methods, and manual list surgery.' The API got MORE complex, not simpler.

The specific friction points:

A. @node params resolve by name against other @nodes in the module. Inside a sub-Construct, the input comes from a port (neo_subgraph_input), not a named @node. So @node can't be used for sub-Construct internals — you fall back to Node() declarative. Two styles in one file.

B. construct_from_functions only accepts @node items, not Construct instances. So you can't mix @node functions and sub-Constructs in one construct_from_functions call. You need manual splicing or a different assembly path.

C. dict-output nodes inside Each fan-out + sub-Construct have THREE layers of type wrapping: per-key output → Each dict aggregation → sub-Construct output port. The validator needs to see through all three. Currently it doesn't (neograph-d8w was filed, said fixed, but the @node-level fan-in validator still fails).

Proposal: either make @node work inside sub-Constructs (resolve params against both the sub-Construct input port AND peer @nodes), OR make construct_from_functions accept Construct instances alongside @node items. The user shouldn't need to know about Neo_subgraph_input or manually splice node lists.
notes:
## The use case driving this

piarch's RW match-verify step is a two-phase pattern per claim:

1. **R1 explores** with tools (search_nodes, read_artifact) — ReAct loop, 3-5 tool calls
2. **R1 judges** in a **fresh conversation** — receives only the research_packet (extracted tool results), applies a rubric, produces a structured disposition

Eval showed R1 same-conversation gets 34% accuracy (hedges with tool context noise). Fresh conversation with extracted research_packet gets 75%. The fresh conversation is critical for quality.

In NeoGraph terms, this should be:

    @node(outputs={'result': ExplorationResult, 'tool_log': list[ToolInteraction]},
          mode='gather', model='reason', prompt='match-verify/claim-single',
          tools=[search, read])
    def explore(claim: VerifyClaim) -> ExplorationResult: ...

    @node(outputs=ClusterMatchResult, model='judge', prompt='match-verify/rubric-v2')
    def score(
        explore_result: ExplorationResult,
        explore_tool_log: list[ToolInteraction],
    ) -> ClusterMatchResult: ...

    verify_claim = Construct(
        'verify-claim', input=VerifyClaim, output=ClusterMatchResult,
        nodes=[explore, score],
    ).map('flatten_claims', key='r_id')

Two declarative nodes. Tool context flows typed. Fresh conversation is implicit (score is a separate produce node). Each fans out per claim. Zero scripted code.

What actually happened: six API surfaces, manual node list splicing, validator failures at three different layers, and we gave up and wrote a 30-line scripted node that delegates to the upstream Python function. The framework made it HARDER than hand-coding.

This is the most important pattern to get right — gather→produce with tool context is the core of every 'agent explores then judges' pipeline. If this is awkward, every serious consumer hits the same wall.
## Design Decision: Option A (2026-04-06)

Research compared two approaches:
- **Option A**: construct_from_functions gains input=/output= kwargs. @node functions work inside sub-Constructs via port param resolution. Changes only in decorators.py (~50-70 lines). Eliminates style mixing.
- **Option B**: construct_from_functions accepts Construct instances. Sub-Construct internals still need declarative Node(). Two styles persist.

**Chose Option A.** Eliminates the root cause — the explore→score pattern becomes pure @node. Six API surfaces → two. Layer discipline preserved (decorator layer produces IR lower layers already accept).

5 subtasks filed: API kwarg → port resolution → shim mapping → tests → docs.
close_reason: All 5 subtasks complete. construct_from_functions(input=, output=) enables @node functions inside sub-constructs.

title: _build_construct_from_decorated: port param resolution
description:
When construct_input is set, params whose type matches construct_input are 'port params' — they read from neo_subgraph_input, not from a peer @node.

Changes in _build_construct_from_decorated:
1. Adjacency building (line 956): if pname not in decorated AND type matches construct_input, skip (no peer dependency)
2. Inputs filtering (line 1021): rewrite port param key in node.inputs from param_name to 'neo_subgraph_input'
3. All lower layers already handle neo_subgraph_input — no changes needed outside decorators.py

~30 lines. Respects layer discipline.
design:
## Core Invariant
Port param resolution must produce the same IR (Node with inputs={'neo_subgraph_input': T}) that the declarative path produces, so all lower layers (validator, factory, compiler) behave identically.

## Implementation Plan
1. In _build_construct_from_decorated, identify port params: for each node, walk param_names and check if param type matches construct_input (issubclass). Store in port_params dict.
2. In fan-out detection (L933): exclude port params from fan_out_params.
3. In constant detection (L950): skip port params.
4. In adjacency (L975-998): skip port params (don't wire dependency, don't raise 'unknown param' error).
5. In inputs cleanup (L1040-1057): for port params, rewrite key from param_name to 'neo_subgraph_input' in n.inputs.
6. All changes in _build_construct_from_decorated only. ~20 lines.
notes:
## Findings

### Code locations
- decorators.py:962-998 — adjacency building, this is where 'pname not in decorated' raises ConstructError for unknown params
- decorators.py:1040-1057 — inputs cleanup, rewriting n.inputs dict
- decorators.py:588-597 — inputs_dict built at decoration time from param annotations
- factory.py:408-423 — _extract_input dict-form reads state[field_name.replace('-','_')]

### Mechanism
When construct_input is set, any param whose TYPE (not name) matches construct_input should be:
1. Skipped in adjacency (no peer dependency) — line 980: add check before the 'not in decorated' error
2. Skipped in fan-out detection — line 933: exclude port params from fan_out_params
3. Rewritten in n.inputs at line 1040: change key from param_name to 'neo_subgraph_input'

### Key insight
_extract_input at L414 does state_key = field_name.replace('-','_'). When we rewrite the inputs key to 'neo_subgraph_input', it reads state['neo_subgraph_input'] which is where the sub-construct input port lives (set in factory.py L616). The lower layers handle it correctly — no changes needed outside decorators.py.

### Type matching
Use issubclass(param_type, construct_input) for type comparison. With from __future__ annotations, types are resolved in the resolved_hints dict at L582-584.

### Risks & Edge Cases
- Multiple params matching construct_input: only one should be a port param. If >1 match, ambiguous. Raise ConstructError.
- DI params (FromInput/FromConfig) that also match construct_input type: DI takes priority (already classified before adjacency).
- Fan-out + port param overlap: fan_out_params identification at L933 should exclude port params.
## Architect Review
- [LOW] Type matching via issubclass is correct. Consider that with from __future__ annotations, we need resolved_hints (already computed at decoration time). At _build_construct_from_decorated time, we have the resolved types in n.inputs dict values. Use those, not raw annotations.
- [LOW] Multiple port params: good that we plan to error on ambiguity. One port param per sub-Construct is the right constraint.
- [LOW] Order of operations: port params must be identified BEFORE fan-out detection and constant detection. Currently the plan says this. Good.
- [MEDIUM] The inputs_dict at decoration time (L588) already has resolved types. But _build_construct_from_decorated receives n.inputs as a dict. We need to match the TYPE from n.inputs[pname] against construct_input. issubclass works for class types but not for generic types (list[X], dict[K,V]). Since construct_input is always a BaseModel, issubclass is safe.

Overall: ALL_LOW
## Regression Test
- Test: tests/test_composition.py::TestNodeSubConstruct (3 tests)
- Status: FAILING (expected -- TDD red step)
- Tests expected behavior: @node params whose type matches construct_input resolve as port params reading from neo_subgraph_input

title: Dogfood: migrate no-dep examples (02, 07, 08, 11) to @node
description:
Part of epic neograph-eiw (Dogfood @node). No blockers — uses only current @node + pending neograph-q54 mode inference (soft dep, can work around with explicit mode='produce').

## Scope

Migrate four examples that don't use Oracle / Each / Operator / raw / sub-construct / heavy fan-in:
- `examples/02_produce_and_gather.py`
- `examples/07_llm_configuration.py`
- `examples/08_structured_output_coercion.py`
- `examples/11_pipeline_metadata_and_prompts.py`

## What to do per file

1. Read the existing file. Understand what it demonstrates (look at the docstring).
2. Replace every `Node(...)` / `Node.scripted(...)` with `@node(...)`.
3. Replace `Construct("name", nodes=[...])` with `construct_from_module(sys.modules[__name__], name="name")`.
4. Update the top-of-file docstring to note the @node style and what the example demonstrates.
5. Re-run: `python examples/02_produce_and_gather.py` (etc.) — must produce equivalent output.
6. Preserve example behavior exactly — this is migration, not enhancement. If the declarative version does X, the @node version must do X.

## Acceptance criteria

- All four examples use @node as the sole construction API (no `Node(...)` or `Node.scripted(...)` calls, no `Construct(nodes=[...])`).
- Each example runs end-to-end without errors.
- Each example produces output functionally equivalent to the pre-migration version (stdout comparison, modulo log formatting).
- Example docstrings updated to note the @node pattern.
- No new test files — these are runnable examples.
- Full test suite stays green (running pytest on examples is not part of this, but the existing tests referencing these examples must not break).
- If any migration reveals an @node gap, STOP and file a new subtask on neograph-4mx describing the gap, then resume the other three examples if possible.

## Files to read

- Each target example file
- `examples/01b_scripted_pipeline_decorator.py` — reference @node example pattern
- `src/neograph/decorators.py::node, construct_from_module` — current API

## Non-goals

- Do NOT touch examples 01, 03, 04, 05, 06, 09, 10 — separate subtasks
- Do NOT touch tests
- Do NOT add new functionality; exact-behavior migration only
close_reason: See prior close reasons. Force-closing because the subtask→epic dependency direction was set backwards at creation (subtask depends on epic instead of epic depends on subtask); cleanup will correct the direction across all epic subtasks in a single pass after this commit.

title: Inline prompt support in _compile_prompt
description:
## What
Currently _compile_prompt loads prompt templates from files by name (e.g. prompt='rw/summarize' → loads a .md file). Spec-driven pipelines need inline prompt text — the prompt IS the string, not a file reference.

## Approach
In src/neograph/_llm.py _compile_prompt: detect whether prompt_template is a file reference (contains '/' or registered name) vs inline text (contains spaces or ${} substitutions). Inline text passes through directly with variable substitution applied.

## Variable substitution
Replace ${node} and ${node.field} with rendered input data. Use the same dotted-path resolution as Each.over. input_data is already available at the call site.

## Deliverable
- _compile_prompt handles inline strings with ${} substitution
- Tests: inline prompt renders correctly, file refs still work (backward compat)

title: Self-referencing input without Loop silently compiles
description: Node.scripted('x', inputs={'x': Alpha}, outputs=Alpha) — node references itself as input without a Loop modifier. Silently compiles and runs (reads None from own empty state field). Should raise ConstructError: 'node x references itself as input without Loop modifier'.
close_reason: Validator rejects self-ref inputs without Loop.

description: state.py:63 says 'must declare output type' while state.py:109 says 'has no output type. Every node must declare output=SomeModel.' Adopt the more helpful longer form. Source: code review LOW-04, CON-10.

title: Extract _make_loop_router shared factory from 2 compiler functions
description:
Phase 2b of architecture-v2.md section 1 (Loop Router Unification).

## What
The loop_router closures in _add_loop_back_edge and _add_subgraph_loop are structurally identical. Extract into _make_loop_router(item_name, field_name, count_field, loop, condition, unwrap_fn).

## Where
- compiler.py _add_loop_back_edge: loop_router closure (~line 580-610)
- compiler.py _add_subgraph_loop: loop_router closure (~line 640-680)

The only difference: value unwrap for the condition check. Node path reads own_field, unwraps dict-form primary key. Construct path reads field_name directly.

## How
1. Create _make_loop_router that takes an unwrap_fn callback
2. _add_loop_back_edge calls it with Node-aware unwrap
3. _add_subgraph_loop calls it with Construct-aware unwrap
4. Delete duplicated closures

## Verification
grep -c "loop_router" src/neograph/compiler.py should decrease. All loop tests pass.

title: Epic: @node decorator production readiness
description:
## Why this epic exists

`@node` is THE public construction API for NeoGraph. Every user-facing doc, example, quickstart, and tutorial leads with `@node`. `Node` and `Construct` classes remain importable as the low-level IR but are positioned as "for advanced users": IR unit tests, programmatic/dynamic pipeline construction from external config, the compiler's internal data structures, and sub-constructs until `ForwardConstruct` (neograph-pub epic) lands. Path A from the 2026-04-05 positioning decision — no existing users, no backwards-compat debt, so we reshape aggressively.

This epic exists because `neograph-dur` shipped @node v1 with known gaps between "works for the readme example" and "works as the primary API of a production framework". None of them are about backwards compat — they're about making @node complete enough to express every pipeline pattern the declarative API supports. Once this epic closes, the sibling Dogfood epic rewrites examples + tests + docs to use @node exclusively, which is both validation and documentation update in one stroke.

## Scope — make @node the complete construction surface

@node must express every pipeline pattern the declarative API supports:

- ✓ Scripted linear + fan-in (v1 shipped in neograph-dur)
- Full static type safety across all fan-in parameters (neograph-8pt)
- Fan-out via `map_over=` kwarg (neograph-jyw)
- Mode inference + LLM-dispatch correctness (neograph-q54, neograph-1ty)
- Scalar / run-input / config parameters via `FromInput[T]` / `FromConfig[T]` / defaults (neograph-edr)
- **NEW**: Oracle ensemble via `ensemble_n=` / `merge_fn=` / `merge_prompt=` kwargs
- **NEW**: Operator human-in-loop via `interrupt_when=` kwarg
- **NEW**: Raw-fn escape hatch via `mode='raw'` (replaces `@raw_node`)
- Error messages at decoration site (neograph-3hk)
- Cross-module composition + collision detection (neograph-jtz)
- Fan-in showcase example (neograph-8vt)

Once complete, a new user learning NeoGraph only needs to learn `@node`. They will never open `Construct` or `Node` unless they're writing IR-level tests, generating pipelines programmatically from config, or hitting the sub-construct use case before `ForwardConstruct` ships.

## Subtasks (11)

### P1 correctness/feature blockers
- neograph-8pt — full fan-in parameter type validation
- neograph-jyw — Each/fan-out interop via `map_over=` kwarg
- neograph-q54 — `mode='produce'` default footgun + dead-body warning for LLM modes
- neograph-edr — scalar / run-input / config parameter support
- **NEW** (see below) — Oracle ensemble kwargs on @node
- **NEW** (see below) — Operator interrupt kwarg on @node
- **NEW** (see below) — @node raw-fn mode (replaces @raw_node)

### P2 test + DX
- neograph-1ty — test coverage for produce/gather/execute modes
- neograph-3hk — error source location at @node decoration site
- neograph-8vt — fan-in showcase example
- neograph-jtz — cross-module composition + name-collision detection

## Done definition

All 11 subtasks closed. @node decorator can express every pipeline pattern currently expressible in the declarative API (scripted + produce + gather + execute + Oracle + Each + Operator + raw). Full test coverage across modes. Zero footguns at decoration time. The sibling Dogfood epic rewrites 11 examples + ~8 test classes + README + top-level docstrings to use @node as the sole construction surface — any migration that reveals an @node gap rolls back into this epic as a new subtask.

## Non-goals (explicit)

- `sys.modules[__name__]` auto-discovery / bare `compile(__name__)` ergonomic — deferred to a later DX epic
- Parameterized @node classes / Dagster-style `group_name=` — deferred to the forward() mode epic (neograph-pub)
- Removing `Node`/`Construct` from the public API (Path B from the positioning decision) — reconsidered after Dogfood completes with migration data

## Source

Positioning decision 2026-04-05 + senior-dev review of `src/neograph/decorators.py` from neograph-dur closure.
close_reason: ALL 11 SUBTASKS CLOSED. @node production readiness epic complete: fan-in validation (8pt), Each interop via map_over (jyw), mode inference (q54), scalar params via FromInput/FromConfig (edr), Oracle kwargs (nqe), Operator kwargs (6y3), raw mode (t5z), LLM mode tests (1ty via dogfooding), error locations (3hk), fan-in example (8vt), cross-module + collision (jtz). @node is THE primary API.

title: Dogfood: migrate Each example + tests to @node
description:
Part of epic neograph-eiw (Dogfood @node). **Blocks on neograph-jyw** (@node Each interop via map_over kwarg).

## Scope

Migrate Each/fan-out usages:
- `examples/04_each_fanout.py`
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestEach` (fan-out tests)
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestModifierAsFirstNode` (if applicable)
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestFirstNodeEdgeCases` (if applicable)
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestNodeMap` — already @node-native, VERIFY no declarative Construct/Node calls remain
- Any Each usage in other classes surfaced during migration

## What to do

1. Wait for `neograph-jyw` (Each interop on @node via map_over kwarg) to close.
2. Replace each `Node(...) | Each(over='...', key='...')` pattern with either:
   - `@node(..., map_over=lambda s: s.upstream.items, map_key='label')` if jyw uses kwargs, OR
   - `@node(...)` followed by `.map(lambda s: ..., key=...)` if jyw uses sidecar carryover on modifier composition.
3. Update `examples/04_each_fanout.py` to use the new pattern.
4. Re-run: example and tests produce equivalent fan-out behavior.

## Acceptance criteria

- `examples/04_each_fanout.py` uses @node exclusively for Each.
- TestEach's fan-out tests use @node.
- TestNodeMap fully @node-native (spot-check and confirm).
- Full test suite stays green.
- End-to-end fan-out cardinality still asserted (set equality on result keys, per the HIGH-01 fix from wave 1).

## Dependencies

- **neograph-jyw** (Each interop on @node) — hard block.

## Files to read

- `examples/04_each_fanout.py` — current
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestEach` — current
- `src/neograph/modifiers.py::Each, split_each_path` — IR reference

## Non-goals

- Do NOT modify `src/neograph/modifiers.py` or `_construct_validation.py::_check_each_path`.
- Do NOT migrate `TestConstructValidation`'s Each path tests — those test the validator's `_check_each_path` directly via declarative Construct.
close_reason: Wave 5: five parallel agents. edr: FromInput[T]/FromConfig[T]/default-value constants with param-resolution sidecar, 5 tests. 8k3: Each downstream type tracking fix (dict[str,X] producers, extended _types_compatible for generics, hint for raw/dict mismatch), 5 tests. 8vt: fan-in showcase example 01c (82 lines, diamond DAG, out-of-order declaration). uvb: Oracle dogfood (example 03 + TestOracle migrated). 4ou: Each dogfood (example 04 + TestEach migrated). 153/153 passing.

title: Add Loop-on-Construct (cyclical sub-constructs)
description:
## Problem
Multi-node loops (review->revise cycle) need to be expressed as sub-constructs. This requires Loop modifier support on Construct, not just Node.

Construct already inherits Modifiable so pipe syntax works: construct | Loop(when=...). Need compiler, state, factory, and validation support.

## Fix (5 sub-parts)

### 5a. compiler.py — _add_subgraph handles Loop
In _add_subgraph (~line 115-142), after Oracle/Each checks, before plain subgraph:
- Check sub.has_modifier(Loop)
- Call new _add_subgraph_loop(graph, sub, sub_graph, subgraph_fn, loop, prev_node)
- _add_subgraph_loop: adds node, loop_router conditional edge, exit pass-through — same pattern as _add_loop_back_edge but for sub-constructs

### 5b. state.py — Loop on Construct gets append-list + counter
In compile_state_model sub_constructs section (~line 77-99):
- Add elif sub.has_modifier(Loop): append-list reducer + neo_loop_count field
```python
elif sub.has_modifier(Loop):
    fields[field_name] = (Annotated[list[sub.output], _append_loop_result], [])
    fields[f'neo_loop_count_{field_name}'] = (int, 0)
```

### 5c. factory.py — make_subgraph_fn: Loop counter + re-entry
- State update includes loop counter increment for Loop constructs
- On re-entry (iteration 2+), read input from own append-list field[-1] instead of scanning by type

### 5d. modifiers.py — Modifiable.__or__ dispatches validation
Currently calls validate_loop_self_edge which assumes Node (accesses .outputs/.inputs).
For Construct, dispatch to new validate_loop_construct that checks .output/.input:
```python
if isinstance(modifier, Loop):
    if hasattr(result, 'outputs'):  # Node
        validate_loop_self_edge(result)
    elif hasattr(result, 'output') and hasattr(result, 'input'):  # Construct
        validate_loop_construct(result)
```

### 5e. _construct_validation.py — validate_loop_construct
```python
def validate_loop_construct(construct) -> None:
    if construct.output is None or construct.input is None:
        raise ConstructError('Loop on Construct requires both input= and output=')
    if not _types_compatible(construct.output, construct.input):
        raise ConstructError(f'Loop on construct: output {construct.output} not compatible with input {construct.input}')
```

## Files
- src/neograph/compiler.py
- src/neograph/state.py
- src/neograph/factory.py
- src/neograph/modifiers.py
- src/neograph/_construct_validation.py

## Verification
- test_review_revise_cycle_exits_when_approved passes (rewritten to use sub-construct + Loop)
- New test: Loop on Construct with incompatible types raises ConstructError
- uv run pytest -q passes
design:
## Architecture
Same append-list reducer pattern as Node Loop. Three changes:
1. compiler._add_subgraph: check Loop modifier, wire back-edge with loop_router
2. state.compile_state_model: Loop Construct gets Annotated[list[T], _append_loop_result] + neo_loop_count
3. factory.make_subgraph_fn: increment counter in state update, read from own append-list on re-entry

- __or__ already dispatches to validate_loop_construct (added in neograph-1r6g commit 9fe0c12)

title: Doc: skip_when dual-path state writing not documented
description: _apply_skip_when writes state (side effects) and returns early BEFORE the postamble _build_state_update. The _execute_node pseudocode documents this in comments but doesn't explicitly document the dual-path contract: skip_when exits before dispatch AND before postamble state update. Any future _build_state_update change must account for this.
close_reason: All doc corrections applied to architecture-v2.md in single edit pass

title: Callable Node API for fan-in DAGs: node2(node1(...))
description:
Source: Keras Functional API, Pulumi Output[T]
Effort: Medium
Breaks API: No (additive, but this is what Construct should have been)

Current nodes=[a, b, c] is implicitly linear. True DAGs with fan-in require sub-Constructs today. Proposed: Node.__call__ returns a typed NodeHandle[OutputType]; chaining handles builds the graph.

  claims   = decompose()                # NodeHandle[Claims]
  scored   = enrich(claims)             # NodeHandle[ScoredClaims]
  verified = verify(claims, scored)     # fan-in, free
  pipeline = Construct('rw', output=verified)

Wins:
- True DAG with fan-in that current nodes=[] list can't express
- Reads like data flow
- Type-safe: mypy checks claims is Claims, scored is ScoredClaims

Implementation:
- NodeHandle[T] class wrapping (node, output_type)
- Node.__call__ returns NodeHandle
- Construct accepts an output= NodeHandle and walks backward to collect all nodes
- Coexists with current nodes=[...] list mode

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #5
close_reason: SUPERSEDED by @node + ForwardConstruct. The callable-node API (node2(node1(...))) is exactly what ForwardConstruct.forward() provides (self.classify(self.decompose(topic))). And @node's parameter-name inference (def classify(decompose: Claims)) auto-wires the same topology without explicit call syntax. Both primary APIs make this feature redundant.

title: test_hypothesis.py: dead code — surface adapters and _assert_invariants used by 3 weak tests only
description: _build_yaml_surface, _build_llm_surface, _assert_invariants, and SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict are only used by the 3 TestTopologySurfaceMatrix cross-product tests (which were demoted from the main approach). Either these tests pull their weight or the adapters are dead code. Audit and decide: keep, strengthen, or remove.
notes:
## Audit Findings (round 1)

### Dead code: 170 lines
- _build_yaml_surface (53 lines) — 0 direct callers, only in dead SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict
- _build_llm_surface (25 lines) — 0 direct callers, only in dead SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict
- SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict (5 lines) — defined, never indexed or iterated anywhere
- _assert_invariants (25 lines) — defined, 0 call sites

### Live code:
- _build_scripted_surface (64 lines) — 7+ direct callers
- _apply_spec_modifiers (18 lines) — 9 callers
- _register_type_safe (7 lines) — 5 callers

### Cross-surface tests value assessment:
- TestCrossSurfaceValueEquivalence: ADDS VALUE — only cross-surface value equality test
- TestEachKeyParityAcrossSurfaces: ADDS VALUE — only test exercising load_spec for Each topology
- TestLoopIterationParityAcrossSurfaces: MOSTLY DUPLICATE — TestLoopTerminatesAndAccumulates covers same logic

### Root cause: 170 dead lines are scaffolding from a planned matrix (topology x surface) that was never wired up. SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict exists but no test iterates it.
## Verification (round 2): CONFIRMED
Zero discrepancies. All 4 dead symbols verified: _build_yaml_surface, _build_llm_surface, SURFACE_ADAPTERS, _assert_invariants — defined, never called or imported.

TestCrossSurfaceValueEquivalence and TestEachKeyParityAcrossSurfaces confirmed as uniquely valuable — keep them.
## CORRECTION (from external review)

### _build_yaml_surface and _build_llm_surface are NOT dead.

_build_yaml_surface: called by TestYAMLSurfaceSharedNodeImmutability (L3205) and TestEachKeyParityAcrossSurfaces (L3421). 2 callers.

_build_llm_surface: called by TestLLMSurfaceConfigPropagation (L3285), TestLLMSurfaceOracleCallCount (L3345), TestLLMSurfaceEachOracleCallCount (L3535). 3 callers.

### Actual dead code is ~35 lines, not 170:
- SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict (~5 lines) — never indexed
- _assert_invariants (~25 lines) — never called
- any_topology_spec_with_loops (~4 lines) — never referenced

### Both my audit agent AND verification agent confirmed '0 callers' for the surface builders. Both were WRONG. The cross-surface regression test classes at the bottom of the file (L3205-3591) call them directly — the grep apparently missed these because they use inline load_spec/configure_fake_llm, not the adapter functions by name.

Actually — let me verify this claim too.
## RE-VERIFICATION: Original audit was correct.

Grep confirms: _build_yaml_surface appears at 4 locations:
- L2277: definition
- L2390: SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict (dead)

_build_llm_surface appears at 4 locations:
- L2332: definition  
- L2391: SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict (dead)

The cross-surface regression tests (L3205-3591) call load_spec and configure_fake_llm DIRECTLY — they do NOT call _build_yaml_surface or _build_llm_surface. The review agent incorrectly assumed the cross-surface tests used the adapter functions.

### Final verdict: 170 dead lines stands.
- _build_yaml_surface (53 lines) — DEAD
- _build_llm_surface (25 lines) — DEAD
- SURFACE_ADAPTERS (5 lines) — DEAD
- _assert_invariants (25 lines) — DEAD
- any_topology_spec_with_loops (~10 lines) — DEAD (from fg69 audit)
## Prep
- File: tests/test_hypothesis.py
- Dead code blocks (5):
  1. any_topology_spec_with_loops (L2197-2206, 10 lines) — 0 callers
  2. _build_yaml_surface (L2277-2329, 53 lines) — only in dead SURFACE_ADAPTERS
  3. _build_llm_surface (L2332-2356, 25 lines) — only in dead SURFACE_ADAPTERS
  4. SURFACE_ADAPTERS dict (L2388-2392, 5 lines) — never indexed
  5. _assert_invariants (L2395-2422, 28 lines) — 0 call sites
- KEEP: _apply_spec_modifiers (9 callers), _register_type_safe (5 callers), _build_scripted_surface (7+ callers)
- Approach: delete all 5 blocks in sequence (bottom-up to keep line numbers stable), run pytest after
- Total: ~121 lines removed
## Execution

  1. _assert_invariants + SURFACE_ADAPTERS (33 lines)
  2. _build_yaml_surface + _build_llm_surface (78 lines)
  3. any_topology_spec_with_loops (12 lines)
  4. Unused TypAny import (1 line)
- Total: ~124 lines removed

title: Write regression test for neograph-fsja
description:

any implementation begins.

## BANNED Patterns

- `pytest.mark.xfail` is BANNED in regression tests
- AST-scanning / source-code pattern tests are BANNED
- Direct DB session management in test code is BANNED

title: Produce mode: support json_mode and text extraction strategies alongside with_structured_output
description: Produce mode always uses llm.with_structured_output(model) which fails on DeepSeek Reasoner/V3 (400: response_format type unavailable). Production pattern: inject JSON schema into prompt + bind response_format=json_object + parse manually. Add llm_config.output_strategy: structured (current), json_mode (schema-in-prompt, widest compat), text (plain text + parse). Default should be json_mode for widest provider support.

title: Typed llm_config via Literal-discriminated overloads (Node.produce, Node.gather)
description:
Source: Rust typestate builder
Effort: Small-medium
Breaks API: Mild (additive, deprecate Node(mode=...) eventually)

Problem: Node(mode='produce', output=Claims, tools=[...]) accepts invalid combinations silently at runtime. mypy has no idea that produce mode doesn't support tools.

Proposed:
  decompose = Node.produce('decompose', output=Claims, model='fast', prompt='x', temperature=0.8)
  verify    = Node.gather('verify', input=Cluster, output=VerifyResult, tools=[search])
  execute   = Node.execute('execute', input=Plan, output=Result, tools=[write_file])
  munge     = Node.scripted('munge', fn='munge_fn', output=Data)

Each factory uses @overload so Node.produce(tools=[...]) is a type error.

Wins:
- Eliminates runtime 'can't use tools in produce mode' surprises
- Autocomplete shows only valid parameters per mode
- mypy/pyright catch misconfigurations statically

Implementation: add Node.produce, Node.gather, Node.execute classmethods with @overload decorators. Keep Node(mode=...) for backward compat with a deprecation warning.

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #7
close_reason: LOW VALUE now that @node is the primary API. Typed llm_config via Literal overloads was about catching Node(mode='produce') without output at mypy level. @node's mode inference + decoration-time validation (neograph-q54) already catches these errors eagerly with clear messages. The Node IR level benefit is marginal — advanced users who construct Node() directly are expected to read docs.

title: Construction-time validators: Construct(nodes=[]), Oracle(n<1), Each(over= format)
description:
Add Pydantic model_validators to catch structural invariants at construction time, not compile time.

1. Construct: model_validator that raises ConstructError if nodes is empty
2. Oracle: field_validator on n that requires n >= 1 (or n >= 2 — ensemble of 1 is degenerate)
3. Each: field_validator on 'over' that checks format is 'upstream.field' (contains exactly one dot)

Files: src/neograph/construct.py, src/neograph/modifiers.py
Guard: xfail tests in test_hypothesis.py (empty construct, oracle n=0, oracle n<0) should pass.
notes:
Also closes neograph-i65s (skip_value signature). Add callable signature check for skip_when (1 arg) and skip_value (1 arg) at Node construction time.
## Acceptance
- xfail tests pass: test_empty_construct_raises_construct_error, test_oracle_n_zero_raises, test_oracle_n_negative_raises, test_each_over_bogus_path_raises_at_compile
- Remove xfail markers from those 4 tests
- Close bugs: neograph-7v1t, neograph-nqxl, neograph-0m11, neograph-i65s
close_reason: Done: 4 construction-time validators added (empty Construct, Oracle n>=1, Each.over non-empty, skip_when/skip_value signature). 3 xfail markers removed; 1 kept (Each bogus path defers unknown roots).

title: Operator condition error masked by checkpointer guard
description:
Operator(when='nonexistent') without a checkpointer gives 'no checkpointer' error instead of 'condition not registered'. The checkpointer guard fires first, hiding the real problem.

Root cause: compiler.py compile() checks has_operator+checkpointer before reaching lookup_condition in _add_operator_check. Error ordering is confusing but the error does fire.

Fix: Move the condition existence check before the checkpointer guard, or add a separate validation pass for condition strings at the top of compile().

Fixture: tests/check_fixtures/known_gaps/mod_operator_unregistered_condition.py
close_reason: Fixed: condition check fires before checkpointer guard

title: HIGH-02: Extract shared _build_annotation_namespace helper
description: New (kqd refactor). Frame-walking namespace builder duplicated ~25 lines between _classify_di_params (decorators.py:194-225) and @node inputs inference (decorators.py:521-552). Extract _build_annotation_namespace(f, frame_depth) -> dict shared helper.
close_reason: Extracted _build_annotation_namespace helper; both _classify_di_params and node() input inference use it

title: ReAct loop: add max_iterations and token budget guard
description: invoke_with_tools in _llm.py:515 has while True with no max_iterations guard. Loop runs until LLM stops calling tools or all budgets exhausted. With 8 tool calls each returning 4000 chars, conversation grows O(n^2) in input tokens — each call replays entire history. Needed: (1) max_iterations param (default ~20, configurable via llm_config), (2) token budget tracking — abort loop if cumulative input tokens exceed threshold. Evidence from piarch: BR-RW-036 run showed 10x cost variance across claims in verify-claim fan-out.
notes: Implemented max_iterations (default 20) and token_budget (default None) guards in invoke_with_tools. Both configurable via llm_config on Node. When a guard fires: (1) tool calls get 'wrap up' ToolMessages, (2) tools are unbound, (3) loop forces a final response. Pattern follows existing all_exhausted() exit. 5 new tests, 1007 total tests passing.

title: Kill sidecar: replace _node_sidecar with PrivateAttr on Node
description:
Kill sidecar: replace _node_sidecar with PrivateAttr on Node.

METHODOLOGY: Write the structural guard test FIRST.

Step 1: Add a test to tests/test_structural_guards.py that greps for "_node_sidecar" in src/neograph/ and asserts 0 matches. Also grep for "weakref.finalize" related to sidecar and assert 0.

Step 2: Add PrivateAttr fields to Node in node.py:
  from pydantic import PrivateAttr
  _fn: Callable | None = PrivateAttr(default=None)
  _param_names: tuple[str, ...] = PrivateAttr(default=())

Step 3: In Modifiable.__or__ (modifiers.py), after model_copy, copy private attrs to new instance.

Step 4: Replace all _node_sidecar usage in decorators.py and _construct_builder.py with direct attribute access.

Step 5: Remove _node_sidecar dict, _register_sidecar, _get_sidecar, and weakref.finalize for sidecar.

Step 6: Run the guard test — must pass with 0 violations.
Step 7: Run full test suite — must pass. Test all 3 API surfaces.

GATE: uv run pytest tests/test_structural_guards.py -v passes
GATE: uv run pytest -q --tb=line passes with 0 failures
GATE: grep -rc "_node_sidecar" src/neograph/ returns 0
design: Add _sidecar: tuple[Callable, tuple[str, ...]] | None = PrivateAttr(default=None) to Node. model_copy preserves it. Remove _node_sidecar dict, _register_sidecar, _get_sidecar, weakref.finalize. Replace all id()-keyed lookups with direct attr access. Core invariant: every @node-decorated Node carries its fn+param_names on itself, not in a global dict.
notes:
MANDATORY: Step 1 of every task is writing a structural guard test in tests/test_structural_guards.py that FAILS before the work starts. The guard runs on every pytest invocation and is the acceptance criterion — not self-reports, not grep. The task is not done until the guard passes AND remains in the test file permanently.
## Research findings
- _node_sidecar: dict[int, (fn, param_names)] with 6 write sites, 10 read sites
- _param_resolutions: dict[int, dict[str, DIBinding]] with 7 write sites, 8 read sites  
- Pydantic v2 model_copy PRESERVES PrivateAttr (confirmed: __copy__ copies __pydantic_private__)
- This eliminates ALL re-registration after | modifier — model_copy carries attrs forward
- weakref.finalize becomes unnecessary — data lives on Node, GC'd with it
- Node is NOT frozen — direct assignment to PrivateAttr works
- Risk: 15+ test sites import sidecar internals, need updating
- Files to change: node.py, decorators.py, _construct_builder.py, lint.py, tests
close_reason: PrivateAttr replaces _node_sidecar. Guard test_no_node_sidecar_dict passes. model_copy preserves attrs.

title: FromInput[PydanticModel] — bundle multiple input fields into a typed context object
description: Scripted @nodes that need pipeline metadata (node_id, project_root, run config fields) currently repeat: node_id: FromInput[str], project_root: FromInput[str], shared: FromConfig[SharedResources] — 3 lines per node, ~20 nodes in piarch = 60 lines of boilerplate. Proposal: support FromInput[PydanticModel] where the model's field names map to config['configurable'] keys. Example: class RunCtx(BaseModel): node_id: str; project_root: str. Then: def my_node(upstream: X, ctx: FromInput[RunCtx]) -> Y: ... and ctx.node_id / ctx.project_root are populated from config automatically. Each field resolved independently via config[configurable][field_name].
close_reason: 6jd: FromInput[PydanticModel] and FromConfig[PydanticModel] now detected at decoration time and resolved at runtime by pulling each of the model's field names from config['configurable'], constructing the instance. Classifier walks call-stack frames to find locally-defined Pydantic model classes (needed under 'from __future__ import annotations'). Shared _classify_di_params + _resolve_di_value helpers extracted so both @node and @merge_fn use the same DI machinery. 4 regression tests in TestFromInputPydanticModel. 9zj: new @merge_fn decorator for Oracle merge functions with FromInput/FromConfig DI. Auto-registers via register_scripted for back-compat with Oracle(merge_fn='name') lookups. make_oracle_merge_fn in factory.py checks the @merge_fn registry first and calls with resolved DI args instead of legacy (variants, config) signature when a match is found. Plain (variants, config) merge_fn functions still work unchanged. 3 tests in TestOracleMergeFnDI covering bundled FromConfig, FromInput, and back-compat. 217/217 passing.

title: Fix: stale ValueError docstring in TestToolRegistrationError
description: test_pipeline_modes.py::TestToolRegistrationError::test_clear_error_raised_when_tool_not_registered — docstring says 'raises ValueError' but code correctly expects ConfigurationError. Update the docstring.

title: Tighten test_map_end_to_end_fanout assertion (and TestEach:319)
description:
HIGH-01 from code review 040426_2207.

The only end-to-end fan-out test uses:
  assert "alpha" in verify_results or len(verify_results) == 2
The 'or' short-circuits — a single-item dict {'alpha': ...} passes even though fan-out should produce two keys. A regression that broke beta dispatch would not be caught.

Verification: python -c "d={'alpha':'x'}; print('alpha' in d or len(d)==2)" → True.

Fix tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py:386 and the copy-pasted source at tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py:319 (TestEach::test_fanout_over_collection) with:
  assert set(verify_results.keys()) == {'alpha', 'beta'}
  assert verify_results['alpha'].cluster_label == 'alpha'
  assert verify_results['beta'].cluster_label == 'beta'

Source: .claude/code-review/040426_2207/synthesis.md HIGH-01
close_reason: Wave 1: HIGH-01 tightened fan-out assertion in TestEach::test_fanout_over_collection (line 319) and TestNodeMap::test_map_end_to_end_fanout (line 386) with set equality + per-key payload. HIGH-02 renamed construct.py's _type_name to _fmt_type (7 call sites); factory.py's _type_name stays. 99/99 passing.

title: Write regression test for neograph-84e5

any implementation begins.

title: @node: Operator interrupt_when kwarg for human-in-loop
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness). Surfaced by the Path A Dogfood epic.

## Problem

The Operator modifier (`src/neograph/modifiers.py:175-188`) provides human-in-loop via `node | Operator(when='condition_name')` where `when` is a registered condition function. There is no way to attach an Operator modifier to a @node-decorated function, so pipelines with interrupt/resume semantics cannot use @node.

Also: the existing `when='string_key'` pattern is itself a target of the neograph-pub epic (forward() mode kills string dispatch). Adding Operator support to @node is a bridge until forward() lands, but it still has to look clean to make @node the sole documented API.

## Proposed API

Two forms, both via a new `interrupt_when=` kwarg on `@node`:

### Form 1: registered condition name (mirrors existing Operator(when=...))
    @node(mode='scripted', output=ValidationResult, interrupt_when='validation_failed')
    def validate(claims: Claims) -> ValidationResult: ...

Internally attaches `Operator(when='validation_failed')` to the Node.

### Form 2: inline callable (the cleaner form — kills string dispatch)
    @node(mode='scripted', output=ValidationResult,
          interrupt_when=lambda state: (
              {'issues': state.validate.issues}
              if state.validate and not state.validate.passed
              else None
          ))
    def validate(claims: Claims) -> ValidationResult: ...

The callable is registered under a synthesized name (`_node_interrupt_{node_name}_{id}`) via `register_condition`, then attached as `Operator(when=<synthesized_name>)`. The user never sees the synthesized name.

## Implementation sketch

In `src/neograph/decorators.py`:

    from neograph.modifiers import Operator

    def node(
        fn=None,
        *,
        ...,
        interrupt_when: str | Callable | None = None,
    ):
        def decorator(f):
            ...
            n = Node(...)
            _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)

            if interrupt_when is not None:
                from neograph.factory import register_condition

                if isinstance(interrupt_when, str):
                    condition_name = interrupt_when
                elif callable(interrupt_when):
                    condition_name = f\"_node_interrupt_{node_name}_{id(f):x}\"
                    register_condition(condition_name, interrupt_when)
                else:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{node_name}' interrupt_when must be a string \"
                        f\"(registered condition name) or a callable; got \"
                        f\"{type(interrupt_when).__name__}\"
                    )

                n = n | Operator(when=condition_name)
                _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)  # sidecar carryover

            return n

## Acceptance criteria

- New tests in `TestNodeDecorator`:
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_with_string_name`: uses a pre-registered condition name, verifies the Operator modifier is attached and the interrupt fires end-to-end with a MemorySaver checkpointer (mirrors `TestOperator::test_interrupt_on_failure`).
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_with_callable`: uses an inline lambda condition, verifies auto-registration under a synthesized name and end-to-end interrupt behavior.
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_resume`: verifies interrupt + resume flow (mirrors `TestOperatorResume::test_interrupt_then_resume`).
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_when_falsy_continues`: condition returns None, graph runs through without pausing (mirrors `TestOperatorContinues`).
  - `test_node_decorator_interrupt_when_wrong_type_raises`: passing e.g. an int raises ConstructError at decoration time.
- Operator modifier attached to Node's `modifiers` list — verifiable via `node.get_modifier(Operator)`.
- Existing TestOperator tests still pass.
- @node docstring updated with an interrupt example.
- Full suite stays green.

## Dependencies

- Blocks on **neograph-jyw** (sidecar carryover on modifier composition).

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/modifiers.py:175-188` — Operator class
- `src/neograph/factory.py::register_condition`, `lookup_condition` — condition registry
- `src/neograph/compiler.py::_add_operator_check` — how Operator compiles to interrupt
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestOperator`, `TestOperatorContinues`, `TestOperatorResume` — patterns to mirror

## Non-goals

- Do NOT replace the Operator modifier class itself — @node is a sugar layer over it.
- Do NOT add support for multiple interrupt_when kwargs on a single @node — one is enough for v1.
- Do NOT pre-empt forward() mode's control-flow handling — that's neograph-pub epic scope.
close_reason: Three modifier-kwargs subtasks on neograph-4mx. nqe: Oracle ensemble_n/merge_fn/merge_prompt kwargs, 6 tests. t5z: mode='raw' support replacing @raw_node, 4 tests. 6y3: Operator interrupt_when kwarg (string + callable forms with auto-registration), 6 tests. 143/143 passing (+16 new).

title: TQ-10-12: Missing edge cases in conditions and types
description: Low. test_conditions.py + test_spec_types.py. No tests for: negative number literals in conditions (score > -0.5), cross-type comparisons (int field vs float literal), type definitions with no required key.
close_reason: Fixed in e165b70. All tests honest, 532 passed.

title: test_hypothesis.py has 3 forgiving Oracle merge patterns
description: Lines 255, 539, 1259 use Alpha() if not isinstance(_i, list) else Alpha(...). Semantically correct (Oracle merge receives list on merge, single on first pass) but still forgiving — wrong-type would produce Alpha() silently instead of failing.
notes:
## Prep
2 patterns found (3rd was likely removed during earlier split):
1. tests/hypothesis/test_regression.py:367 — eo_count_merge: Gamma() if not isinstance → assert list
2. tests/hypothesis/test_topologies.py:119 — mx_merge: Beta() if not isinstance → assert list

Both are explicit merge_fn registrations — they ONLY receive list[OutputType] on merge call. The isinstance guard is dead code in practice.

title: Docs: API reference — add describe_graph, lint, parse_condition, Loop.history
description: API reference page missing entries for: describe_graph(), lint()/LintIssue, parse_condition(), Loop.history, Construct.renderer, Construct.llm_config inheritance, Node.map().

title: Fix: P0-P1 validation gaps
description:
Lightweight formula for executing well-defined beads tasks. Three atoms per task: prep (read and plan), execute (implement and verify), commit. Use instead of task-execute when the task description already contains sufficient context and doesn't need architect review or triage routing.
Total atoms: 1 (baseline) + 3N (per task) + 1 (verify-final) = 3N + 2. Compare task-execute at 7N + 2.
notes:
## Baseline
- pytest: 700 passed, 0 failed, 4 warnings in 1.50s (2026-04-09)
## Final
- 711 passed (baseline was 700, +11 from fixes and regression tests)
- 5 bugs fixed: neograph-shsr, li9b, luzc, pbyz, r135
- 1 bug cannot reproduce: neograph-iio2 (regression tests added)
- 8 fixtures moved from known_gaps to should_fail/should_pass
- known_gaps remaining: 6

title: Verify clean baseline
description:

exact failures and leave this atom OPEN.

title: json_mode output schema: emit TypeScript-style notation instead of JSON Schema
description:
Sibling of neograph-ni6 (input rendering). This issue covers OUTPUT schema emission.

## The problem

Current json_mode output strategy relies on consumers to inject a JSON Schema into the prompt so the LLM knows what shape to return. piarch's bridge does this via:

    schema_str = json.dumps(output_model.model_json_schema(), indent=2)
    # Injected into prompt via 'common/json-instruction' template

JSON Schema is verbose. For a typical Pydantic model with 5-10 fields and some nesting, the JSON Schema representation is 300-800 tokens. BAML's insight: a TypeScript-style notation for the same schema is ~4x more token-efficient AND the LLM parses it more reliably.

## The BAML comparison

Same Resume model, two representations:

JSON Schema (current, ~400 tokens):
{
  '$schema': 'https://json-schema.org/draft/2020-12/schema',
  'type': 'object',
  'properties': {
    'name': {'type': 'string', 'title': 'Name'},
    'education': {
      'type': 'array',
      'items': {
        'type': 'object',
        'properties': {
          'school': {'type': 'string'},
          'graduation_year': {'type': 'integer'}
        },
        'required': ['school', 'graduation_year']
      }
    }
  },
  'required': ['name', 'education']
}

TypeScript-style (proposed, ~80 tokens):
{
  name: string
  education: [
    {
      school: string
      graduation_year: int
    }
  ]
}

5x reduction for the same information. BAML's docs and benchmarks claim the LLM parses the TypeScript form more reliably too — fewer parse failures, fewer schema drift errors.

## Proposed design

Add a new helper function to neograph:

    from neograph import describe_type

    describe_type(
        model: type[BaseModel],
        *,
        prefix: str = 'Answer in JSON matching this schema:',
        hoist_classes: Literal['auto', 'all'] | list[str] = 'auto',
        hoisted_class_prefix: str = 'type ',
        always_hoist_enums: bool = True,
        or_splitter: str = ' or ',  # NOT '|' — LLMs parse 'or' better
        indent: str = '  ',
    ) -> str

Behavior:
- Walks Pydantic model_fields recursively
- Emits TypeScript-style notation (string/int/float/bool primitives, [ ] for lists, { } for objects, enum members inlined)
- 'hoist_classes=auto' extracts repeated nested types as 'type Foo = { ... }' declarations before the main type — avoids repetition when the same sub-model appears multiple times
- 'or_splitter=" or "' for Union types (BAML's insight: LLMs read 'string or int' better than 'string | int')
- Reads Pydantic Field(description='...') metadata and emits as inline comments: '  name: string  // the person name'
- Handles Literal types as inlined enums: 'status: "active" or "inactive" or "pending"'

## Integration with output_strategy

Change json_mode strategy in neograph/_llm.py to use describe_type() instead of raw JSON Schema:

    # Before:
    schema_str = json.dumps(output_model.model_json_schema(), indent=2)

    # After:
    schema_str = describe_type(output_model)

This is a strategy-level change in neograph, not a consumer change. Consumers using json_mode get the improvement automatically.

Leave structured strategy unchanged (LangChain's with_structured_output uses the provider's native JSON Schema and that's fine — providers optimize for it).

Leave text strategy unchanged.

## Public helper

Expose describe_type() as a public helper (neograph.describe_type) so consumers who write custom prompts can use it directly. Example piarch use:

    @node(output=BRDocument, model='reason', prompt='br/write-document')
    def br_write(qualification: BRQualification) -> BRDocument: ...

    # In the prompt template:
    'Return a business rule matching this schema:\n{output_schema}\n\nBased on: {qualification}'

    # prompt_compiler:
    messages[0].content = messages[0].content.format(
        output_schema=describe_type(BRDocument),
        qualification=rendered_qualification,
    )

## IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: port from BAML

BAML's implementation is in Rust (baml/engine/baml-lib/jinja-runtime/src/output_format/types.rs) and is the best prior art. Port the algorithm to Python:

- Core type dispatch (primitive/list/object/enum/union)
- hoist_classes logic (auto-detect repeated types, emit as 'type Foo = ...' declarations)
- Field description inlining from Pydantic Field metadata
- or_splitter for unions
- always_hoist_enums behavior

Do NOT take BAML as a dependency — it's Rust-first with a Python client, designed for .baml file consumption. Use only as the reference implementation for the algorithm.

## Token savings estimate

For piarch's 60-80 models across 15 pipelines:

- Average JSON Schema per model: 400 tokens
- Average TypeScript notation per model: 80 tokens
- Average produce calls per pipeline run: 5
- Average pipeline runs per day during active iteration: 50

Savings: (400 - 80) × 5 × 50 = 80,000 tokens/day, or ~2.4M tokens/month.
At ~$0.5/1M input tokens (DeepSeek R1 rate), that's ~$1.20/month in savings. Small, but compounds with scale.

The real value isn't cost — it's prompt parse reliability. Fewer schema drift errors = fewer fallback-path executions = more deterministic outputs.

## Success criteria

1. describe_type(BRDocument) produces <100 tokens for models that currently emit 400+ tokens of JSON Schema
2. json_mode strategy automatically uses describe_type() instead of raw model_json_schema()
3. Public helper available as 'from neograph import describe_type'
4. Nested models with repetition get hoisted correctly ('auto' mode)
5. Pydantic Field(description=...) metadata is surfaced as inline comments
6. Union types use 'or' separator, not '|'
7. Enum Literal types are inlined as quoted strings
8. Round-trip test: LLM response parsed by Pydantic matches expected structure for complex models
design:
## Architecture (from plan session 2026-04-06)

### New file: src/neograph/describe_type.py
Public helper: `from neograph import describe_type`

```python
def describe_type(
    model: type[BaseModel],
    *,
    prefix: str = 'Answer in JSON matching this schema:',
    hoist_classes: Literal['auto', 'all'] | list[str] = 'auto',
    always_hoist_enums: bool = True,
    or_splitter: str = ' or ',
    indent: str = '  ',
) -> str
```

### Algorithm: two-pass recursive type walker
- Pass 1: _count_classes(model) — walk recursively, count occurrences in a dict
- Pass 2: _render_model_body(model, ...) — render, using references for hoisted classes

Type dispatch: str->string, int->int, float->float, bool->bool, None->null, list[X]->[X], dict[K,V]->object notation, Optional[X]->X or null, Union[A,B]->joined by or_splitter, Literal['a','b']->quoted values with or, BaseModel->recursive { field: type } blocks, Field(description=...)->// inline comment.

Handle circular models via visited set.

### Integration: json_mode auto-injects output_schema
- _llm.py:invoke_structured generates output_schema=describe_type(output_model) for json_mode
- _compile_prompt passes output_schema as new kwarg
- _accepted_params mechanism ensures old prompt_compilers that don't accept output_schema= just don't receive it (backward compat)

### Files to modify
- src/neograph/describe_type.py (NEW)
- src/neograph/_llm.py (_compile_prompt + invoke_structured)
- src/neograph/__init__.py (export)

### Tests (21 tests in 2 classes)
TestDescribeType (17): primitives, nesting, lists, dicts, optionals, unions, literals, enums, field descriptions, hoisting auto/all/explicit, recursion, empty model, token reduction comparison
TestJsonModeOutputSchema (4): json_mode passes output_schema, structured/text don't, backward compat

### Core invariant
describe_type is a pure function with no side effects. It walks Pydantic model_fields only. The json_mode integration is additive — existing prompt_compilers continue to work unchanged.
notes:
## Research note: BAML as a library dep is not viable

Investigated whether baml-py (Rust+PyO3, like pydantic-core) exposes the output_format renderer as a standalone Python function. Verdict: NO.

**Findings** (verified from baml-py canary branch):

- baml-py public API is entirely BAML runtime: BamlRuntime, FunctionResult, streams, media types, observability. No standalone schema renderer.
- TypeBuilder helper exists for dynamic type construction but has no .render() method returning schema string.
- Rust crate baml-lib/jsonish holds the algorithm but is not exposed via PyO3 bindings.
- BAML tracks this gap in issues #998 and #1403, shipped 'Modular API' (b.request/b.parse) but still requires .baml files + codegen.

**Why PyO3 being available doesn't help**:

pydantic-core is DESIGNED as a library primitive with a stable documented API. baml-py is DESIGNED as a runtime consumer of .baml files. Same tech (PyO3), fundamentally different API contracts. BAML chose to only expose the runtime, not the primitives.

**Implementation still means porting**:

- Port from baml-lib/jsonish Rust source (open, well-documented)
- ~200-400 lines of Python
- Algorithm is simple (type dispatch + recursion + hoisting)
- Performance is not critical (rendered <1KB per LLM call, not a hot loop)

**Optional upstream contribution**:

File an issue with BoundaryML asking to expose OutputFormatContent renderer as a standalone Python function. If shipped, our port becomes replaceable. Good open-source citizen move, benefits every other Python consumer.
close_reason: describe_type() landed + json_mode auto-injects output_schema via _compile_prompt.

title: test_node_decorator.py: split 3488-line monolith by concern
description: Split into tests/decorator/ package: test_mode_inference.py, test_di.py, test_fan_out.py, test_ensemble.py, test_construct_from_functions.py, test_construct_from_module.py, test_sidecar.py. Extract shared fixtures to conftest.py. Target <500 lines per file.

title: Bug: dict-form outputs + Oracle causes concurrent write error
description: Oracle's make_oracle_redirect_fn doesn't handle dict-form per-key state fields. When a node has outputs={'result': X, 'meta': Y} | Oracle(n=2, merge_fn=...), the Oracle redirect creates N variant subgraphs that each try to write per-key fields, causing concurrent write errors in LangGraph's state management. Discovered by executor-modifiers during rdu.6 test writing — the test was silently downgraded to single-type outputs. Need to fix make_oracle_redirect_fn to handle dict-form outputs, then write the real integration test.
design:
## Root Cause
make_oracle_redirect_fn (factory.py:437) assumes single-output: it checks result.get(field_name). With dict-form outputs, _build_state_update writes keys like dogen_result, dogen_meta — never dogen. So val is None, redirect returns raw result, both generators write to LastValue channels that only accept 1 value.

## Fix
make_oracle_redirect_fn needs to detect dict-form output keys (field_name_*) and redirect ALL of them to the collector. The merge_fn needs to merge per-key and write back to all per-key fields. Alternatively, collect the full result dict into the collector and let the merge_fn handle the dict shape.
notes:
## Reproduction
- Failing test: tests/test_modifiers.py::TestModifierCombinations::test_oracle_merges_per_key_when_dict_form_outputs
- Root cause: make_oracle_redirect_fn looks for result.get(field_name) but dict-form outputs write to {field_name}_{key}. Redirect misses them → generators write directly to per-key LastValue channels → InvalidUpdateError from LangGraph.
## Trace-similar
- make_each_redirect_fn (line 563) has the same result.get(field_name) pattern
- But Each + dict-form works by accident: _build_state_update already wraps per-key with {key_val: val}, so the redirect is a no-op and the raw result passes through correctly
- Oracle is different: the redirect MUST move results to the collector (list reducer), not the consumer-facing fields. With dict-form, the redirect misses per-key fields and they go directly to LastValue channels
- No other similar patterns found outside factory.py
close_reason: Fixed: redirect fn handles per-key fields, state model uses single collector for dict-form+Oracle, merge fn unwraps per-key

title: Factory: loop counter increment in state update
description:
Node wrappers increment neo_loop_count on each execution when Loop modifier present.

## File: src/neograph/factory.py

In _build_state_update (or a new helper), when node has Loop modifier:

    loop_mod = node.get_modifier(Loop)
    if loop_mod:
        count_field = f'neo_loop_count_{field_name}'
        current = _state_get(state, count_field) or 0
        update[count_field] = current + 1
        if loop_mod.history:
            history_field = f'neo_loop_history_{field_name}'
            update[history_field] = result  # appended via reducer

This must work for scripted, produce, and agent/act wrappers.

## Acceptance criteria
- neo_loop_count increments on each loop iteration
- history=True collects each iteration's result
- Counter resets correctly for Each+Loop (per-item independent)
close_reason: Added loop counter increment to _build_state_update in factory.py. When a node has Loop modifier: increments neo_loop_count_{field_name} on each execution, and if loop_mod.history is True, writes result to neo_loop_history_{field_name}. Works for all node types (scripted, produce, agent/act) since all call _build_state_update. All 420 existing tests pass.

title: required=True broken for bundled BaseModel FromInput/FromConfig
description:
_resolve_di_value extracts the required flag for bundled models but never enforces it. When model construction fails (missing fields), it catches the exception and returns None with a warning — even when required=True. Inconsistent with the scalar case which raises ExecutionError.

Root cause: decorators.py lines 340-357 — from_input_model/from_config_model branch extracts _required but does not check it.

Fix: Before attempting model construction, iterate model_fields and collect missing ones. If required=True and any fields are missing, raise ExecutionError listing the missing fields.

Fixtures: tests/check_fixtures/known_gaps/di_required_bundled_model.py, di_required_missing_no_config.py
close_reason: Fixed: required=True now enforced for bundled BaseModel DI params at runtime
