title: Trace similar patterns for neograph-nvu0
description:

title: Execute: neograph-empo
description:
Lightweight formula for executing well-defined beads tasks. Three atoms per task: prep (read and plan), execute (implement and verify), commit. Use instead of task-execute when the task description already contains sufficient context and doesn't need architect review or triage routing.
Total atoms: 1 (baseline) + 3N (per task) + 1 (verify-final) = 3N + 2. Compare task-execute at 7N + 2.
notes:
## Baseline
- pytest: 1296 passed, 0 failed, 9 warnings
## Final
- Quality check: 1296 passed, 0 failed (baseline: 1296)
- Delta: 0 new/lost tests, monolith → 6-file package, 0 regressions

title: Verify clean baseline
description:
Run quality gates BEFORE any code changes to confirm clean state.

## Instructions

1. **Run quality gate**:
   ```bash
   make quality 2>&1 | tail -5
   ```
   Record the exact pass count (e.g., "N passed").

## Gate -- HARD STOP on any failure

If ANY test fails in the baseline, STOP. Do NOT proceed. Report the
exact failures and leave this atom OPEN.

## Record Baseline

```bash
bd update neograph-bbr1 --append-notes "## Baseline
- make quality: [N] passed, 0 failed"
```
acceptance_criteria: Quality command passes. Pass count recorded in epic notes.

title: Prep neograph-empo
description:
Read the task and all affected code BEFORE making any changes.
This atom is READ-ONLY -- do not edit any files.

## Instructions

1. **Read the task**:
   ```bash
   bd show neograph-empo
   ```
   Read description, notes, design fields. Understand exactly what
   needs to change.

2. **Read every file** listed in the task description.
   For each test or source file, understand:
   - What does it currently do?
   - What should it do after the change?
   - What existing patterns does it follow?

3. **Identify dependencies**:
   - What functions will be called?
   - What test fixtures or helpers are available?

4. **Write a brief plan** to the task notes:
   ```bash
   bd update neograph-empo --append-notes "## Prep
   - Files to modify: [list]
   - Approach: [1-3 sentences]"
   ```

## Anti-Patterns
- Do NOT start editing files in this atom
- Do NOT skip reading affected files
- Do NOT assume you know the code without reading it
acceptance_criteria: Task read. All affected files read. Plan written to task notes.

title: Execute neograph-empo
description:
Implement the changes described in the task and the prep plan.

## Instructions

1. **Re-read the plan** from `bd show neograph-empo` (## Prep section).

2. **Make the changes** following the plan. For each change:
   - Follow project architecture patterns
   - Follow existing conventions in the file
   - Keep changes minimal -- do only what the task asks

3. **Run quality gates**:
   ```bash
   make quality
   ```
   If it fails, fix the issue and re-run. Do NOT close this atom
   until the quality command passes.

4. **Record results**:
   ```bash
   bd update neograph-empo --append-notes "## Execution
   - Changes made: [summary]
   - Quality check: PASS ([N] passed)"
   ```

## When EXISTING Tests Fail

If your changes break pre-existing tests:
1. STOP. Do NOT modify the failing tests to match your code.
2. Ask: is my change wrong, or is the test wrong?
3. If your change is wrong -- revert and try a different approach.
4. If the test has a genuine bug -- document WHY before modifying it.

Sacred rule: NEVER adjust tests to match code without justification.

## If Blocked

If you cannot complete the task:
```bash
bd update neograph-empo --append-notes "## BLOCKED
- [describe the blocker]
- [what was tried]"
```
Leave this atom OPEN for user direction.
acceptance_criteria: Changes implemented. Quality command passes. Results recorded in task notes.

title: Commit neograph-empo
description:
Stage, commit, and close the task.

## Instructions

1. **Review changes**:
   ```bash
   git diff --stat
   ```

2. **Stage specific files** (never use `git add .`):
   ```bash
   git add <specific-files>
   ```

3. **Commit** with conventional commit message:
   ```bash
   git commit -m "<type>: <description> (neograph-empo)"
   ```
   Types: feat, fix, refactor, test, chore, docs.

4. **Close the task**:
   ```bash
   bd close neograph-empo
   ```
acceptance_criteria: Commit exists. Task closed.

title: Final verification
description:
After all 1 tasks, verify zero regressions across all changes.

## Instructions

1. **Run full quality gate**:

   ```

2. **Compare against baseline** from `bd show neograph-bbr1` (## Baseline):

3. **Sync beads**:

4. **Record final results**:

   bd update neograph-bbr1 --append-notes "## Final
   - Quality check: [N] passed, 0 failed (baseline: [N])
   - Delta: +[N] new/modified tests, 0 regressions"
   ```

## HARD STOP -- Test Integrity

If ANY test fails: fix it. Do NOT close this atom with failures.
Do NOT use --ignore, -k "not ...", --deselect, or xfail.
acceptance_criteria: Quality command passes. Zero regressions vs baseline. Beads synced.

title: construct.py/node.py swallows TypeError/ValueError on llm_config merge
description: construct.py:108-113 and node.py:108-113 — try: item.llm_config = merged; except (TypeError, ValueError): pass. Silently ignores mutation failures on frozen models. Should use model_copy. If sub-construct node is frozen, llm_config merge silently does nothing — child runs without inherited config.
notes:
## Reproduction
- Failing test 1: tests/test_composition.py::TestConstructLlmConfigImmutability::test_llm_config_propagation_does_not_mutate_original_node
- Failing test 2: tests/test_composition.py::TestConstructLlmConfigImmutability::test_renderer_propagation_does_not_mutate_original_node
- Root cause: construct.py:109 does item.llm_config = merged directly (and :119 does item.renderer = self.renderer). Both mutate shared Node instances. The try/except (TypeError, ValueError) never fires because Node isn't frozen — the mutation silently succeeds, corrupting the original.
## Trace Similar
Same pattern as gy9r. See trace analysis on gy9r for full codebase scan.
The construct.py bug has 2 mutation sites (llm_config and renderer), both fixed together.
## Architect Review
Fix is mechanical: replace direct assignment with model_copy + list replacement. Combine llm_config and renderer propagation into a single loop pass.
Core invariant: Node instances are immutable after construction.
Routing: ALL_LOW
close_reason: Fixed: model_copy in construct.py, single-pass propagation

title: Oracle models= parameter + body-as-merge DX
description:
## Feature
Extend Oracle with models= parameter for multi-model ensemble. Body of @node IS the merge function.

See GitHub issue KonstantinMirin/neograph#1 for full design.

## Key changes
1. Oracle.models: list[str] | None field in modifiers.py
2. oracle_router injects neo_oracle_model per Send (round-robin)
3. Factory reads neo_oracle_model from state, overrides node.model
4. @node decorator: models= kwarg creates Oracle, registers body as merge_fn
5. Body-as-merge: when models= set, function body receives list[OutputType] at runtime
6. Info log when n % len(models) != 0

## Verification
- New test: models=['reason','fast','creative'] produces 3 variants merged by body
- New test: n=7 + 3 models → round-robin 3,2,2 with info log
- New test: body-as-merge receives list[OutputType]
- Existing Oracle tests still pass (backward compat)
design:
## Architecture
Option A: body-as-merge with models= on Oracle.
1. Oracle.models: list[str] | None. Validation: if models set, n = len(models) unless n also set.
2. oracle_router: Send(..., neo_oracle_model=models[i % len(models)]). Info log if n % len(models) != 0.
3. Factory produce/tool wrappers: read neo_oracle_model from state, use as model_tier override.
4. State model: neo_oracle_model: str | None field when any Oracle has models=.
5. @node decorator: models= kwarg. If models set without merge_fn/merge_prompt, register body as merge_fn.
6. Body-as-merge: function body receives list[OutputType] at runtime.
notes:
## Findings

### Oracle flow (injection points):
1. compiler.py:383-391 — oracle_router creates Send() with neo_oracle_gen_id per generator
2. factory.py:279-282 — scripted wrapper injects gen_id into config
3. factory.py:326-327 — produce wrapper uses model_tier=node.model (OVERRIDE POINT)
4. factory.py:391-403 — tool wrapper uses model_tier=node.model (OVERRIDE POINT)

### Implementation path:
- Oracle.models field in modifiers.py
- oracle_router injects neo_oracle_model=models[i % len(models)] into each Send state
- Factory wrappers (produce + tool) read neo_oracle_model from state, override model_tier
- State model adds neo_oracle_model field when any Oracle has models=
- @node decorator: models= kwarg creates Oracle, body-as-merge

### Body-as-merge:
- When models= set without merge_fn/merge_prompt, register the function body as the merge_fn
- The body receives list[OutputType] at runtime (not the upstream type)
- The decorator synthesizes a merge_fn name and registers it

### Core Invariant:
Oracle with models= is Oracle with per-generator model override — the existing fan-out/barrier/merge topology is unchanged.
close_reason: Implemented in a120656. Oracle models= + body-as-merge.

title: Consolidation pass: close gap between architecture docs and code
description: Senior-architect-ready codebase. Every task has a machine-verifiable grep gate — the task is not done until the gate command returns the expected value. No self-certification.

title: merge_fn exceptions should surface at compile time or fail loud at runtime
description:
## Problem
A merge_fn that throws an exception during execution is silently caught by the consumer (piarch's group_claims), causing the entire Oracle merge to fall back to no-op behavior (singleton groups). The pipeline runs to completion producing garbage results — 3x too many claims, 3x the LLM cost — with zero errors in the log.

The root cause in this case was an AttributeError inside piarch_llm_call (ModelRole.FAST doesn't exist). But any exception in the merge_fn is equally dangerous.

## What NeoGraph should do

1. **At compile time**: validate that merge_fn can be called with the expected signature. If the merge_fn references external symbols, at minimum verify the function is importable without errors.

2. **At runtime**: if the merge_fn throws, NeoGraph should NOT silently continue. It should either:
   a. Re-raise the exception (let RetryPolicy handle it), OR
   b. Log the exception at ERROR level with full traceback and mark the node as failed

Currently: NeoGraph calls the merge_fn and returns whatever it returns. The silent fallback is in the consumer's code (piarch's except Exception: fallback). But NeoGraph could provide a safer pattern — e.g., merge_fn results are validated against the output type, and type mismatches are flagged.

## Impact
An unnoticed AttributeError caused every pipeline run to produce 62 singleton groups instead of ~20 merged groups. This tripled the downstream workload (62 explore+score calls instead of ~20), costing 3x in LLM tokens and time, with no indication anything was wrong.
design:
## Root Cause
make_oracle_merge_fn (factory.py:651-700) calls the merge function and passes the result to _build_oracle_merge_result without validating the return type. A merge_fn that returns the wrong type silently writes garbage to state.

## Fix Approach
In make_oracle_merge_fn, after calling the merge function, validate the result:
- isinstance check against output_model (or the primary type for dict-form outputs)
- If wrong type: raise ExecutionError with clear message naming the merge_fn, expected type, and actual type

This is a one-line addition in each of the three merge_fn paths (merge_prompt, @merge_fn metadata, scripted merge).
notes:
## Reproduction
- Test 1 (merge_fn raises): PASSES — neograph already propagates the exception. The silent failure was in the consumer (piarch's except Exception fallback), not neograph.
- Test 2 (merge_fn returns wrong type): FAILS — neograph silently accepts a string where Claims was expected. No type validation on merge_fn result.
- Failing test: tests/test_modifiers.py::TestOracleMergeFnErrors::test_wrong_return_type_raises_when_merge_fn_returns_bad_type
- Root cause: make_oracle_merge_fn in factory.py does not validate the merge result against output_model.
## Trace Similar
Pattern: unvalidated function results written to state.
1. make_oracle_merge_fn: merge result not validated against output_model (THE BUG)
2. _build_oracle_merge_result: blindly accepts whatever merged value it gets
3. make_subgraph_fn: output extraction uses isinstance check (line 780) — this one IS validated
4. Scripted node wrappers: result from raw_fn written directly to state — no type check

The merge_fn case is the highest-risk because the merge is user code that can silently return wrong types. Scripted nodes are also user code but the type mismatch is more likely to cause downstream errors (next node gets wrong input). The subgraph path is already safe.
## Architect Review
Core invariant: merge_fn results must be validated against the declared output type before being written to state.
Approach: ALL_LOW — add isinstance check in _build_oracle_merge_result, raise ExecutionError on mismatch.
Risk: None — single function, clear fix.
close_reason: Fixed: merge_fn result validated against output type

title: Langfuse import path in observability.mdx is stale
description:
website/src/content/docs/concepts/observability.mdx:129 says: from langfuse.callback import CallbackHandler as LangfuseHandler. Raises ModuleNotFoundError on current langfuse (v4+). The working import is: from langfuse.langchain import CallbackHandler (used correctly in examples/observable_pipeline.py:23).

Fix: update the doc to match the working example. Source: code review issue #4.

title: Audit and fix 13 bare except Exception handlers across src/
description:
## Problem
13 bare `except Exception` handlers across src/neograph/*.py. These suppress real errors, making debugging impossible when things go wrong silently.

## Inventory (13 sites)

### Category 1: get_type_hints fallbacks (6 sites) — NARROW the exception
These catch failures from `typing.get_type_hints()` which can raise NameError, AttributeError, TypeError, or RecursionError on malformed annotations. Should catch `(NameError, AttributeError, TypeError)` instead of bare Exception.
- _construct_validation.py:580
- _di_classify.py:155
- _sidecar.py:116
- decorators.py:404
- decorators.py:465
- decorators.py:658

### Category 2: Best-effort diagnostics (3 sites) — ADD structlog.warning
These are genuinely best-effort (frame walk, graph visualization, dev-mode print). The except Exception is acceptable but should log the swallowed error via structlog.warning so it's visible in debug mode.
- _construct_validation.py:759 (_source_location frame walk)
- compiler.py:265 (describe_graph)
- compiler.py:274 (_print_dag_summary)

### Category 3: Genuinely wrong (3 sites) — FIX
- _llm.py:82 — cost callback inner: except Exception: pass — should catch specific exceptions or at minimum log
- _llm.py:84 — cost callback outer: except Exception: pass — same
- _sidecar.py:103 — lookup_scripted: silently returns None instead of propagating ConfigurationError

### Category 4: Correct (1 site) — NO CHANGE
- _llm.py:368 — except Exception as exc: raise ExecutionError(...) — wraps and re-raises, correct pattern

## Fix
1. Category 1: Replace `except Exception` with `except (NameError, AttributeError, TypeError)`
2. Category 2: Add `log.warning('swallowed_error', exc=str(e))` inside the handler
3. Category 3: Fix the actual logic — cost callback should catch specific exceptions, lookup_scripted should propagate
4. Add structural guard test: no bare `except Exception: pass` in src/

title: Extract _inject_oracle_config helper from 3 factory wrappers
description:
Phase 1.1 of architecture-v2.md section 1 (Factory Unification).

## What
Extract the Oracle config injection block (gen_id + model override) from 3 identical copies in factory.py into a single _inject_oracle_config(state, config) -> RunnableConfig helper.

## Where
- factory.py lines ~298-305 (_make_scripted_wrapper)
- factory.py lines ~339-346 (_make_produce_fn)  
- factory.py lines ~407-414 (_make_tool_fn)

All 3 blocks are identical: read neo_oracle_gen_id from state, read neo_oracle_model, inject into config[configurable].

## How
1. Create helper at the top of factory.py (near _state_get)
2. Replace all 3 inline blocks with one-line calls
3. Run pytest — must pass with zero behavior change

## Verification
grep -c "_inject_oracle_config" src/neograph/factory.py should return 4 (1 def + 3 calls).
All 1076+ tests pass.

title: First-class testing: Node.run_isolated() + fake_llm fixture
description:
Source: Prefect .fn, Dagster direct-invocation
Effort: Small
Breaks API: No

Problem: Testing a NeoGraph Node means mocking FakeLLM, registering scripted fns, running full compile()+run() pipeline. Dagster lets you call my_asset(upstream_value) directly; Prefect exposes my_task.fn(*args).

Proposed:
  # Direct invocation for unit tests
  result = decompose.run_isolated(
      input={'topic': 'x'},
      llm=FakeLLM(...),      # optional, uses configured if None
      config={},              # optional
  )

  # Or a pytest fixture for test-scoped config
  @pytest.fixture
  def neograph_fake_llm():
      with neograph.test.fake_llm() as llm:
          yield llm

  def test_decompose(neograph_fake_llm):
      neograph_fake_llm.returns(Claims(items=['a']))
      result = decompose.run_isolated(input={'topic': 'x'})
      assert result.items == ['a']

Implementation: Node.run_isolated(input, llm=None, config=None) calls make_node_fn(self) and invokes it with a minimal state. No graph, no checkpointer. fake_llm() context manager patches configure_llm for the scope.

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #9
close_reason: Node.run_isolated() shipped — direct invocation for unit tests without compile/run

title: Oracle+Each modifier conflict unguarded in __or__
description: modifiers.py Modifiable.__or__ validates Each+Loop and Oracle+Loop mutual exclusion but NOT Oracle+Each. A node with both Oracle and Each would produce an invalid topology. Fix: add the same guard pattern for Oracle+Each.

title: Rewrite loop tests for new API design
description:
## Problem
5 tests in test_loop.py need updates to match the new API design:
- loop_to= removed (multi-node loops use sub-constructs)
- Each+Loop restricted (use sub-construct inside Each)
- Self-loop param resolution by type

## Test rewrites needed

### test_self_loop_respects_max_iterations
Should pass after self-loop param resolution fix. No test change needed unless it still fails.

### test_self_loop_raises_on_exhaust_error
Same — should pass after param resolution fix.

### test_review_revise_cycle_exits_when_approved
Rewrite: review+revise become a sub-construct with Loop:
```python
refine = construct_from_functions('refine', [review, revise], input=Draft, output=Draft)
refine = refine | Loop(when=lambda d: d is None or d.score < 0.8, max_iterations=10)
pipeline = construct_from_functions('multi-loop', [draft, refine])
```

### test_sub_construct_with_internal_loop
Should pass after sub-construct output extraction fix. Verify.

### test_per_item_loop_with_each_fanout
Rewrite: verify becomes a single-node sub-construct with Loop, then Each wraps the sub-construct:
```python
verify_loop = construct_from_functions('verify', [verify_impl], input=ClaimItem, output=ClaimItem)
verify_each = verify_loop | Each(over='make_claims.items', key='claim_id')
pipeline = construct_from_functions('each-loop', [make_claims, verify_each])
```

### Also add new tests:
- Each+Loop on same node raises ConstructError
- Loop on Construct with incompatible types raises ConstructError
- Self-loop ambiguous type match raises ConstructError

## Files
- tests/test_loop.py

## Verification
- uv run pytest tests/test_loop.py -v → all 8+ tests pass
- uv run pytest -q → full suite passes
close_reason: Tests rewritten as part of neograph-4xia. All 10 loop tests pass.

title: Fix bug neograph-gpn

title: Test audit fixes: TQ-01 through TQ-12
description:
Lightweight formula for executing well-defined beads tasks. Three atoms per task: prep (read and plan), execute (implement and verify), commit. Use instead of task-execute when the task description already contains sufficient context and doesn't need architect review or triage routing.
Total atoms: 1 (baseline) + 3N (per task) + 1 (verify-final) = 3N + 2. Compare task-execute at 7N + 2.
notes:
## Baseline
- pytest: 528 passed, 0 failed
## Final
- Quality: 532 passed, 0 failed (baseline: 528)
- Found 1 real bug: ForwardConstruct tracer doesn't compile for-loops to cycles (neograph-mwx3)
- Fixed: 2 critical closure echoes, 1 trivially-true assertion, 3 forgiving fallbacks, 1 misleading name, 4 missing edge cases

title: Verify clean baseline
description:

title: LOW-11: Trivial assertions in happy-path validation tests
description: TestFanInValidation::test_fan_in_dict_matching_upstreams_passes asserts len==4 which is trivially true. test_zero_upstream_node_inputs_none restates constructor arg. Consider strengthening or accepting 'no exception' as the assertion.

title: render_for_prompt() returning Pydantic model should be BAML-rendered automatically
description:
## Current behavior
_render_single() calls value.render_for_prompt() and returns the result directly. If render_for_prompt() returns a Pydantic model, it becomes a raw object in template vars — not BAML-rendered.

## Desired behavior
If render_for_prompt() returns a BaseModel instance, pass it through the active renderer (BAML/XML/JSON) before inserting into the template. This lets models define typed presentation projections without doing string formatting themselves.

## Pattern
```python
class HydratedResearch(BaseModel):
    # full data for pipeline
    ...
    def render_for_prompt(self) -> ResearchPresentation:
        # returns a slim Pydantic model, NOT a string
        return ResearchPresentation(...)
```

NeoGraph would then BAML-render ResearchPresentation the same way it renders any other Pydantic input.

## Fix
In renderers.py _render_single():
```python
def _render_single(value, renderer):
    if hasattr(value, 'render_for_prompt') and callable(value.render_for_prompt):
        result = value.render_for_prompt()
        if isinstance(result, BaseModel):
            return renderer.render(result)  # re-render the projection
        return result  # string, return as-is
    return renderer.render(value)
```

title: C3: Loop + skip_when — skip bypasses counter increment, infinite hang
description: factory.py: _apply_skip_when returns early (line 120-121) without calling _build_state_update, so neo_loop_count is never incremented. The loop_router sees count < max_iterations, routes back, skip fires again — infinite loop. Fix: _apply_skip_when must increment the loop counter when it fires on a Loop node, or the loop_router must handle the skip case.
close_reason: Fixed in 6a6ac47 with comprehensive regression tests

title: Compile-time: verify sub-construct output boundary contract
description: Verify that at least one internal node in a sub-construct produces a type compatible with Construct.output. Currently a sub-construct can declare output=Foo while no internal node produces Foo — fails silently at runtime as None propagation.
notes: ## Research context: BAML's VIR phase validates boundary contracts (function output types match declared return types). This is the pattern to adopt — verify sub-construct output matches Construct.output at assembly time.

title: Inline error messages with source locations (Pydantic v2-style)
description:
Source: Pydantic v2, Prefect, Dagster
Effort: Medium
Breaks API: No

Problem: Current compile errors are technical but not immediately actionable. Best-in-class frameworks compete on errors.

Target:
  neograph.CompileError: Node 'verify' declares input=ClusterGroup but upstream
    'cluster' produces Clusters.
    └─ hint: did you forget .map(lambda s: s.groups) to fan out per cluster?
    at examples/10_full_pipeline.py:225

Implementation:
- Custom exception class with file, line, snippet, hint fields
- inspect.stack() to capture source location at Node/Construct construction
- Catalogue of common failure modes with tailored hints
- Pydantic-style error format with context arrow

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #8
close_reason: SUBSTANTIALLY ADDRESSED. ConstructError has structured multi-line error messages with source locations, producer inventories, and .map() hints. @node errors include decoration-site file:line pointers (neograph-3hk). The remaining one-liner errors in factory.py/state.py/compiler.py are already clear and actionable. Full Pydantic v2-style overhaul of all error sites is diminishing returns.

title: No test verifies output type correctness (isinstance)
description: Tests check result['x'] is not None and result['x'].items == [...] but never isinstance(result['x'], ExpectedModel). A node returning the wrong Pydantic type passes all tests. Add isinstance assertions to key tests. Source: code review MED-09, TEST-04.

title: Docs: Renderers dedicated page (XML, Delimited, JSON)
description: XmlRenderer, DelimitedRenderer, JsonRenderer only listed in API reference import block. Need a concepts page explaining when to use which, render_for_prompt protocol, Construct.renderer inheritance. Example 12 exists as reference.

title: Create NodeInput/NodeOutput typed containers + ModeDispatch protocol
description:
Phase 2 of architecture-v2.md section 1 (Factory Unification) — Step 1.

## What
Create the typed dispatch protocol that eliminates Any from the factory core:
- NodeInput: wraps single value or fan_in dict. NOTE: values are NOT constrained to BaseModel — real inputs include str, int, list, dict, BaseModel. Use a shape discriminator instead.
- NodeOutput: wraps single value or multi-output dict. Same — not BaseModel-only.
- ModeDispatch(Protocol): execute(node, NodeInput, config, context) -> NodeOutput

## Where
Create in factory.py near the top. These are internal types, not public API.

## Design
See docs/design/architecture-v2.md section 1 for the REVISED specification.

Key points from review:
- NodeInput.single and NodeOutput.single must accept Any (not BaseModel) because _extract_input returns bare values, neo_each_item items, etc.
- ToolDispatch.execute() must include: ToolBudgetTracker creation, dual-path renderer resolution (node.renderer or _get_global_renderer()), oracle_gen_type resolution for dict-form outputs with tools
- ScriptedDispatch receives context_data but does not use it — document this explicitly

## How
1. Define NodeInput, NodeOutput as dataclasses with shape discriminator
2. Define ModeDispatch Protocol with typed execute()
3. Create 3 implementations: ScriptedDispatch, ThinkDispatch, ToolDispatch
4. ToolDispatch must handle: BudgetTracker, renderer, dict-form oracle_gen_type
5. Do NOT wire into _execute_node yet — that is the next task

## Verification
- Import test: all 3 dispatches instantiate
- ToolDispatch handles dict-form outputs with tool_log
- Type check: mypy passes on the protocol

title: Incomplete Each/replicate rename in compiler
description: compiler.py node-level Each expansion still uses old name: _add_replicate_nodes, replicate_router, variable replicate. Subgraph-level uses _add_each_subgraph, each_router, variable each. Incomplete rename. Rename _add_replicate_nodes to _add_each_nodes, replicate_router to each_router, replicate variable to each. Source: code review HIGH-03, CON-02.

title: YAML bomb DoS — no size limit on load_spec input
description: loader.py _parse_input uses yaml.safe_load which is safe against code execution but has no protection against exponential alias expansion (billion laughs). A malicious YAML could exhaust memory. Fix: add a max-size check on input string before parsing (e.g. 1MB limit).

title: TestOperator.test_interrupt_on_failure has bare except
description: Test catches except Exception: pass around the interrupt. Any error (not just GraphInterrupt) is silently swallowed. Resume is commented out. Test proves almost nothing. TestOperatorResume already covers interrupt/resume properly. Delete or fix this test. Source: code review HIGH-05, TEST-02.

title: ToolBudgetTracker.all_exhausted() returns True for empty tool list
description: Edge case: no tools means all_exhausted() is True, which forces immediate response. Source: code review LOW-05, PP-08.

title: Research neograph-beyr
description:

bead itself -- NOT the filesystem.

4. **Check documentation** (doc-first rule)
5. **Engineering checklist** -- answer concretely:
   - DRY: Does similar logic exist?
   - Library idioms: How do your frameworks solve this?
   - Data flow trace: Walk one concrete example through the full chain
   - Existing conventions: What pattern do similar implementations follow?
   - Test infrastructure: What fixtures/helpers exist?
6. **Identify architecture decisions**

   principle that ALL changes for this task must preserve.

- [key findings]

## Risks & Edge Cases

[One sentence: the architectural principle every change must preserve]

## Implementation Plan
1. [first step]
2. [second step]"
```

bd label add neograph-beyr research:complete
```

title: Architect review of neograph-beyr
description:

Run `bd show neograph-beyr` and verify research:complete label is present.

## Review Criteria

1. **Invariant alignment**: Does EVERY step maintain the Core Invariant?
2. **Approach soundness**: Will the plan work?
3. **Risk coverage**: Are risks identified and covered?
4. **Pattern compliance**: Does the plan follow project architecture patterns?
5. **Specificity**: Is the plan specific enough to implement without guessing?
6. **Alternatives**: Was a better approach missed?

## Rate Each Finding

- **LOW**: Minor suggestion, cosmetic, nice-to-have
- **MEDIUM**: Real concern but agent can resolve autonomously
- **HIGH**: Fundamental issue -- user input required

## Write Review

bd update neograph-beyr --append-notes "## Architect Review

Overall: [ALL_LOW | NEEDS_REFINEMENT | NEEDS_USER_INPUT]"

title: Wrap Loop/branch condition calls in try/except
description: User-provided lambda conditions (Loop.when, branch op_fn) can crash on None input with AttributeError. Wrap condition(value) calls in compiler.py with try/except that converts to clear ExecutionError naming the condition and the None value.

title: _extract_json fallback heuristic is dead code (lines 278-282)
description:
The while True loop in _extract_json (_llm.py:239) can only exit via return statements (line 242 when no { found, line 273 when balanced JSON found). The fallback code at lines 278-282 (first-to-last brace heuristic) is unreachable dead code.

This means for unbalanced JSON that looks like a valid start (e.g. '{"key": "value with unmatched brace'), the function retries at pos+1 until it runs out of { characters, then returns text.strip() — never reaching the first-to-last fallback that was intended to handle this case.

Impact: Low. json_repair downstream handles most malformed cases. But the documented fallback behavior is a lie.

Location: src/neograph/_llm.py lines 278-282
close_reason: Invalid: lines 278-282 are covered (100% coverage on _llm.py). The fallback IS reachable — the agent's analysis was speculative.

title: 8 remaining weak assert X is not None in tests
description: test_loop.py (1) and test_spec_loader.py (4) plus others. Followed by isinstance on next line — is not None guard is redundant. grep -c 'assert.*is not None' tests/ shows 8, not 0 as neograph-nesk acceptance said. Merge into isinstance checks.

title: _validate_node_chain doesn't resolve dict-form outputs as per-key producers
description:
neograph-1bp.4 was supposed to add per-key producer resolution for dict-form outputs in the validator. The factory and state compilation handle it correctly (explore_result and explore_tool_log are separate state fields), but _validate_node_chain in _construct_validation.py still sees the whole dict as one producer and rejects downstream nodes that reference individual keys.

Error: Node 'score' in construct 'rw-ingestion' declares inputs['explore_result']=ExplorationResult but upstream 'explore_result' produces dict.

Expected: the validator should see explore's dict outputs {result: ExplorationResult, tool_log: list[ToolInteraction]} and register explore_result and explore_tool_log as individual typed producers.

Location: _construct_validation.py:_validate_node_chain, around where it appends to the producers list. For dict-form Node.outputs, each key should become a separate producer entry.
close_reason: Fixed by neograph-1bp.4 (commit 5d65c90). _validate_node_chain now registers per-key producers for dict-form outputs. Verified: Construct with explore(dict outputs) → score(explore_result) validates correctly.

title: vs_langgraph/04_human_in_the_loop.py unused register_scripted import
description: examples/vs_langgraph/04_human_in_the_loop.py:116 imports register_scripted but never uses it (no Node.scripted in the example). Lints as warning, confuses readers. Fix: remove register_scripted from the import. Source: code review issue #11.

title: MED-03: Stale docstring references deleted _validate_fan_in_types
description: New (kqd refactor). _construct_validation.py:43-44 effective_producer_type docstring says 'Both validator walkers' but the second was deleted in kqd.4. Rewrite to 'The sole validator walker (_validate_node_chain) consults it'.
close_reason: Rewrote effective_producer_type docstring to reference sole validator walker _validate_node_chain.

title: inputs=list[X] (single-type) fails to extract from Each dict[str,X] producer
description: _extract_input in factory.py has two code paths for input extraction: dict-form inputs and single-type inputs. The dict-form path (line ~569) has logic to unwrap dict[str,X] into list[X] via list(values()). The single-type path (line ~578) does NOT have this unwrap — it uses _is_instance_safe which checks isinstance(dict_value, list) which is False. Result: inputs=list[X] (single type) for a downstream of Each receives None at runtime. Workaround: use inputs={'name': list[X]} (dict-form) instead. @node always generates dict-form inputs from the function signature, so this only affects surfaces 2 (Node.scripted) and 3 (programmatic Node). Fix: add dict-to-list unwrap logic to the single-type path, or document that list[X] consumers of Each producers must use dict-form inputs.
close_reason: Fixed: single-type path unwraps dict→list for Each producers

title: forward: ForwardConstruct base class + class-attribute node discovery
description:
Introduce `ForwardConstruct`, a subclass of `neograph.construct.Construct` whose nodes are discovered from class attributes instead of being passed as `nodes=[...]`. No tracing yet — this subtask only lands the class-attribute discovery scaffolding so subsequent subtasks can plug tracing into it.

## Context

- Parent epic: `neograph-pub` — `Construct.forward()` subclass mode for branching logic.
- Design spike: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md` (chosen strategy: Symbolic Proxy). Read this first.
- Reference prototype: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/spike.py` — shows the discovery + shim pattern this subtask productionizes.

## Files

- NEW `src/neograph/forward.py` — module housing `ForwardConstruct`, the `Proxy` class, the tracer, and the `_ForwardSelf` shim. Keep tracing infrastructure scoped to this one module.
- `src/neograph/__init__.py` — re-export `ForwardConstruct` at the top level.
- `tests/test_forward_construct_discovery.py` — new test file (do NOT modify existing tests).

## Signatures

```python
# src/neograph/forward.py
class ForwardConstruct(Construct):
    def __init__(self, name_: str | None = None, /, **kwargs: Any) -> None:
        # 1. Discover Node class attrs via _discover_node_attrs()
        # 2. For this subtask ONLY: pass the discovered nodes in declaration order
        #    directly as the nodes list. (Tracing happens in the next subtask.)
        # 3. Call super().__init__(name_, nodes=discovered, **kwargs)
        ...

    @classmethod
    def _discover_node_attrs(cls) -> dict[str, Node]:
        """Walk cls.__mro__ in reverse; return attr_name -> Node for every
        class attribute that isinstance(v, Node). Subclass attrs shadow base.
        """
        ...

    def forward(self, *args, **kwargs):
        raise NotImplementedError(
            "ForwardConstruct subclasses must override forward()"
        )
```

## Acceptance criteria

1. `class Foo(ForwardConstruct)` with two class-level `Node.scripted(...)` attrs instantiates and `foo.nodes` returns the two Nodes in class-declaration order.
2. Declaration order is preserved across Python versions (rely on `__dict__` insertion order, documented since 3.7).
3. Inheritance works: a subclass that adds a new Node attribute gets parent + new. Overriding an attribute name replaces the parent's.
4. Instantiating `ForwardConstruct` directly (no subclass) raises a clear error — "subclass ForwardConstruct and declare node attributes".
5. `_validate_node_chain` still runs on the discovered list (inherited from `Construct.__init__`), so a bad chain raises `ConstructError`.
6. New test file covers: basic discovery, inheritance/override, direct instantiation error, validation still fires, declaration order.

## Out of scope

- Running `forward()` at all. Discovery only. The next subtask (forward-tracer) adds the actual trace.
- Any behavior that depends on the order being call-order rather than declaration-order — that distinction only matters once tracing lands.
- Touching `compiler.py` or `state.py`. The nodes list is already in the shape they consume.
close_reason: Straight-line ForwardConstruct MVP complete. di0: base class with node discovery via __mro__ walk + Pydantic ignored_types fix. 3us: symbolic proxy tracer (_Proxy + _Tracer + _ForwardSelf + _NodeCall) wired into ForwardConstruct.__init__. vxv: zero compiler changes — tracer output is a plain nodes list that compile()/state-builder/factory consume unchanged. 10 new tests across 3 classes. 169/169 passing.

title: test_hypothesis.py: remove redundant (Construct, meta) strategies — TopologySpec replaces them
description: bare_pipeline(), each_pipeline(), oracle_pipeline(), loop_pipeline(), each_oracle_pipeline() (lines 115-281) produce (Construct, meta) tuples. bare_topology(), each_topology() etc produce TopologySpec. Both exist in parallel doing the same thing. Delete the old strategies and migrate all tests to TopologySpec.
notes:
## Audit Findings (round 1)

### Old system (System A): 13 strategies producing (Construct, dict)
bare_pipeline (L116), each_pipeline (L161), oracle_pipeline (L186), loop_pipeline (L216), each_oracle_pipeline (L246), dict_output_pipeline (L460), sub_construct_pipeline (L487), mixed_modifier_pipeline (L515), each_empty_collection (L568), di_pipeline (L586), skip_when_pipeline (L615), fan_in_pipeline (L902), each_with_dict_outputs (L939) + 5 more inline builders

### New system (System B): 10 strategies producing TopologySpec
bare_topology (L1845), each_topology (L1889), oracle_topology (L1915), each_oracle_topology (L1941), fan_in_topology (L1976), sub_construct_topology (L2011), deep_chain_topology (L2039), loop_topology (L2080), loop_exhaustion_topology (L2127), skip_when_topology (L2157)

### 5 old strategies have TopologySpec equivalents:
bare, each, oracle, loop, each_oracle, fan_in, skip_when

### 8 old strategies have NO TopologySpec equivalent (coverage lost if deleted):
dict_output_pipeline, mixed_modifier_pipeline, each_empty_collection, di_pipeline, node_decorator_pipeline, sub_construct_with_each, nested_sub_construct, each_with_dict_outputs

### 31 test classes use old system, 17 use new system, 7 regression guards are inline

### Breakage risk: any_pipeline union used by 5 test classes (15 test methods)
## Verification (round 2): DISCREPANCIES FOUND

1. Old strategy count is 17, not 13. Audit missed 4: node_decorator_with_di, and 3 others already in the list but undercounted.

2. 'No equivalent' count is 9, not 8. Audit missed node_decorator_with_di.

3. CRITICAL: node_decorator_pipeline is the ONLY Hypothesis-driven test exercising @node + construct_from_functions with randomized type pairs. Losing it loses @node-surface coverage. Deterministic @node tests exist in test_node_decorator.py but the randomized type-pair dimension would be lost.

4. All 9 'no equivalent' strategies are single-use (1-2 callers each). Could be inlined, BUT node_decorator_pipeline and node_decorator_with_di test the @node code path which TopologySpec surface adapters cannot reach (they build via Node.scripted).

5. any_topology_spec_with_loops is dead code (never referenced).
## Prep
### Analysis after reading the full file (3460 lines)

The two strategy systems serve DIFFERENT purposes:
- Old system (L115-L998): Strategies produce (Construct, meta) tuples. Tests exercise specific topology axes (dict outputs, sub+Each, nested subs, @node path, DI, skip_when, operators, error patterns). These are NOT redundant with the new system.
- New system (L1843-L2193): TopologySpec strategies feed a surface adapter (_build_scripted_surface). Tests verify framework-level invariants (immutability, idempotency, determinism, isolation). Different purpose.

### Truly redundant: 5 old strategies + 3 test classes
1. bare_pipeline → bare_topology (both produce random bare chains)
2. each_pipeline → each_topology (both produce Each fan-out)
3. oracle_pipeline → oracle_topology (both produce Oracle ensemble)
4. loop_pipeline → loop_topology (both produce Loop iteration)
5. each_oracle_pipeline → each_oracle_topology (both produce Each+Oracle)
6. any_pipeline → any_topology_spec (union of above 5)

Old-system test classes using ONLY these 5+1:
- TestPipelineTopologyInvariants (4 tests, any_pipeline) — subsumed by TestStateFieldsMatchDeclaredOutputs, TestNoInternalStateLeaks, TestEveryNodeExecutesExactlyOnce
- TestPerComboInvariants (5 tests, per-strategy) — subsumed by framework promise tests
- TestEachKeyCompleteness (2 tests) — subsumed by TestEachFansOutToEveryItem
- TestLoopTermination (2 tests) — subsumed by TestLoopTerminatesAndAccumulates
- TestDescribeGraph (2 tests, bare/each only) — borderline, uses describe_graph() which no new test exercises

### NOT redundant — must keep:
All old strategies from L459-L998 and their test classes (L648-L1793). These exercise unique axes:
- dict_output_pipeline → TestDictFormOutputs (dict-form outputs)
- sub_construct_pipeline → TestSubConstructs (sub isolation)
- mixed_modifier_pipeline → TestMixedModifiers (Each→collector→Oracle in one Construct)
- each_empty_collection → TestEachEmptyCollection (empty Each regression)
- di_pipeline → TestDIPositive (DI params)
- skip_when_pipeline → TestSkipWhen (conditional execution)
- node_decorator_pipeline → TestNodeDecoratorPath (@node code path)
- sub_construct_with_each → TestSubConstructWithEach
- nested_sub_construct → TestNestedSubConstructs
- fan_in_pipeline → TestFanIn (N→1 fan-in)
- each_with_dict_outputs → TestEachWithDictOutputs
- node_decorator_with_di → TestNodeDecoratorWithDI
- Inline tests: TestNodeDecoratorMapOver, TestNodeDecoratorEnsemble, TestListConsumerOfEach, TestMultipleSubConstructs, TestSubConstructWithOracle, TestSubConstructWithLoop, TestTypeSubclassCompat, TestOperatorCombo, TestContextParam, TestRunIsolated, TestCreativeHumanErrors, TestInvalidTopologyErrors, TestDIErrorMessages, TestDIPreFlight

### Approach
1. Delete 5 redundant old strategies + any_pipeline
2. Delete 4 redundant test classes (13 test methods total)
3. Keep TestDescribeGraph (moves to use bare_topology/each_topology instead)
4. Keep all other old strategies and test classes — they test unique axes

Core invariant: Every deleted test must be verifiably subsumed by a kept test. No coverage loss.
## Execution
- Deleted 5 redundant old strategies: bare_pipeline, each_pipeline, oracle_pipeline, loop_pipeline, each_oracle_pipeline + any_pipeline union (~170 lines)
- Deleted 4 redundant test classes (13 tests): TestPipelineTopologyInvariants (4), TestEachKeyCompleteness (2), TestLoopTermination (2), TestPerComboInvariants (5)
- Migrated TestDescribeGraph (2 tests) from old bare_pipeline/each_pipeline to TopologySpec bare_topology/each_topology + _build_scripted_surface
- Moved TestDescribeGraph after TopologySpec section to resolve forward reference
- Quality check: PASS (1296 passed = 1309 - 13 deleted, ruff clean)
- Remaining old strategies (8 unique) kept: dict_output_pipeline, sub_construct_pipeline, mixed_modifier_pipeline, each_empty_collection, di_pipeline, skip_when_pipeline, node_decorator_pipeline, fan_in_pipeline, each_with_dict_outputs, sub_construct_with_each, nested_sub_construct, node_decorator_with_di. All test unique axes not covered by TopologySpec.

title: Adopt msg = ...; raise X(msg) idiom in construct.py raises
description:

title: CRITICAL: No test for gather→produce with tool_log sub-construct pattern
description:
The entire neograph-4a7 epic was motivated by the explore→score pattern: a gather node with tools + dict-form outputs (including tool_log: list[ToolInteraction]) feeding a produce node that consumes explore_result + explore_tool_log in a fresh conversation. This is the core 'agent explores then judges' topology.

No integration test exercises this pattern end-to-end through construct_from_functions(input=, output=). The closest test (test_dict_outputs_flow_when_two_nodes_inside_sub_construct) uses scripted nodes only, no tools, no ToolInteraction.

Required test: construct_from_functions with a gather-mode @node (tools=[...], outputs={'result': X, 'tool_log': list[ToolInteraction]}) feeding a produce-mode @node that consumes {upstream}_result and {upstream}_tool_log, compiled and run inside a parent pipeline. Use FakeTool + ReActFake from tests/fakes.py.

File: tests/test_composition.py, TestNodeSubConstruct class.
close_reason: Example 13 + 4 e2e tests proving the gather→produce+tool_log sub-construct pattern works end-to-end

title: construct_from_functions: accept Construct items alongside @node items
description:
construct_from_functions rejects Construct instances with 'not decorated with @node'. This breaks the primary use case for sub-constructs: mixing @node functions with sub-construct pipelines in the same parent.

## The pattern that doesn't work

piarch has 15 @node functions + 1 sub-construct (verify_claim = explore→score) in the same pipeline. The @node functions reference the sub-construct output by parameter name:

    @node(outputs=ClusterMatchResult, mode='gather', ...)
    def explore(claim: VerifyClaim) -> ...: ...

    @node(outputs=ClusterMatchResult, mode='produce', ...)
    def score(explore_result, explore_tool_log) -> ...: ...

    verify_claim = construct_from_functions('verify-claim', [explore, score],
                                            input=VerifyClaim, output=ClusterMatchResult)

    # Parent pipeline: 15 @node functions + 1 sub-construct
    @node(outputs=ConsolidatedDisposition)
    def deterministic_merge(verify_claim: dict[str, ClusterMatchResult], ...) -> ...: ...

    rw_ingestion = construct_from_functions('rw-ingestion', [
        ..., flatten_claims, verify_claim, deterministic_merge, ...
    ])

This fails at TWO points:

1. construct_from_functions line 862: 'argument is not decorated with @node (got Construct)' — rejects verify_claim
2. Even if you splice it in afterward, the @node validator rejects 'deterministic_merge' because parameter 'verify_claim' doesn't match any @node in the list

## What example 13 does differently

Example 13 avoids this by using Construct(nodes=[...]) with Node.scripted for the parent — NOT construct_from_functions with @node. This means the parent pipeline can't use @node's signature-based dependency inference. The workaround defeats the purpose of @node.

## What should work

construct_from_functions should accept both @node items AND Construct items. For Construct items:
- Name: construct.name (already available)
- Output type: construct.output (already declared)
- The validator should resolve @node params against BOTH peer @nodes AND peer Constructs
- Topology: Construct items are opaque — they produce their declared output type

Alternatively: provide construct_from_mixed('name', [@node_fns...], sub_constructs=[...]) if type heterogeneity in the list is a concern.

## Impact

This blocks the gather→produce sub-construct pattern (neograph-4a7 fix) from being usable in @node-based pipelines. Example 13 proves the sub-construct works, but only in the programmatic API — not in the primary @node API that the README leads with.
design:
## Core Invariant
_build_construct_from_decorated must produce the same node ordering and type relationships as a hand-built Construct(nodes=[...]) containing the same Nodes and sub-Constructs.

## Implementation Plan
1. construct_from_functions: accept both Node and Construct items. Separate into two lists. Validate: Nodes must have @node sidecar; Constructs must have output declared.
2. _build_construct_from_decorated: new param sub_constructs: list[Construct]. Register them in decorated dict (field_name -> Construct). They participate in adjacency (as producers) but skip all sidecar-dependent processing.
3. Adjacency: when pname matches a sub-construct field_name, wire the edge. No sidecar check needed.
4. Topo sort: sub-constructs participate like nodes. They get placed in ordered list.
5. Inputs cleanup + scripted shim: skip for Construct items (isinstance check).
6. Final Construct(): include all items (Nodes + Constructs) in topo-sorted order.

~40 lines of changes, all in decorators.py.
notes:
## Findings

### Two rejection points
1. construct_from_functions L852: isinstance(item, Node) check rejects Construct items
2. _build_construct_from_decorated L980-998: adjacency loop raises 'does not match any @node' for params naming a sub-construct

### Code locations to change
- decorators.py L850-860: construct_from_functions — separate Constructs from Nodes, pass both
- decorators.py L886: _build_construct_from_decorated — accept sub_constructs param
- decorators.py L903-915: decorated dict — also register sub-constructs by field_name (for adjacency resolution)
- decorators.py L940-998: adjacency loop — skip sidecar access for Construct items, resolve params against sub-construct names
- decorators.py L1066-1098: inputs cleanup + scripted shim — skip for Construct items
- decorators.py L1106-1112: final Construct() — include sub-constructs in nodes list at their topo-sorted position

### How Construct items differ from Node items
- No sidecar (no @node decorator) — skip all sidecar-dependent logic
- No inputs/outputs dict (Construct has input/output singular) — use construct.output for producer type
- No mode/fan_out_param/scripted_fn — skip modifier and shim processing
- Constructs already validated via their own __init__

### Pattern from existing code
The Construct.__init__ in construct.py already handles mixed Node+Construct lists: 'nodes: list[Any]'. The compiler's _add_node_to_graph checks isinstance(item, Construct) vs isinstance(item, Node) and dispatches accordingly. So the LOWER LAYERS already handle mixed lists. The gap is purely in the decorator layer's adjacency builder.

### Risks
- Construct items in topo sort: must be treated as nodes with edges. If @node Y depends on Construct X, X must appear before Y in the sorted order.
- Construct.output is singular (not dict-form). A downstream @node param matching a sub-construct name gets the sub-construct's output type. This is already how it works in programmatic Construct(nodes=[...]).
- Each/Oracle on a sub-construct already works in the programmatic API. The topo sort just needs to place it correctly.
## Architect Review
- [LOW] Approach correct: register Constructs in 'decorated' dict for adjacency resolution, skip sidecar processing. Minimal changes.
- [MEDIUM] Type in decorated dict: currently dict[str, Node]. Changing to dict[str, Node | Construct] means isinstance checks needed in sidecar-accessing loops. Alternative: keep a separate sub_constructs dict and check both during adjacency. PREFER separate dict — less risky, clear separation.
- [LOW] Construct.output validation: construct_from_functions should validate Construct has output declared (required for producer type resolution).

Overall: ALL_LOW (the MEDIUM is a style preference, not a blocker)
## Regression Test
- Tests: tests/test_composition.py::TestMixedNodeAndConstruct (3 tests)
- Status: FAILING (TDD red)
- test_construct_from_functions_accepts_construct_items: ConstructError 'not decorated with @node (got Construct)'
- test_construct_from_functions_rejects_construct_without_output: same (doesn't reach output validation yet)
- test_node_param_resolves_against_sub_construct_name: same

title: Model compatibility test suite: schema round-trip across strategies and models
description:
Build a test harness that sends a moderately complex Pydantic schema (nested models, enums, lists, optional fields) through each output strategy (structured, json_mode, text) and verifies the round-trip.

Purpose:
- Catch model-specific parsing failures (R1 XML, thinking tags, markdown fences) BEFORE users hit them
- Verify renderer output (BAML/XML/JSON) produces parseable prompts for each model
- Document which models work with which strategies (compatibility matrix)

The schema should be complex enough to expose real issues:
- Nested BaseModel (e.g., Lead with Experience list)
- Enum fields
- Optional fields with defaults
- list[str] and dict fields

Test dimensions:
1. Rendering: how the schema appears in the prompt (describe_type, XmlRenderer, JsonRenderer)
2. Round-trip: schema → rendered prompt → LLM → parse response → validate against original schema
3. Strategies: structured, json_mode, text
4. Models: reason (Claude), fast (Gemini), creative (GPT-4o), and edge cases (R1, Qwen)

This replaces the whack-a-mole approach of discovering and patching parsing edge cases one model at a time.
notes:
## Scale
Target: every model on OpenRouter (100+ models). Run as a scheduled CI job, not just local tests. Results published as a compatibility matrix — which models work with which strategies (structured/json_mode/text) and which renderers (XML/JSON/delimited).

This becomes a feature of neograph itself: 'pick a model, we already know if it works with your pipeline configuration.'

## Implementation sketch
- One parametrized pytest test: @pytest.mark.parametrize over (model, strategy, renderer)
- Model list pulled from OpenRouter API or a maintained YAML
- Complex Pydantic schema as fixture (nested models, enums, optionals, lists)
- Results stored as JSON artifacts — CI publishes to a page on neograph.pro
- Failures are not test failures — they're compatibility data points. Mark as expected/known.
close_reason: Built and verified: compiles, tests pass

title: Triage review findings for neograph-1bp.2
description:
Read the architect review and decide next steps for neograph-1bp.2.
This is a TRIAGE atom -- it routes, not executes.

### If ALL_LOW:
Note "Clean review -- proceeding to implement." Close this atom.

### If NEEDS_REFINEMENT:
Create a refine atom addressing MEDIUM findings.

### If NEEDS_USER_INPUT:
Block for human direction. Leave this atom OPEN.

## Key Principle

title: Write regression test for neograph-1bp.2
description:
Write a failing test that defines the expected behavior for neograph-1bp.2.
This is the TDD "red" step -- the test MUST exist and MUST fail before
any implementation begins.

## Instructions

1. Identify what to test from the implementation plan
2. Choose test level (prefer integration over unit when DB involved)
3. Write the failing test
4. Use test factories for test data (never inline model construction)
5. Run and confirm it fails for the RIGHT reason (AssertionError, not ImportError)

## BANNED Patterns

- `pytest.mark.xfail` is BANNED in regression tests
- AST-scanning / source-code pattern tests are BANNED
- Direct DB session management in test code is BANNED

## Verification: Paste the FAILED Output

You MUST see the word "FAILED" in pytest output.

## Store Test Reference

```bash
bd update neograph-1bp.2 --append-notes "## Regression Test
- Test: <test_path>::<test_name>
- Status: FAILING (expected -- TDD red step)
- Tests expected behavior: [one sentence]"
```
acceptance_criteria: Failing test exists. Test reference written to task notes.

title: Implement neograph-1bp.2
description:
Implement beads task neograph-1bp.2 -- make the failing test pass (TDD green),
then refactor.

## Workflow

1. **Green**: Write minimum code to make the failing test pass
2. **Verify**: Run the SAME test that FAILED -- must now PASS
3. **Refactor**: With passing tests as safety net
4. **Quality gate**: Run project quality command

## When EXISTING Tests Fail

1. STOP. Do NOT modify the failing tests.
2. Re-read the Core Invariant.
3. If your approach violates the invariant -> revert and rethink.
4. If the test has a genuine bug -> document WHY before modifying.

Sacred rule: NEVER adjust tests to match code without justification.

## If Successful

Close the original beads task:
```bash
bd close neograph-1bp.2
```
acceptance_criteria: Quality command passes. Regression test passes. Original task closed.

title: Research neograph-1bp.3

- [potential issues]"

title: Implement neograph-1bp.7
description:

then refactor.

## Workflow

2. **Verify**: Run the SAME test that FAILED -- must now PASS
3. **Refactor**: With passing tests as safety net
4. **Quality gate**: Run project quality command

## When EXISTING Tests Fail

1. STOP. Do NOT modify the failing tests.
2. Re-read the Core Invariant.
3. If your approach violates the invariant -> revert and rethink.
4. If the test has a genuine bug -> document WHY before modifying.

Sacred rule: NEVER adjust tests to match code without justification.

title: DI pre-flight validation in run()
description:
Before starting any node execution, scan all nodes in the compiled graph, collect their required DI params, and validate that all required keys are present in run(input=) and config.

This catches missing params at the gate instead of mid-pipeline. Especially valuable for long pipelines where a missing param on node 5 of 10 wastes the first 4 nodes' execution.

Implementation: in run() (or a new _preflight_di_check), walk the Construct's nodes, collect DIBindings where required=True, check config['configurable'] keys. Raise ExecutionError listing ALL missing params at once (not one at a time).

Files: src/neograph/factory.py or new src/neograph/_preflight.py
Depends on: required=True default (so there are required params to check)
Guard: test that run() with missing required params raises before any node starts (check that node_start log event never fires).
notes:
## Acceptance
- Write test: run() with missing required params raises ExecutionError before any node starts (node_start log never fires)
- Pre-flight scan collects ALL missing params and reports them in one error (not one at a time)
close_reason: Pre-flight DI check in run(): collects all missing required params before graph.invoke().

title: @node decorator: parameter-name dependency inference (Dagster-style)
description:
Source: Dagster @asset, LlamaIndex @step
Effort: Large
Breaks API: No (additive — legacy Node(...)/Construct(nodes=[...]) stays)

# Motivation

KILLER IDEA #1. The function signature IS the dependency graph. No list, no order, no add_edge.

Current NeoGraph:
    decompose = Node('decompose', mode='produce', output=Claims, ...)
    classify  = Node('classify',  mode='produce', input=Claims, output=Classified, ...)
    pipeline  = Construct('rw', nodes=[decompose, classify])

Dagster-style (proposed):
    @node(mode='produce', output=Claims)
    def decompose(topic: str) -> Claims: ...

    @node(mode='produce', output=Classified)
    def classify(decompose: Claims) -> Classified: ...
    # parameter name 'decompose' matches upstream node 'decompose' → auto-wires

    pipeline = construct_from_module(my_pipeline_module)

Wins:
- No nodes=[...] list, topology inferred from signatures
- Refactor-safe: rename function → downstream parameter is a NameError surface
- Fan-in is free: def report(claims: Claims, scored: Scored, verified: Verified)
- IDE jump-to-definition works on parameter names
- Zero string coupling for topology

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #1

---

# Template: how @tool works (mirror this exactly)

Read `src/neograph/tool.py` lines 89-133 fully before writing a line of code. `@node` must follow the same shape:

1. Accepts both `@node` and `@node(...)` forms (see tool.py:130-132 — the `if fn is not None: return decorator(fn)` trick).
2. Inside the inner `decorator(f)`, does a *function-local* import of the factory to dodge the circular import:
       from neograph.factory import register_scripted   # cf. tool.py:116 'register_tool_factory'
3. Returns a `Node` *instance* (NOT the wrapped function). The instance is what downstream code references. The original function is stashed on the instance so the factory can execute it.
4. Auto-registers any side-effect needed for execution (for @tool it's `register_tool_factory`; for @node it's `register_scripted` when mode='scripted').

Sample shape for @node (pseudocode — not for copy-paste, implementer decides final form):

    def node(
        fn: Callable | None = None,
        *,
        mode: Literal["produce", "gather", "execute", "scripted"] = "produce",
        input: Any = None,
        output: Any = None,
        model: str | None = None,
        prompt: str | None = None,
        llm_config: dict[str, Any] | None = None,
        tools: list[Tool] | None = None,
        name: str | None = None,
    ) -> Any:
        def decorator(f: Callable) -> Node:
            from neograph.factory import register_scripted

            node_name = (name or f.__name__).replace("_", "-")
            inferred_input  = input  if input  is not None else _infer_input_from_hints(f)
            inferred_output = output if output is not None else _infer_output_from_hints(f)

            n = Node(
                name=node_name,
                mode=mode,
                input=inferred_input,
                output=inferred_output,
                model=model,
                prompt=prompt,
                llm_config=llm_config or {},
                tools=tools or [],
                scripted_fn=(f.__name__ if mode == "scripted" else None),
            )
            if mode == "scripted":
                register_scripted(f.__name__, f)
            # Stash the original fn + declared param order for construct_from_module
            n.__neo_fn__ = f                           # type: ignore[attr-defined]
            n.__neo_param_names__ = tuple(             # type: ignore[attr-defined]
                p.name for p in inspect.signature(f).parameters.values()
            )
            return n

        if fn is not None:
            return decorator(fn)
        return decorator

Note: stashing `__neo_fn__` / `__neo_param_names__` requires `Node` to tolerate attribute setting. `Node` is a pydantic BaseModel with `model_config = {"arbitrary_types_allowed": True}` (node.py:67). Pydantic v2 BaseModel blocks arbitrary attr-setting by default — implementer must either (a) add `model_config["extra"] = "allow"` locally for decorator use, (b) use a sidecar `WeakValueDictionary[id(node)] -> fn` in decorators.py, or (c) add explicit (but private) fields on Node. The sidecar approach is recommended because it keeps Node's schema clean.

---

# Node class surface (src/neograph/node.py)

Full list of Node fields `@node` must map from decorated function + decorator kwargs:

| Node field      | Source                                                                        |
|-----------------|-------------------------------------------------------------------------------|
| `name`          | `name` kwarg if given, else `fn.__name__.replace("_", "-")`                   |
| `mode`          | `mode` kwarg (default `'produce'`)                                            |
| `input`         | `input` kwarg if given, else inferred from the first-non-upstream param's annotation (see precedence below) |
| `output`        | `output` kwarg if given, else `fn.__return_annotation__`                      |
| `model`         | `model` kwarg                                                                 |
| `prompt`        | `prompt` kwarg                                                                |
| `llm_config`    | `llm_config` kwarg (default `{}`)                                             |
| `tools`         | `tools` kwarg (default `[]`)                                                  |
| `scripted_fn`   | `fn.__name__` iff `mode == 'scripted'`; else None                             |
| `raw_fn`        | NOT set by @node — raw_node() already covers that path                        |
| `modifiers`     | NOT set — modifiers applied post-hoc via `| Oracle(...)` etc.                 |

Constructor: `Node(name_=..., **kwargs)` — accepts name positionally or as kwarg (node.py:69-73).

The name->state-field transform `node.name.replace("-", "_")` is the canonical rule (see node.py:147, state.py:65/115, factory.py:107, _construct_validation.py:60). Node names are hyphenated; Python identifiers are underscore-separated. @node MUST use this rule in both directions:
- Function name `make_clusters` → node name `make-clusters`
- Downstream parameter name `make_clusters` → matches node `make-clusters`

---

# Construct assembly (src/neograph/construct.py + _construct_validation.py)

`Construct(nodes=[...])` today:
1. Pydantic model with `name`, `description`, `nodes: list[Node|Construct]`, `input`, `output`, `modifiers`.
2. `__init__` accepts name positionally, then calls `_validate_node_chain(self)` AFTER pydantic validation (construct.py:72-79).
3. `_validate_node_chain` walks nodes in list order, accumulating `(field_name, output_type, label)` producers. For each node with declared input, checks upstream produces a compatible type. Uses `name.replace("-", "_")` for field naming (_construct_validation.py:60).

`construct_from_module(mod)` must:
1. Walk `mod.__dict__` (or `vars(mod)`) collecting every value that `isinstance(x, Node)` AND has the `__neo_fn__` sidecar (so it only picks up @node-built nodes, not plain `Node(...)` instances that happen to be module-level — leave those alone).
2. For each such node, read its stashed `__neo_param_names__` to learn its upstream dependencies (by parameter name).
3. Build name-to-node map keyed by `node.name.replace("-", "_")` (the state-field form, which matches the parameter name).
4. Topologically sort: node N depends on every node whose state-field-name appears in N's param list. Raise `ConstructError` on cycles or unknown params.
5. Raise `ConstructError` if any parameter name does not match a node in the module (v1 rule: no scalar params, all params must be upstream nodes — see decisions below).
6. Hand the sorted list to `Construct(name=mod.__name__.split('.')[-1], nodes=sorted_list)`. From that point on, Construct's own `_validate_node_chain` runs the existing type-compatibility walker, so the IR is IDENTICAL to hand-written Constructs. No new validation path.

Return value: a `Construct` instance — same class, same compile()/run() pipeline.

Default construct name: last segment of `mod.__name__` (e.g. `my_app.pipelines.rw_ingest` → `'rw_ingest'`). Allow override via `construct_from_module(mod, name='...')`.

---

# RESOLVED OPEN QUESTIONS (these are decisions, not debate)

## 1. Parameter-name -> node-name mapping

RULE: `param_name == node.name.replace("-", "_")`.

- Function written as `def classify(decompose: Claims) -> Classified: ...` binds to node `'decompose'`.
- Function written as `def report(make_clusters: Clusters) -> Report: ...` binds to node `'make-clusters'`.
- `@node` itself derives node name from `fn.__name__.replace("_", "-")`, so a function `def make_clusters(...)` becomes node `'make-clusters'` whose downstream parameters spell it `make_clusters`. Symmetric.
- Explicit `@node(name="custom-label")` kwarg overrides function name; downstream params then use `custom_label` to reference it.

Justification: matches existing state-field convention everywhere in the codebase.

## 2. Where does @node + construct_from_module live?

FILE: new `src/neograph/decorators.py` — does not exist yet, creating it is clean.

Rationale:
- @tool lives in `tool.py` next to `Tool` — the decorator + data class co-location pattern.
- @node is different: the Node class is large and declarative; adding the decorator there would clutter it and create more circular-import surface (decorator needs to `register_scripted` from factory, which already imports Node). A dedicated `decorators.py` keeps `node.py` declarative-only.
- `construct_from_module` belongs next to `@node` because it's the second half of the same feature — it only makes sense with decorated functions as input.
- Alternative considered (rejected): extend `construct.py`. Rejected because `construct.py` should stay focused on the Construct class + its validation entry point.

Export from `neograph/__init__.py`:
    from neograph.decorators import node, construct_from_module
    __all__ += ["node", "construct_from_module"]

## 3. Type-hint-based `input` / `output` inference

PRECEDENCE (strict): explicit decorator kwarg wins; annotation is the fallback.

    @node(mode='produce', output=Classified)              # output explicit
    def classify(decompose: Claims) -> Classified: ...     # input inferred from param annotation

    @node(mode='produce')
    def classify(decompose: Claims) -> Classified: ...     # BOTH inferred

    @node(mode='produce', input=SomeOtherThing, output=Classified)
    def classify(decompose: Claims) -> Classified: ...     # kwargs override annotations entirely

Inference rules:
- `output` fallback = the function's return annotation. If none → `output=None` (Construct will fail at assembly if a state-writing mode needs it).
- `input` fallback = the annotation of the FIRST parameter whose name matches an upstream node. (For multi-param fan-in, the per-node `.input` field only tracks a single type today — matches the existing Node contract. The other params still drive topology; they just don't feed `_extract_input`.) If the function has no parameters, `input=None`.
- When the kwarg is provided, annotations are ignored entirely (not cross-checked). Simpler rule, fewer surprises.

## 4. Fan-out interaction with Each / .map()

DECISION: OUT OF SCOPE for v1.

@node v1 handles 1:1 dependencies only. A parameter annotated `list[ClusterGroup]` whose upstream produces `Clusters` with `groups: list[ClusterGroup]` will NOT auto-generate `Each(over='clusters.groups', ...)`. The developer must still apply `.map(...)` or wrap the node with `Each(...)` manually AFTER decoration.

Rationale: auto-fan-out requires deciding a `key=` expression, which has no safe default. Deferring to v2 keeps v1 small and obvious.

## 5. Positional vs keyword-only params

DECISION: accept positional (match Dagster).

`def classify(decompose: Claims) -> Classified: ...` — `decompose` is a normal positional param. `@node` uses `inspect.signature(fn).parameters` in declaration order; positional, keyword-only, and positional-or-keyword all count the same. `**kwargs` and `*args` are rejected with `ConstructError` at decoration time (no sensible mapping).

## 6. Non-node function parameters (scalars, run-input kwargs)

DECISION: v1 rejects them.

    @node(mode='produce', output=Report)
    def report(claims: Claims, tenant_id: str) -> Report: ...
    # ConstructError: parameter 'tenant_id' in node 'report' does not match any
    # @node in module 'my_pipeline'. All parameters must name upstream nodes.

Error is raised by `construct_from_module(mod)`, not by `@node` itself (because at @node time we don't yet know which other nodes exist in the module).

Scalar / run-input params are a v2 feature. Workaround for v1: put the scalar in a seed scripted node.

---

# Acceptance criteria

Done when ALL of the following hold:

1. `src/neograph/decorators.py` exists and exports:
   - `node` — decorator implementing sections above (explicit-kwargs path + annotation-fallback path, both `@node` and `@node(...)` call shapes).
   - `construct_from_module(mod, name: str | None = None) -> Construct` — topologically sorts the module's @node-built nodes and hands off to `Construct(...)`.
2. `src/neograph/__init__.py` exports `node` and `construct_from_module`; both are appended to `__all__`.
3. New test class `TestNodeDecorator` in `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py` (add it immediately after `TestToolDecorator`, around line 2167). Required tests:
   - `test_node_decorator_basic_chain` — a 2-node scripted chain built with @node + construct_from_module compiles, runs, and produces the expected output.
   - `test_node_decorator_fan_in_three_upstreams` — `def report(a: A, b: B, c: C)` wires correctly; topological order puts `a`, `b`, `c` before `report`.
   - `test_node_decorator_explicit_kwargs_override_annotations` — `@node(output=X)` wins over `-> Y` return annotation.
   - `test_node_decorator_unknown_param_raises` — param name not matching any @node in module raises `ConstructError` with a clear message.
   - `test_node_decorator_topological_ordering` — given a shuffled module-level declaration order, `construct_from_module` still emits nodes in DAG order.
   - `test_node_decorator_name_underscore_to_hyphen` — function `def make_clusters(...)` becomes node `'make-clusters'`; downstream param spelled `make_clusters` resolves correctly.
4. `examples/01b_scripted_pipeline_decorator.py` exists: it is `examples/01_scripted_pipeline.py` rewritten with `@node(mode='scripted', ...)` + `construct_from_module(sys.modules[__name__])`, producing identical output.
5. `pytest` full suite + the new tests pass. No existing test modified (additive only).
6. No changes to `src/neograph/node.py`, `src/neograph/construct.py`, or `src/neograph/_construct_validation.py`. The decorator must work entirely through the existing public constructors. (Exception: if the sidecar-attribute approach from the Node section requires adding `extra='allow'` to Node's `model_config`, that single-line change is permitted, but the WeakValueDictionary approach is preferred to keep node.py untouched.)

---

# Files to read before starting (in this order)

1. `src/neograph/tool.py` lines 89-133 — the `@tool` decorator. This is the template. Understand the two-form invocation trick, the function-local import for circular avoidance, and the "return a spec instance, not the wrapped function" shape.

2. `src/neograph/node.py` (entire file, ~176 lines) — the `Node` class fields, the `Node.__init__(name_, /, **kwargs)` signature, and `Node.scripted(...)` classmethod. Also note `raw_node` at the bottom — it's a different decorator (wraps a LangGraph-style `(state, config)` function) and must not be conflated with `@node`.

3. `src/neograph/_construct_validation.py` lines 38-67 — `_validate_node_chain`. This is the existing producer-labeling logic @construct_from_module will feed into. Pay attention to line 60: `field_name = name.replace("-", "_")` is the state-field naming rule that matches parameter names.

4. `src/neograph/construct.py` (entire file, ~82 lines) — shows that `Construct(nodes=[...])` already does all validation. `construct_from_module` is purely a sort-and-forward shim.

5. `src/neograph/factory.py` lines 38-68 — `register_scripted`, `register_condition`, `register_tool_factory`. `@node(mode='scripted', ...)` must call `register_scripted(fn.__name__, fn)` so the scripted wrapper in factory.py:124 can find the function by name.

6. `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestToolDecorator` at line 2094 — the test-class template. Use the same style: one test verifying the decorator returns the right spec type, additional tests verifying it works end-to-end through `Construct` + `compile` + `run`. Imports (`configure_fake_llm`, `Claims`, etc.) are already at the top of the file.

7. `examples/01_scripted_pipeline.py` (entire file, ~82 lines) — the canonical example. The decorator-port lives next to it as `01b_scripted_pipeline_decorator.py`; same schemas, same scripted functions, same output, but the pipeline is built from @node-decorated functions + `construct_from_module`.

---

# Non-goals (explicit out-of-scope for v1)

- Fan-out auto-generation from `list[T]` annotations (see Decision #4).
- Scalar / run-input parameters (see Decision #6).
- Class-based module walking (`construct_from_module(SomeClass)`). v1 walks module objects only.
- Replacing or deprecating the legacy `Node(...) + Construct(nodes=[...])` API. Both coexist forever.
- Changes to the compile() / run() / runner layer. @node is purely an ergonomic front-end; the IR is unchanged.
close_reason: Parallel team execution via /team (execute-dur-ebe team). ebe: 3-line validator widening for dict class + dict[X,Y] generic shapes, preserves dict-instance case. 2 new tests in TestConstructValidation. dur: new src/neograph/decorators.py implementing @node + construct_from_module (~289 lines), 6 new tests in new TestNodeDecorator class, new examples/01b_scripted_pipeline_decorator.py as a port of 01_scripted_pipeline.py. Node.py untouched (sidecar pattern via id-keyed dict + weakref.finalize). Scripted @node functions route through raw_fn to support fan-in nodes with N upstream parameters — small deviation from the brief, verified by test_node_decorator_fan_in_three_upstreams. 111/111 passing (103 prior + 2 ebe + 6 dur).
