title: ForwardConstruct: for/while loops don't compile to graph cycles
description: ForwardConstruct traces forward() once symbolically and records node call order. Python for/while loops don't compile to LangGraph cycles — the tracer sees each node called once in the loop body but doesn't detect the loop structure itself. The test test_for_loop_compiles_to_cycle had a trivially-true assertion that hid this. Review only ran once. This is a known limitation — ForwardConstruct loop support requires cycle detection in the tracer.
design:
## Decision: Explicit loop primitive (JAX/TF model)

Python for/while loops cannot be detected by a tracer without AST inspection. AST locks us into Python and breaks TypeScript parity. Every major tracing framework (JAX, TF, Haiku, Flax) uses explicit loop primitives instead.

### API: self.loop()

```python
class Writer(ForwardConstruct):
    draft = Node.scripted('draft', ...)
    review = Node.scripted('review', ...)
    revise = Node.scripted('revise', ...)

    def forward(self, topic):
        d = self.draft(topic)
        d = self.loop(
            body=[self.review, self.revise],
            when=lambda r: r.score < 0.8,
            max_iterations=5,
        )(d)
        return d
```

### Loop types
- for (fixed N): self.loop(body=[...], max_iterations=N)(state)
- while (condition): self.loop(body=[...], when=lambda s: ..., max_iterations=N)(state)
- do-until: same — Loop already runs body at least once before checking

### Implementation
self.loop() returns a proxy that the tracer records as a sub-construct with Loop modifier. Maps 1:1 to the YAML spec format and to the programmatic construct | Loop() API.

### Why not AST
- Locks into Python (no TypeScript parity)
- Restricted Python subset (same pitfall as TorchScript)
- Two parsers to maintain long-term

### Why not pure tracing  
Every framework that tried (torch.trace, JAX raw) failed. The tracer sees duplicate node calls but can't extract loop semantics (condition, max_iterations, break predicate).
notes:
## Reproduction
- Failing test: tests/test_loop.py::TestForwardConstructLoop::test_for_loop_review_cycles_when_score_below_threshold
- Root cause: _Tracer.record() deduplicates by id(node) -- repeated calls to the same Node in a for-loop are no-ops. The tracer discovers loop-body nodes but does not detect the loop structure itself. No back-edge is generated, so the compiled graph is a linear chain (review runs once), not a cycle.
## Triage Result: NEEDS_REFINEMENT
- Failing regression test: tests/test_loop.py::TestForwardConstructLoop::test_for_loop_review_cycles_when_score_below_threshold (skip-marked)
- Root cause confirmed: _Tracer dedup prevents cycle detection
- Fix requires design spike: tracer architecture change for loop detection + Loop modifier lowering
- Loop modifier infrastructure (compiler, state, factory) already works
- All 7 triage atoms closed
## Resolution Path
Option C (explicit loop primitive) chosen. Language-agnostic, maps to YAML spec format. Python for/while in forward() documented as unsupported — use self.loop() instead.

title: _neo_input DI stash lost across process restarts — checkpoint resume breaks FromInput
description:
## Problem
run() stashes input fields in config['configurable']['_neo_input'] (in-memory) for Operator resume to re-inject FromInput DI params. This stash does not survive process restarts. After a crash, the restarted process calls run(graph, config=...) to resume from checkpoint, but config has no _neo_input — FromInput params resolve to None.

## Current behavior
1. First run: run(graph, input={'node_id': 'X'}) → stashes _neo_input in caller's config dict
2. Process crashes
3. New process: run(graph, config={'configurable': {'thread_id': 'same'}}) → resumes from checkpoint but FromInput('node_id') resolves to None

## Expected
FromInput params should resolve correctly after crash recovery.

## Fix options
1. Persist _neo_input in checkpoint metadata (LangGraph supports custom metadata)
2. Require the caller to re-provide input values in config on resume
3. Store DI values in the graph state itself (not just config)
design:
## Design: Option C + preflight check
1. Add _preflight_di_check(graph, config) to the crash-recovery path (input=None, resume=None)
2. Update run() docstring with crash-recovery DI pattern
3. Keep _neo_input stash for Operator in-process resume (works, harmless)
4. Do NOT remove _neo_input — it's a convenience for the common case (same process)
Scope: ~5 lines production + docstring + 1 test
notes:
## Research: Option C (caller re-provides) is correct

### Why other options fail
- Option A (checkpoint metadata): LangGraph drops non-primitives from metadata, doesn't merge metadata back into config on resume
- Option B (state fields): Creates split world — FromInput auto-persisted but FromConfig (rate limiters) not. Inconsistency worse than the bug
- Option D (configurable persistence): LangGraph explicitly does NOT persist full configurable dict. By design.

### Why Option C is correct
- DI contract: values come from caller's config. On resume, re-provide them.
- FromInput and FromConfig follow the same contract — both read from config['configurable']
- piarch's DI params (node_id, project_root) are static per-run, trivially re-providable
- FromConfig params (rate limiters) are inherently non-serializable — MUST be re-provided

### Implementation
1. Add _preflight_di_check to crash-recovery path (3 lines)
2. Document crash-recovery DI pattern in docstring
3. Consider removing _neo_input stash (creates false expectation, only works in-process)

### Core invariant
DI values come from the caller's config. Checkpoints persist graph state, not DI config.

title: Loop modifier: cyclical graphs for iterative refinement (0.3.0)
description:
Design research complete. neograph currently only supports DAGs. Cycles enable iterative refinement — the most common real-world agent pattern (draft→review→revise until approved).

## Recommended design: Loop modifier + ForwardConstruct while loops

Three API surfaces, same IR:

### @node decorator
@node(outputs=Draft, loop_when='needs_refinement', max_iterations=5)
def refine(draft: Draft) -> Draft: ...

### ForwardConstruct
def forward(self, topic):
    d = self.draft(topic)
    for _ in range(5):
        r = self.review(d)
        if r.approved:
            break
        d = self.revise(d, r)
    return d

### Programmatic
node | Loop(when='condition', max_iterations=5, reenter='earlier_node')

## Type safety challenge
Back-edge requires: node output compatible with reentry node input. Self-loop: outputs must be subtype of inputs. Multi-node: last node output must match reentry node input. The validator checks this at assembly time.

## Framework survey summary
- LangGraph: conditional back-edges via routing functions + recursion_limit
- Pydantic AI: implicit agent loop, model decides when to stop
- DSPy: assertion-driven retry (module-level, not topology)
- Temporal: plain while loops in workflow code
- Naiad: typed ingress/feedback/egress loop boundaries (academic, strongest type model)

## Implementation plan (7 steps)
1. Loop modifier in modifiers.py (when, max_iterations, reenter, on_exhaust, history)
2. Validator rule: loop output compatible with reentry input
3. effective_producer_type: Loop does not change output type
4. State compiler: neo_loop_count + optional neo_loop_history
5. Compiler: _add_loop_back_edge with conditional edge + iteration guard
6. @node kwarg: loop_when= and max_iterations= sugar
7. ForwardConstruct tracer: recognize for/while, emit _LoopNode sentinels

## Open questions
1. Self-loop accumulation: last-write-wins vs list? (propose: LWW default, history=True for list)
2. Multi-node validation: simple last→reentry check or full sub-chain? (propose: simple)
3. Nested loops: defer to Phase container for 0.4.0
4. Each+Loop composition: Loop wraps Each (entire fan-out re-executes)
5. Operator+Loop: Operator fires after loop exits (modifier ordering)

Deferred to 0.3.0 — not blocking the 0.2.x release.
design:
## Feature Design: Loop Modifier

### Loop class (modifiers.py)

class Loop(Modifier):
    when: str | Callable    # condition function or registered name. Returns True to continue looping.
    max_iterations: int = 10
    reenter: str | None = None  # back-edge target node name (None = self-loop)
    on_exhaust: Literal['error', 'last'] = 'error'
    history: bool = False   # collect iteration history in state

Validation in model_post_init:
- max_iterations >= 1
- when is str or callable

### State model (state.py)

When a node has Loop modifier:
- Add neo_loop_count_{field_name}: int = 0  (tracks current iteration)
- If history=True: Add neo_loop_history_{field_name}: list = []  (append reducer)

### Validator (_construct_validation.py)

New rule in _validate_node_chain:
- Self-loop: node.outputs must be compatible with node.inputs via _types_compatible
- Multi-node (reenter=X): last node's outputs must be compatible with reentry node's inputs
- Raise ConstructError('loop output type X not compatible with reentry input type Y') on mismatch

### Compiler (compiler.py)

New function _add_loop_nodes(graph, node, loop, prev_node, retry_policy):

For self-loop:
1. Add node function (graph.add_node)
2. Create loop_condition_fn(state) that:
   - Reads neo_loop_count, checks < max_iterations
   - Calls loop.when(state) or lookup_condition(loop.when)(state)
   - Returns node.name to continue loop, or next node/END to exit
3. graph.add_conditional_edges(node.name, loop_condition_fn)

For multi-node (reenter=X):
1. Add all nodes in the loop body normally
2. On the last node (the one with Loop modifier), add conditional back-edge to reenter node
3. The condition_fn checks neo_loop_count + loop.when

Iteration counter:
- The node wrapper increments neo_loop_count_{field_name} on each execution
- Compiler wrapper or factory wrapper handles the increment

on_exhaust='error': condition_fn raises ExecutionError when count >= max_iterations and when() still True
on_exhaust='last': condition_fn returns next/END when count >= max_iterations regardless of when()

### Factory (factory.py)

The existing _make_produce_fn / _make_tool_fn / _make_scripted_wrapper wrappers need to increment neo_loop_count when the node has Loop modifier. Add to _build_state_update:
- If node has Loop: update['neo_loop_count_{field}'] = current_count + 1
- If Loop(history=True): update['neo_loop_history_{field}'] = result

### @node decorator (decorators.py)

New kwargs: loop_when=, max_iterations=, loop_to=, on_exhaust=
Sugar that compiles to | Loop(...):

if loop_when is not None:
    loop_kwargs = {'when': loop_when, 'max_iterations': max_iterations or 10}
    if loop_to: loop_kwargs['reenter'] = loop_to
    if on_exhaust: loop_kwargs['on_exhaust'] = on_exhaust
    n = n | Loop(**loop_kwargs)
    _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)

### __init__.py

Export Loop from neograph.

### Modifier composition order

Loop interacts with other modifiers:
- Loop + Operator: Operator fires after loop exits (last in modifier chain)
- Loop + Each: Each wraps Loop — each fan-out item loops independently
- Loop + Oracle: Not supported initially (raise ConfigurationError)

### Implementation sequence (7 subtasks)

1. Loop class in modifiers.py + export in __init__.py (schema only)
2. State model: neo_loop_count + neo_loop_history fields
3. Validator: back-edge type compatibility check
4. Compiler: _add_loop_nodes with conditional back-edge
5. Factory: loop counter increment in state update
6. @node decorator: loop_when= / max_iterations= / loop_to= kwargs
7. Integration test verification (make test_loop.py pass)
close_reason: Loop modifier feature complete. Self-loops on Nodes, Loop-on-Construct for multi-node bodies, Each+Loop restriction. 430 passed, 0 failed.

title: Stop mutating Node.inputs during assembly
description: _construct_builder.py:468-469 mutates Node.inputs after construction to strip DI params. Violates immutable IR. Fix: use model_copy to produce a new Node with cleaned inputs, or separate the 'raw inputs from signature' from 'wiring inputs after DI strip' into two fields.
design:
## Core Invariant
Node instances must be immutable after construction — all assembly-time adjustments produce new Node instances via model_copy, never mutate existing ones in place.

## Implementation Plan
1. In the inputs cleanup loop (lines 486-521): accumulate updates dict, call model_copy once per node, replace in ordered list
2. In the oracle_gen_type inference loop (lines 542-548): same pattern — model_copy + replace
3. In _register_node_scripted (line 628): return the synthetic_name, let caller do model_copy + replace
4. Structural guard: AST-scan _construct_builder.py for direct attribute assignment on loop variables (pattern: identifier.attr = expr where identifier matches loop vars iterating ordered). Must be 0.

## Design decision: single model_copy per node
Rather than 3 separate model_copy calls (inputs, fan_out_param, scripted_fn), accumulate all updates into one dict and call model_copy once. More efficient, cleaner.

## _register_node_scripted change
Change from: mutates n.scripted_fn in place
Change to: returns synthetic_name string, caller does model_copy(update={'scripted_fn': name})
notes:
## Findings

### Mutation sites in _construct_builder.py
1. **Line 516**: `n.inputs = filtered` — rewrites inputs dict to strip DI/constant params and rewrite port/loop keys
2. **Line 521**: `n.fan_out_param = fan_out_name` — marks which input key reads from neo_each_item
3. **Line 628**: `n.scripted_fn = synthetic_name` — sets scripted function dispatch name
4. **Line 548**: `n.oracle_gen_type = gen_type` — deferred oracle merge type inference

### Additional mutation in decorators.py (NOT in scope)
Lines 503, 586: oracle_gen_type set during decoration (creation-time, acceptable)

### How ordered list flows
`ordered` list → `Construct(nodes=ordered)` at line 550. Same object references. All mutations happen in-place on objects that become the Construct's node list.

### Fix pattern
`model_copy(update={...})` is the established pattern (modifiers.py:193, 530, 544, 564, 572). Replace node in `ordered` list at mutation index.

### Key risk: sidecar preservation
model_copy preserves __pydantic_private__ (node.py:111), so _sidecar and _param_res survive. Verified in test_sidecar_survives_model_copy.

### Relevant code
- src/neograph/_construct_builder.py:486-548 (cleanup loop + oracle inference)
- src/neograph/_construct_builder.py:559-628 (_register_node_scripted)
- src/neograph/modifiers.py:193 (model_copy pattern)

### Risks & Edge Cases
- _register_node_scripted receives `n` by reference and mutates `n.scripted_fn`. After model_copy for inputs/fan_out_param, need to either: (a) pass the new copy to _register_node_scripted, or (b) also model_copy for scripted_fn inside _register_node_scripted. Option (a) is cleaner — replace node in ordered list BEFORE the scripted registration loop.
- oracle_gen_type mutation at line 548 is in a separate loop AFTER the inputs cleanup loop. Must also be converted to model_copy + list replacement.
- Ordered list is a plain list; replacement is ordered[i] = new_node.
## Architect Review
- [LOW] Finding: Design says 'single model_copy per node' but mutations happen across 3 separate loops (inputs cleanup, oracle inference, scripted registration). Suggestion: Accept 2-3 model_copy calls per node rather than merging loops — simpler, less risky. The cost is negligible (happens once at assembly time, not in the hot path).
- [LOW] Finding: _register_node_scripted return-value change is clean but changes the function signature. Suggestion: Proceed — internal function, no external callers.
- [LOW] Finding: Guard should also catch oracle_gen_type mutation (line 548), not just inputs/fan_out_param. Suggestion: Guard scans for ANY direct attr assignment on loop variables iterating `ordered`.

Overall: ALL_LOW
## Regression Tests
- Test: tests/test_structural_guards.py::TestNoNodeMutationInAssembly::test_no_direct_attr_assignment_on_nodes_in_ordered_loops
  Status: FAILING (4 violations: n.inputs, n.fan_out_param, n.scripted_fn, n.oracle_gen_type)
- Test: tests/test_node_decorator.py::TestNodeImmutabilityDuringAssembly::test_inputs_not_mutated_after_construct_assembly
  Status: FAILING (port param rewrite mutates original node's inputs dict)
- Test: tests/test_node_decorator.py::TestNodeImmutabilityDuringAssembly::test_fan_out_param_not_mutated_on_original_node
  Status: FAILING (fan_out_param set from None to 'item' on original node)
close_reason: Immutable Node during assembly: 4 mutation sites → model_copy. Structural guard + behavioral tests.

title: Compile-time type errors at Construct assembly, not at compile() call
description:
Source: Haystack cautionary tale (GitHub issue #8862), Dagster static analysis
Effort: Small-medium
Breaks API: No (catches bugs earlier)

Problem: When a Node declares input=Claims, we check at compile() time whether any upstream produces Claims. Move this check to Construct(nodes=[...]) assembly time via __post_init__ with full Pydantic error messages and source locations from inspect.stack().

Implementation:
- Construct.__post_init__ walks nodes in order
- For each node with declared input, verifies an upstream node produces a compatible output
- Raises a Pydantic-style error with file:line from inspect.stack() frame
- Error includes: node name, declared input, upstream chain, suggestion

Example target:
  neograph.CompileError: Node 'verify' declares input=ClusterGroup but upstream
    'cluster' produces Clusters.
    └─ hint: did you forget .map() to fan out per cluster?
    at examples/10_full_pipeline.py:225

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #4
close_reason: Assembly-time type validation: Construct.__init__ now walks the node chain after super().__init__() to validate each node's declared input against upstream producers. Raises ConstructError (ValueError subclass) with source location + .map() hint when upstream has list[X]. Handles Each path resolution (walks dotted path through model_fields, unwraps list[X], verifies element type). ForwardRefs resolved via get_type_hints for modules using . Defers to runtime when producers empty (first-of-chain) or Each root is runtime-seeded. 14 new tests, 99/99 passing. Examples 04/10 continue to work.

title: Audit and strengthen coverage-driven tests (behavioral contracts)
description:
Phase continuous of architecture-v2.md section 4 (Test Behavioral DISCIPLINE — renamed from "contract" per review).

## What
Audit and strengthen tests from the coverage push. This is process discipline, not structural enforcement — be honest about that.

## Audit targets
1. Empty test classes with pass bodies — fill or remove (test_coverage_gaps.py, test_obligation_r1r2.py)
2. Assertions that check "is not None" instead of specific values — strengthen
3. Forgiving scripted test functions that accept any input shape via isinstance+fallback — make strict
4. Manual monkeypatching with try/finally in test_cli.py — convert to pytest monkeypatch fixture

## Principle
Every test assertion must trace to a specific behavioral claim. "It does not crash" is not a claim. "It returns Claims with items=[a,b,c]" is.

## Enforcement mechanisms (from revised architecture doc)
1. /test-obligations analysis catches weak assertions during review
2. grep -c "assert.*is not None" as a CI metric (track, dont gate yet)
3. Strict test function factories: test_helpers.make_strict_scripted(expected_type, fn)
4. Obligation coverage in beads: every test links to an obligation ID

This is NOT structurally enforceable via the type system. It requires review discipline and the obligation analysis workflow.

## How
1. grep for "assert.*is not None" in test files — replace with value checks
2. grep for "class.*:$" followed by "pass" — fill or remove
3. Replace isinstance+fallback patterns with strict type assertions
4. Convert try/finally monkeypatching to pytest fixtures

## Verification
- grep -c "assert.*is not None$" tests/ = 0 (no lone is-not-None checks)
- grep -c "^    pass$" tests/ in test classes = 0
- All scripted test fns assert expected input type on first line

title: Pluggable prompt input renderer — the missing half of produce mode
description:
Produce mode has three output strategies (structured / json_mode / text) but only one input strategy: JSON dump. This asymmetry is visible in every production pipeline the moment a produce node receives a Pydantic model with prose or nested structure — the LLM sees escaped \n-laden JSON and the consumer pays the token cost.

**SCOPE:** This issue is about INPUT rendering only. Output schema emission is handled by output_strategy — see sibling issue for TypeScript-style output schema improvement to the json_mode path.

## The problem

When decompose(read_node: ReadContext) runs, its input is serialized via model_dump_json(indent=2) and dropped into the prompt. For ReadContext with a 5KB markdown file in raw: str, the LLM sees 8KB of escaped JSON with backslash-n everywhere. Every piarch pipeline will have this issue — ~60-80 distinct models containing prose across 15 artifact types.

No existing framework solves this cleanly:
- DSPy: delimited field headers + per-type dispatch (close, but still JSON-dumps nested models)
- BAML: user-directed Jinja templating (punts rendering to the author)
- Pydantic AI: format_as_xml helper (good but isolated, not integrated with templating)
- LangChain: f-string interpolation with str() coercion (worst)

## Proposed design — three-layer approach

### Layer 1: Pluggable Renderer protocol

class Renderer(Protocol):
    def render(self, model: BaseModel) -> dict[str, str]: ...

Three built-ins:
- XmlRenderer (default) — Pydantic AI format_as_xml style with include_field_info='once'
- DelimitedRenderer — DSPy-style [[ ## field ## ]] with per-type dispatch
- JsonRenderer — current behavior, opt-in backward compat

Output is dict[str, str] — pre-rendered string values per field, ready to drop into a Mustache/Jinja template.

### Layer 2: Per-node/per-model override

@node(renderer=XmlRenderer(include_field_info='once'))
def decompose(read_node: ReadContext) -> ClaimGroupingResult: ...

Or at model level:
class ReadContext(BaseModel):
    model_config = {'neograph_renderer': 'xml'}
    ...

Or full custom via method:
class ReadContext(BaseModel):
    def render_for_prompt(self) -> str: ...

Dispatch order: method > model config > node override > graph default.

### Layer 3: Inspector

graph.render_prompt(node_name, input) -> str

Returns the exact string the LLM would see, without making an LLM call. Matches BAML's VS Code playground and DSPy's inspect_history(). Critical for prompt engineering iteration.

## What it does NOT change

- Templating stays separate (Mustache/Jinja)
- Registry stays separate (Langfuse/LangSmith Hub/Braintrust)
- Output strategies (structured/json_mode/text) unchanged
- The prompt_compiler hook in configure_llm stays as the final override

## IMPLEMENTATION NOTE: port, do not import

PORT the algorithm from these sources, do NOT take them as dependencies:

1. DSPy's format_field_value() from dspy/adapters/utils.py (~40 lines) — per-type dispatch for str/int/list/dict/BaseModel. Apache-2.0, port with attribution.

2. Pydantic AI's format_as_xml() from pydantic_ai.format — XML rendering with include_field_info='once'. MIT, port or import (small scope, clean API).

3. Field description extraction from Pydantic's native Field(description='...') metadata — no external dep needed.

Rationale against importing DSPy as a library:
- DSPy owns the LM call via dspy.LM, conflicts with LangChain/langfuse
- DSPy adapters work on Signatures, not Pydantic models — requires translation
- DSPy Predict drives the whole call; using adapter alone means calling semi-private functions
- ~50MB of transitive deps (datasets, optuna, litellm, etc.) for 300 lines of rendering
- DSPy's 'programming not prompting' philosophy conflicts with @node's 'functions ARE the prompt structure'

Porting takes ~1 day, understanding DSPy enough to use it right takes a week, and porting gives us full control over rendering for eval determinism.

## Concrete piarch examples

ReadContext — large raw markdown field, small scalar fields
DecompositionResult — list of RawClaim with prose statements
ClusterMatchResult — dict of dispositions with evidence text
StakeholderInterests — nested stakeholders with interests list
UCOverview — multi-section prose document
BRDocument — structured fields all containing prose
BDDFeature — Gherkin scenarios with steps

All 15 piarch pipelines have models like these. Current JSON dump is visibly wrong for every one.

## References

- DSPy ChatAdapter format_field_value — dspy/adapters/chat_adapter.py + dspy/adapters/utils.py
- Pydantic AI format_as_xml — ai.pydantic.dev/api/format_as_xml/
- Anthropic prompting guide — XML-for-inputs rationale
- Research report: see piarch constructs research session on 2026-04-05

## Success criteria

1. ReadContext renders as markdown block with sections list as bullets, not JSON
2. DecompositionResult renders as numbered claims with prose, not JSON array
3. Token count per render ≥30% lower than current JSON dump for prose-heavy models
4. Users can inspect rendered prompts without calling LLM via graph.render_prompt()
5. Backward compat via JsonRenderer opt-in
6. Zero changes to Langfuse/prompt registry integration
7. Zero new heavy dependencies (no dspy-ai, no baml, no litellm)
design:
## Architecture (from plan session 2026-04-06)

### Dispatch hierarchy (4 levels + backward compat)

| Level | Where set | How |
|-------|-----------|-----|
| 1. Model method | `def render_for_prompt(self) -> str` on BaseModel | Most specific. Checked per-value inside render_input() before any renderer runs. |
| 2. Node | `Node('x', renderer=XmlRenderer())` or `@node(renderer=...)` | Per-node override. |
| 3. Construct | `Construct('p', renderer=DelimitedRenderer(), nodes=[...])` | Pipeline default. Propagated to child nodes via __init__ (same pattern as llm_config). |
| 4. Global | `configure_llm(..., renderer=XmlRenderer())` | Applies to all pipelines. Checked at factory render time. |
| 5. None | No renderer configured | Raw Pydantic model to prompt_compiler. Identical to today. |

Resolution: model method > node.renderer (may be from Construct propagation) > global > None.

### New file: src/neograph/renderers.py

```python
@runtime_checkable
class Renderer(Protocol):
    def render(self, value: Any) -> str: ...

class XmlRenderer:  # Pydantic AI format_as_xml style
    def __init__(self, *, include_field_info: Literal['once', 'always', 'never'] = 'once'): ...

class DelimitedRenderer:  # DSPy-style [[ ## field ## ]] headers
class JsonRenderer:       # Explicit opt-in for model_dump_json

def render_input(input_data: Any, *, renderer: Renderer | None) -> Any:
    # For dict-form (fan-in): render per-value. Checks render_for_prompt() first.
```

### Integration points

**Node + Construct fields:**
- node.py: add `renderer: Any = None`
- construct.py: add `renderer: Any = None` + propagation in __init__ (same llm_config pattern)
- decorators.py: add `renderer=` kwarg to @node, pass through to Node()

**Factory (between _extract_input and invoke_*):**
```python
input_data = _extract_input(state, node)
effective_renderer = node.renderer or _get_global_renderer()
rendered = render_input(input_data, renderer=effective_renderer)
result = invoke_structured(..., input_data=rendered, ...)
```
Scripted nodes NOT rendered (Python code, not LLM prompts).

**configure_llm:**
```python
def configure_llm(llm_factory, prompt_compiler, *, renderer=None): ...
```

**Inspector:**
```python
def render_prompt(node: Node, input_data: Any, *, config=None) -> str:
    # Returns exact prompt without LLM call. Public export.
```

### Files to modify
- src/neograph/renderers.py (NEW)
- src/neograph/node.py (1 field)
- src/neograph/construct.py (field + propagation)
- src/neograph/decorators.py (kwarg passthrough)
- src/neograph/factory.py (render_input in produce + tool fns)
- src/neograph/_llm.py (configure_llm renderer kwarg, render_prompt)
- src/neograph/__init__.py (exports)

### Tests (~29 tests in 6 classes)
TestRendererDispatch (10): all 5 levels, fan-in dict per-value, model method wins, None passthrough
TestRendererThreeSurfaces (4): @node / declarative / programmatic / ForwardConstruct
TestXmlRenderer (7): flat, nested, lists, include_field_info modes, prose no-escaping
TestDelimitedRenderer (3): flat, nested, lists
TestJsonRenderer (2): model_dump_json, custom indent
TestRenderPromptInspector (3): basic, with renderer, json_mode includes schema

### Depends on
- neograph-7as (describe_type) — renderers reuse it for include_field_info

### Core invariant
Rendering is additive. No renderer configured = raw passthrough = identical to today. The consumer's prompt_compiler contract is unchanged; it just receives pre-rendered strings instead of raw Pydantic models when a renderer is active.
close_reason: All 6 subtasks complete. Renderer protocol with 4-level dispatch, 3 built-in renderers, render_prompt inspector, full test coverage.

title: Doc: .modifiers backward-compat bridge creates list on every access
description: Modifiable.modifiers property returns self.modifier_set.to_list(), creating a new list on every access. In hot paths this could be wasteful. Compiler uses .modifier_set.combo (good), but any accidental .modifiers use in a loop creates lists repeatedly. Document this explicitly.
close_reason: All doc corrections applied to architecture-v2.md in single edit pass

title: prompt_compiler should receive output_model and llm_config for json_mode prompts
description: When output_strategy is json_mode, the prompt compiler needs the output Pydantic model's JSON schema to inject it into the prompt (e.g. 'Return a JSON object matching this schema: ...'). Currently prompt_compiler only receives (template, data, node_name, config). It should also receive output_model and llm_config so consumers can build provider-specific prompts. Without this, json_mode produces FILE blocks instead of JSON because the prompt doesn't ask for JSON.

title: Move _BranchNode definition to IR layer (node.py or construct.py)
description:
## Problem
_BranchNode is defined in forward.py (DX layer) but imported by state.py, compiler.py, _wiring.py, and _construct_validation.py (IR/compiler layer). This violates the layer discipline rule that forward-specific logic should not leak down.

_BranchNode already inherits from Modifiable (which is in the IR layer). The class definition should be co-located with its base class.

## Proposed fix
Move _BranchNode and _BranchMeta to node.py or a shared _ir_types.py module. forward.py imports from there instead of defining it.

## Source
Expert architectural review concern #6 (Medium). Already discussed in session — inherits Modifiable, fast path handles it. Move is cosmetic but improves layer discipline.
close_reason: Won't-fix: moving _BranchNode/_BranchMeta/_ConditionSpec from forward.py to IR layer touches 6+ files for a purely cosmetic improvement. _BranchNode already inherits Modifiable (IR layer).

title: Deprecate single-type inputs= in favor of dict-form only
description: The senior architect review's #1 recommendation. _extract_single_type does O(N) isinstance scan of all state fields — first match wins, ambiguous when two upstreams produce same type. Dict-form inputs={'name': Type} matches by name, no ambiguity. @node already infers dict-form from function signatures. Deprecation path: warn on single-type inputs for non-first nodes, then remove _extract_single_type and InputShape.SINGLE_TYPE entirely.
design:
## Architect Decision: NEEDS_REFINEMENT — split into phases

### Analysis
The auto-normalize approach (inputs=X → inputs={'_input': X} at construction time) DOESN'T WORK at Node construction time because the producing node name isn't known yet. It must happen at Construct assembly time after topology is resolved.

The correct approach:
1. In _validate_node_chain, when encountering single-type inputs, resolve the producing upstream by type and rewrite to dict-form inputs={'upstream_name': Type}
2. Remove InputShape.SINGLE_TYPE and _extract_single_type from factory.py
3. Update EACH_ITEM classification (isinstance check on single-type path)
4. Update 179 test occurrences, 15 examples, 17 website pages

### Problem: scope too large for one task
- Auto-normalize requires _validate_node_chain refactor
- 179 test + 15 example + 17 website changes = massive churn
- Risk is theoretical (no real bug from type ambiguity yet)
- Only downstream user (piarch) uses @node exclusively (already dict-form)

### Recommended phased approach
Phase 1 (this task, minimal): Add DeprecationWarning when single-type inputs used on non-first nodes. No behavioral change. Signals direction.
Phase 2 (future task): Auto-normalize in _validate_node_chain at assembly time.  
Phase 3 (future task): Remove _extract_single_type, update tests/examples.

### Triage: NEEDS_REFINEMENT
Rescope this task to Phase 1 only (deprecation warning). File Phase 2+3 as separate tasks.
notes:
## Research Findings

### InputShape.SINGLE_TYPE references
- factory.py: definition (403), classification fallback (428), extraction (495-508), dispatch (523-524)
- test_structural_guards.py: guard tests (613, 633, 640-641)

### _extract_single_type (factory.py:495-508)
O(N) isinstance scan of all state fields — first match wins. Fragile: depends on field insertion order, can match wrong value when multiple fields have compatible types.

### Blast radius: HIGH
- 179 test occurrences across 40 files
- 15 example occurrences across 8 files
- 17 website doc occurrences across 11 pages
- Programmatic API (Node(inputs=X)) depends on it — documented as primary for LLM-driven runtime construction
- ForwardConstruct loop body wiring (forward.py:582) produces single-type
- EACH_ITEM classification path only works with single-type

### Cannot simply delete
Programmatic API surface depends on it. CLAUDE.md: 'the programmatic form is not legacy — primary path for runtime-assembled pipelines.'

### Recommended approach: auto-normalize at construction time
Convert inputs=X → inputs={'_input': X} at Node.__init__/validator time. External API unchanged (Node(inputs=X) still works). Internally always dict-form. Eliminates InputShape.SINGLE_TYPE, _extract_single_type, and EACH_ITEM isinstance-scan.

### Core invariant
Three API surfaces produce different inputs shapes converging through single validator + single runtime extractor. Removing SINGLE_TYPE requires either (a) converting programmatic/FC to produce dict-form (inventing param names), or (b) auto-wrapping single-type into synthetic dict at construction time.
close_reason: Phase 1 done: DeprecationWarning for single-type inputs. Phases 2+3 (auto-normalize, remove) deferred.

title: @node: Oracle ensemble kwargs (ensemble_n=, merge_fn=, merge_prompt=)
description:
Part of epic neograph-4mx (@node production readiness). Surfaced by the Path A Dogfood epic.

## Problem

The Oracle modifier (`src/neograph/modifiers.py:127-156`) provides N-way ensemble + judge-merge via `node | Oracle(n=3, merge_prompt='rw/merge')` or `node | Oracle(n=3, merge_fn='combine')`. There is no way to attach an Oracle modifier to a @node-decorated function. Users writing LLM pipelines with ensemble-judge patterns cannot use @node.

Repro:
    # Desired
    @node(mode='produce', output=Claims, prompt='rw/decompose', model='reason',
          ensemble_n=3, merge_prompt='rw/decompose-merge')
    def decompose(topic: RawText) -> Claims: ...

    # Today
    decompose = Node(name='decompose', mode='produce', output=Claims,
                    prompt='rw/decompose', model='reason') | Oracle(n=3, merge_prompt='rw/decompose-merge')
    # Can't combine with @node at all — the Oracle modifier would have to be
    # applied after decoration, and neograph-jyw shows that modifier composition
    # on decorated nodes is broken (sidecar doesn't carry).

## Proposed API

Add three kwargs to `@node`:
- `ensemble_n: int | None = None` — number of parallel generators
- `merge_fn: str | None = None` — registered scripted function name (alternative to merge_prompt)
- `merge_prompt: str | None = None` — LLM judge prompt (alternative to merge_fn)

Semantics:
- If any of the three is set, attach `Oracle(n=ensemble_n, merge_fn=merge_fn, merge_prompt=merge_prompt)` to the Node at decoration time (not at construct_from_module time — the modifier is node-local).
- Validation at decoration time:
  - `ensemble_n` set but neither merge_fn nor merge_prompt → raise ConstructError
  - Both merge_fn and merge_prompt set → raise ConstructError (matches existing Oracle.model_post_init)
  - `ensemble_n` < 2 → raise (ensemble of 1 is pointless)
  - merge_fn or merge_prompt set without ensemble_n → default ensemble_n to 3
- The resulting Node carries the Oracle modifier in its `modifiers` list, same as the declarative path.

## Implementation sketch

In `src/neograph/decorators.py`:

    from neograph.modifiers import Each, Oracle

    def node(
        fn=None,
        *,
        mode='produce',  # or after q54 lands: None (inferred)
        ...,
        ensemble_n: int | None = None,
        merge_fn: str | None = None,
        merge_prompt: str | None = None,
    ):
        ...
        def decorator(f):
            ...
            n = Node(...)
            _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)

            # Attach Oracle modifier if any ensemble kwarg is set.
            if ensemble_n is not None or merge_fn is not None or merge_prompt is not None:
                if merge_fn is None and merge_prompt is None:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{node_name}' sets ensemble_n={ensemble_n} but \"
                        f\"neither merge_fn nor merge_prompt. One is required.\"
                    )
                if merge_fn is not None and merge_prompt is not None:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{node_name}' sets both merge_fn and merge_prompt. \"
                        f\"Choose exactly one.\"
                    )
                n_copies = ensemble_n if ensemble_n is not None else 3
                if n_copies < 2:
                    raise ConstructError(
                        f\"@node '{node_name}' ensemble_n must be >= 2, got {n_copies}.\"
                    )
                # Use `|` to append the modifier (returns a new Node via model_copy).
                # Must re-register the sidecar on the new instance — see neograph-jyw
                # (sidecar carryover on modifier composition). This subtask assumes
                # neograph-jyw has landed.
                n = n | Oracle(n=n_copies, merge_fn=merge_fn, merge_prompt=merge_prompt)
                _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)

            return n

## Acceptance criteria

- New tests in `TestNodeDecorator`:
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_with_merge_fn`: `@node(mode='produce', ensemble_n=3, merge_fn='combine')` end-to-end with a FakeLLM and registered merge function; result matches N-way ensemble semantics.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_with_merge_prompt`: same with `merge_prompt='judge/prompt'`.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_default_n`: `@node(merge_fn='combine')` without `ensemble_n` defaults to `n=3`.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_no_merge_raises`: `ensemble_n=3` without merge_fn or merge_prompt raises at decoration time.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_both_merge_raises`: both merge_fn and merge_prompt raises.
  - `test_node_decorator_oracle_n_too_small_raises`: `ensemble_n=1` raises.
- Oracle modifier is attached to the Node's `modifiers` list — verifiable via `node.get_modifier(Oracle)`.
- Integration tests from TestOracle still pass unchanged (this adds an API surface, doesn't change semantics).
- Update `@node` docstring with an Oracle usage example.
- Full suite stays green.

## Dependencies

- Blocks on **neograph-jyw** (Each interop via sidecar carryover) — the same sidecar-on-modifier-copy mechanism is reused here for Oracle. Do not duplicate the fix.
- Blocks on **neograph-q54** (mode inference) — for the docstring example. Soft dep, not hard.

## Files to read

- `src/neograph/modifiers.py:127-156` — Oracle class, model_post_init validation
- `src/neograph/decorators.py` — current `@node` decorator
- `tests/test_e2e_piarch_ready.py::TestOracle` — existing Oracle test patterns
- `tests/fakes.py::StructuredFake` — LLM fake for Oracle tests

## Non-goals

- Do NOT edit `src/neograph/modifiers.py` — Oracle class stays as-is.
- Do NOT support Oracle on non-produce modes (ensemble over tool-calling is a different feature).
close_reason: Three modifier-kwargs subtasks on neograph-4mx. nqe: Oracle ensemble_n/merge_fn/merge_prompt kwargs, 6 tests. t5z: mode='raw' support replacing @raw_node, 4 tests. 6y3: Operator interrupt_when kwarg (string + callable forms with auto-registration), 6 tests. 143/143 passing (+16 new).

title: Each over non-existent field path silently compiles
description: Node | Each(over='nonexistent.field', key='x') compiles without error. Should raise ConstructError at compile time: the 'over' path must reference a real upstream field. Guard: test that bogus Each.over raises ConstructError.
notes: REOPENED: executor closed this as 'defers unknown roots to runtime' — but that IS the bug. Each.over='nonexistent.field' should fail at compile time, not silently pass through. The xfail test in test_hypothesis.py documents the expected behavior.
close_reason: Validator now rejects unknown Each.over roots at compile time.

title: Kill sidecar: replace _param_resolutions with PrivateAttr on Node
description:
Kill sidecar: replace _param_resolutions with PrivateAttr on Node.

METHODOLOGY: Write the structural guard test FIRST.

Step 1: Add a test to tests/test_structural_guards.py that greps for "_param_resolutions" dict usage in src/neograph/ and asserts 0 matches (the type alias ParamResolution can stay).

Step 2: Add PrivateAttr to Node:
  _param_res: dict | None = PrivateAttr(default=None)

Step 3: Replace all _register_param_resolutions(n, res) with n._param_res = res.
Replace all _get_param_resolutions(n) with (n._param_res or {}).

Step 4: In Modifiable.__or__, copy _param_res after model_copy.

Step 5: Remove _param_resolutions dict, _register_param_resolutions, _get_param_resolutions functions.

Step 6: Run guard test — must pass.
Step 7: Run full test suite — must pass.

Depends on neograph-6ipl (same pattern, do _node_sidecar first).

GATE: uv run pytest tests/test_structural_guards.py -v passes
GATE: uv run pytest -q --tb=line passes with 0 failures
design: Add _param_res: dict[str, DIBinding] = PrivateAttr(default_factory=dict) to Node. Remove _param_resolutions dict, _register_param_resolutions, _get_param_resolutions. Replace all lookups with n._param_res. Core invariant: DI bindings travel with the Node, not in a global dict.
notes:
MANDATORY: Step 1 of every task is writing a structural guard test in tests/test_structural_guards.py that FAILS before the work starts. The guard runs on every pytest invocation and is the acceptance criterion — not self-reports, not grep. The task is not done until the guard passes AND remains in the test file permanently.
## Research findings
Same as neograph-6ipl. _param_resolutions follows identical pattern.
PrivateAttr: _param_res: dict[str, DIBinding] = PrivateAttr(default_factory=dict)
All _register_param_resolutions(n, res) → n._param_res = res
All _get_param_resolutions(n) → n._param_res or {}

title: H2: Dunder field traversal in condition evaluator — info disclosure

title: Each fan-in validation double-skip: fan_out_param + _each_skip_used both fire
description:
When a node has both fan_out_param set (from @node decorator) AND has_each=True, the _check_fan_in_inputs validator double-skips unmatched keys. fan_out_param skips the fan-out receiver at line 416, but _each_skip_used remains False, so line 422-424 allows ONE ADDITIONAL unmatched key to pass silently. A typo in inputs dict is never caught.

Repro:
  n = Node.scripted('consumer', fn='f', inputs={'claims': Alpha, 'item': Beta, 'mystery': Gamma}, outputs=Gamma)
  n = n | Each(over='claims', key='value')
  object.__setattr__(n, 'fan_out_param', 'item')
  producer = Node.scripted('claims', fn='f', outputs=Alpha)
  Construct('test', nodes=[producer, n])  # should raise ConstructError for 'mystery' but doesn't

Fix: initialize _each_skip_used = True when fan_out_key is not None (the fan-out receiver slot is already consumed by the explicit fan_out_param).

Location: src/neograph/_construct_validation.py line 414
Test: tests/test_obligation_r1r2.py::TestEachFanInValidationEdgeCases::test_fan_out_param_set_plus_each_skip_double_fires

title: RetryPolicy support for LLM nodes
description:
NeoGraph-compiled graphs have no retry on LLM failures. The classic LangGraph pipeline uses RetryPolicy(max_attempts=3) on all LLM-calling nodes, which handles:
- Malformed JSON from json_mode (R1 occasionally produces broken JSON)
- Pydantic validation errors (LLM output doesn't match schema)
- Transient API errors (429s, timeouts)

Without retry, a single malformed JSON response crashes the entire pipeline. With retry, the node silently retries and usually succeeds on the second attempt.

Options:
1. compile() accepts a retry_policy= that applies to all LLM nodes
2. @node(retry=3) per-node declaration
3. construct_from_functions(..., retry_policy=...) at construct level

The rate limiter already handles 429 retries at the HTTP level. This is about node-level retry for parse/validation failures.

title: Each modifier: suppress duplicate_key warnings on checkpoint resume
description: When a pipeline resumes from checkpoint, the Each modifier encounters keys already written by the previous execution pass. It logs each_duplicate_key warnings with action=kept_existing. This is correct behavior (keeping existing results) but produces 76+ noisy warnings per resume. Either suppress the warning on resume, or log once with a count instead of per-key.
close_reason: Fixed: batched duplicate key log (debug level, single line with count)

title: Oracle: generators use node.outputs type for LLM schema, but merge_fn changes the type
description:
When a node has Oracle(merge_fn=...) and the merge_fn returns a DIFFERENT type than what each generator should produce, the framework uses node.outputs as the LLM schema for each generator. This is wrong — the generator should produce its own type, and the merge transforms it.

## Concrete case

```python
@node(outputs=ClaimGroupingResult, model='reason', prompt='rw/decompose',
      ensemble_n=3, merge_fn='group_claims')
def decompose(read_node: ReadContext) -> ClaimGroupingResult: ...

@merge_fn
def group_claims(variants: list[DecompositionResult], ...) -> ClaimGroupingResult:
    # Takes 3x DecompositionResult, groups equivalent claims, returns ClaimGroupingResult
    ...
```

What happens:
- outputs=ClaimGroupingResult, so each generator's LLM call uses ClaimGroupingResult.model_json_schema() as the json_mode schema
- The LLM is asked to produce ClaimGroupingResult (which has groups: list[ClaimGroup] with gen_id cross-references) — impossible for a single generator to produce
- The LLM produces something that doesn't parse → generators return None/empty → merge gets empty variants → pipeline produces nothing

What should happen:
- Each generator should produce DecompositionResult (inferred from merge_fn's first parameter type: list[DecompositionResult] → element type is DecompositionResult)
- The framework should use DecompositionResult.model_json_schema() for each generator's LLM call
- The merge_fn receives list[DecompositionResult], returns ClaimGroupingResult
- node.outputs should reflect the POST-MERGE type (ClaimGroupingResult) for downstream consumers

## The type flow

```
Generator LLM call → DecompositionResult (per-generator type, from merge_fn signature)
merge_fn(list[DecompositionResult]) → ClaimGroupingResult (post-merge type, = node.outputs)
downstream node consumes → ClaimGroupingResult (from node.outputs)
```

## How to infer the generator type

The merge_fn's first parameter is typed as list[DecompositionResult]. The element type IS the generator output type. The framework can inspect this at compile time:

```python
import inspect, typing
sig = inspect.signature(merge_fn)
first_param = list(sig.parameters.values())[0]
# first_param.annotation is list[DecompositionResult]
element_type = typing.get_args(first_param.annotation)[0]  # DecompositionResult
```

## Impact

This blocks any Oracle where the merge transforms the type — the most common real-world pattern. The workaround (making generators and merge return the same type) forces unnatural API design.
notes:
## Reproduction
- Test: test_oracle_generator_uses_merge_input_type_not_node_outputs
- Status: FAILING (confirmed bug)
- Log shows 'output=PostMerge' for generator calls — should be PerVariant
- Pydantic validation error: 'Input should be PostMerge' when PerVariant is written to collector
- Root cause: _resolve_primary_output(node) returns node.outputs which is the POST-MERGE type
- Fix locations:
  1. factory.py: _resolve_primary_output or the produce wrapper needs to use the generator type, not node.outputs, when Oracle is active
  2. state.py: compile_state_model builds the collector as list[node.outputs] — should be list[generator_type]
  3. compiler.py: _add_oracle_nodes could store the inferred generator type on the node or pass it to the factory
  4. Inference: inspect merge_fn signature, get_args(first_param.annotation)[0] = element type
## Trace Similar
- The same issue applies to gather/execute modes (not just produce) — _make_tool_fn also uses _resolve_primary_output(node)
- The state model collector type in state.py:_add_output_field (line 169) is Annotated[list[output_type], _collect_oracle_results] where output_type = node.outputs — also wrong for type-transforming merge_fn

## Architect Review
- [MEDIUM] Where to store the generator type? Options:
  A. Node field (node.oracle_generator_type) — requires node.py change (IR layer)
  B. Infer at compile time from merge_fn signature — requires compiler to pass it down
  C. Infer at factory time from merge_fn registry lookup — runtime overhead each call
  PREFER B: infer once at compile time, store result for factory use. The compiler already resolves the merge_fn. Use a new field on Node: oracle_gen_type.
- [LOW] State model collector needs the generator type too (list[gen_type] not list[output_type])
- [LOW] The @node decorator with ensemble_n= should also infer and store the gen type

Overall: ALL_LOW (the MEDIUM is a placement choice, B is clearly best)

title: Loop + dict-form inputs with multiple keys crashes on iteration 2+
description: _extract_input lines 515-518: on Loop re-entry, wraps latest output under first_key=next(iter(node.inputs)). If the first key is NOT the self-reference key, the Loop output gets placed under the wrong key. Other keys are never populated. Iteration 2+ crashes with AttributeError. Root cause: blindly uses first_key instead of finding the input key whose type matches the Loop output. Fix: find the compatible key, place latest there, read remaining keys from state normally. Discovered by /test-obligations analysis (obligation EI-10).
close_reason: Fixed: validation exempts Loop self-ref key, runtime dispatches correctly for multi-key dicts

title: Harden _types_compatible against future Union/UnionType drift
description:
_types_compatible handles Union/Optional unwrapping with target_origin is Union or target_origin is types.UnionType. This is subtly correct but fragile across Python versions.

Fix: add explicit tests for all Union forms:
- Optional[X] (which is Union[X, None])
- X | None (which is types.UnionType on 3.10+)
- Union[X, Y]
- Annotated[Optional[X], ...] (nested)

GUARD: Write parametrized tests in test_validation.py covering all Union forms against _types_compatible. Include a test with from __future__ import annotations to catch string-annotation edge cases.

GATE: uv run pytest tests/test_validation.py -q passes
close_reason: Fixed _types_compatible Union handling and added missing imports in test guard class

title: Tool results in ToolMessage: use describe_type schema + renderer, not model_dump_json
description:
When a tool returns a Pydantic model, the ToolMessage content (what the agent LLM sees during the ReAct loop) should use describe_type for the schema header + the configured renderer for the instance — same as every other prompt input. Currently it uses model_dump_json which is raw JSON values with no field descriptions.

## File: src/neograph/_llm.py, lines ~367-380

### Current (just shipped in neograph-iura):
if isinstance(result, _BM):
    rendered = result.model_dump_json(indent=2)
...
messages.append(ToolMessage(content=rendered, ...))

### Should be:
if isinstance(result, _BM):
    from neograph.describe_type import describe_type
    schema_header = describe_type(type(result))
    # Use the node's renderer (or global) to render the instance
    instance_text = _render_tool_result(result, renderer)
    rendered = f'{schema_header}\n\n{instance_text}'
...
messages.append(ToolMessage(content=rendered, ...))

## What the LLM sees now (model_dump_json):
{
  "source_file": "auth.py",
  "line": 42,
  "snippet": "def authenticate(user, password): ...",
  "relevance": 0.95
}

## What it should see (describe_type + renderer):
Tool result schema:
{
  source_file: string  // Path to the source file
  line: int  // Line number
  snippet: string  // Code snippet at that line
  relevance: float  // Relevance score 0-1
}

<source_file>auth.py</source_file>
<line>42</line>
<snippet>def authenticate(user, password): ...</snippet>
<relevance>0.95</relevance>

## Why this matters
- describe_type includes Field(description=...) annotations — the LLM knows what each field MEANS
- The renderer (XML/delimited/JSON) is consistent with how all other inputs are rendered
- model_dump_json gives values without context — the LLM has to guess field semantics

## Implementation detail
invoke_with_tools needs access to the node's renderer. Currently it doesn't receive it. Two options:
A. Pass renderer as a parameter to invoke_with_tools
B. Use the global renderer via _get_global_renderer() inside invoke_with_tools

Option A is cleaner — the factory already has the node, passes renderer down.

## Scope
- Only the ToolMessage content changes (what the agent LLM sees)
- ToolInteraction.result gets the same rendered text (backward compat: still a string)
- ToolInteraction.typed_result stays the original model (unchanged)
- Downstream nodes receive typed_result — how THEY render it is their business

## Acceptance criteria
- ToolMessage.content includes describe_type schema header for Pydantic tool results
- ToolMessage.content uses the configured renderer for the instance
- Non-Pydantic tool returns (str, dict) unchanged — str() as before
- All existing tests pass
- New e2e test: verify ToolMessage content has schema header + rendered instance

title: _BranchNode duck-typing persists — plain class mimicking Node
description: Still a plain class mimicking Node/Construct attributes. Every isinstance check in compiler wiring must explicitly handle it. Should inherit from a shared Protocol or base.
close_reason: Won't-fix: _BranchNode already inherits Modifiable and sets modifier_set. Making it a Pydantic model adds complexity for zero benefit.

title: Create di.py module with DIResolver and DIBinding
description:
Phase 2 of architecture-v2.md section 3 (DI Unification).

## What
Extract DI resolution into a dedicated module: src/neograph/di.py.

REVISED from review:
- DIKind enum: FROM_INPUT, FROM_CONFIG, FROM_INPUT_MODEL, FROM_CONFIG_MODEL, FROM_STATE, CONSTANT (6 values — NO UPSTREAM, that is _extract_input not DI)
- DIBinding dataclass: name, kind, inner_type, required, payload
- DIResolver class with resolve(binding, config, state=None) -> value
- DIResolver handles config-based resolution ONLY. Upstream resolution stays in _extract_input.
- FROM_STATE is merge_fn-only — resolved from graph state by param name. Handled by resolve() when state is provided.
- Loop unwrap (_unwrap_loop_value) applies to FROM_STATE values only (they come from state which may have append-lists).

## Where
New file: src/neograph/di.py. Extracted from decorators.py (_resolve_di_value, _classify_di_params).

## Design
See docs/design/architecture-v2.md section 3 (REVISED). Three resolution scopes:
1. DIResolver — config-based (from_input, from_config, bundled, constant)
2. Upstream — stays in _extract_input (NOT part of DIResolver)
3. from_state — merge_fn-only, DIResolver.resolve() with state param

The scripted shim in _register_node_scripted interleaves DI-resolved args with upstream-resolved args. DIResolver covers ONLY the DI part. The shim still handles port params, loop renames, and fan-out params itself.

## How
1. Create di.py with DIKind (6 values), DIBinding, DIResolver
2. Move _resolve_di_value logic into DIResolver.resolve()
3. from_state handling: if kind == FROM_STATE, read from state + apply _unwrap_loop_value
4. Have decorators.py import from di.py
5. _resolve_merge_args becomes: for each param, call DIResolver.resolve(binding, config, state)
6. Keep ParamResolution type alias as bridge
7. Run full test suite

## Verification
- Import: from neograph.di import DIResolver, DIBinding, DIKind
- DIKind has exactly 6 values (no UPSTREAM)
- All existing DI tests pass
- grep -c "_resolve_di_value" src/neograph/decorators.py = 0 (moved to di.py)
- Bare except Exception replaced with except (ValidationError, TypeError, ValueError) — import ValidationError from pydantic

title: Test gap: FromConfig(required=True) + config=None not tested in lint
description: Obligation W-21: Symmetric gap — from_input(required) + config=None is tested, but from_config(required) + config=None is not. Same code path (lint.py lines 85-96) handles both, but the from_config equivalence class is untested.

title: Docs: Node.map() lambda fan-out method
description: Node.map(source, key=) provides refactor-safe lambda-based fan-out. Barely documented — mentioned in passing only. Needs dedicated example comparing string-form vs lambda-form.

title: @node context= for verbatim state injection into prompts
description:
Nodes that consume pre-formatted context (graph catalog, domain briefing) need a way to declare state fields that get injected verbatim into the prompt — not BAML-rendered, not part of the typed input model.

Use case: piarch's explore node needs the graph catalog (incident-encoded, BFS-ordered) alongside its typed VerifyClaim input. The catalog is pre-computed by an upstream node and stored in state. It must not be re-rendered — the format is crafted for LLM consumption.

Proposed API:
  @node(
      mode='agent',
      prompt='match-verify/explore',
      context=['build_catalog'],  # state fields injected verbatim
      tools=[search, read],
  )
  def explore(claim: VerifyClaim) -> ExplorationResult: ...

Template uses {context.build_catalog} placeholders. Framework looks up state fields by name, passes strings through as-is. Typed input still BAML-rendered via {input}.

Three rendering strategies unified:
- Typed input (VerifyClaim) → BAML (generic)
- Tool results (SearchResult) → renderer dispatch (custom or BAML)
- Context (catalog, briefing) → verbatim (this feature)
close_reason: context= field on Node + @node, factory extraction, prompt compiler injection. 416 tests.

title: Each×Oracle fusion + dict-form outputs: state model creates wrong collector field
description:
## Bug

When a Node has Both Each and Oracle modifiers (Each×Oracle fusion) AND dict-form outputs, the state model creates the wrong collector field name. The compiler's _add_each_oracle_fused() writes to neo_eachoracle_{field_name}, but _add_output_field() in state.py takes the Oracle-only branch (line 232) which creates neo_oracle_{field_name} instead.

## Root cause

In state.py:_add_output_field (line 231-247), when node.outputs is a dict AND node.has_modifier(Oracle), it unconditionally takes the Oracle path. It does NOT check for Each×Oracle fusion. The Each×Oracle handling exists in _add_single_output_field (line 260), but that function is never reached for dict-form outputs when Oracle is present.

## Impact

Runtime crash: InvalidUpdateError from LangGraph because per-key fields (verify_result, verify_meta) are created as plain fields without reducers, but M×N concurrent generators all write to them simultaneously.

## Reproduction

Node with outputs={'result': Result, 'meta': Meta} | Each(over='seed.items', key='id') | Oracle(n=2, merge_fn='merge_fn')

Crashes at runtime with: InvalidUpdateError: At key 'verify_result': Can receive only one value per step.

## Fix direction

In _add_output_field, when node.outputs is dict AND node has BOTH Each and Oracle, route to the Each×Oracle fusion state creation (tagged collector + merge_dicts reducer per key), not the Oracle-only path. Also fix make_eachoracle_redirect_fn to handle per-key state fields.
close_reason: Fixed: state model + redirect + barrier handle dict-form fusion

description:
Stage, commit, and close the task.

## Instructions

title: Optional[X] crashes _types_compatible with TypeError
description:
outputs=Optional[Claims] causes TypeError: issubclass() arg 1 must be a class. typing.Union is not a class, but the code calls issubclass(typing.Union, target) at _construct_validation.py line 474.

PEP 604 (X | None via types.UnionType) works fine. Only typing.Optional[X] / typing.Union[X, Y] crashes.

Fix: Add Union/Optional unwrapping at the top of _types_compatible, BEFORE the producer_origin block:
- Producer is Union: strip None, check all non-None members are compatible with target
- Target is Union: strip None, check producer is compatible with any non-None member

This also fixes the Optional[X] consumer false-positive rejection (same root cause, target-side).

Fixtures: tests/check_fixtures/known_gaps/type_optional_producer_crash.py, type_optional_consumer.py

title: @node outputs= inference from return annotation
description: When @node is used without outputs=, infer from -> T. When both present and disagree, ConstructError. Simplifies the common scripted case to just a return annotation. See architecture-v2 DX consistency discussion.
close_reason: Implemented outputs= inference from -> T. Mismatch raises ConstructError. 13 new tests + 2 check_fixtures. 1164 tests pass.

title: run() does not re-inject input fields into config on Operator resume path
description: When run(graph, input=..., config=...) is called initially, _inject_input_to_config merges input fields (node_id, project_root) into config[configurable]. But on resume (run(graph, resume=..., config=...)), _inject_input_to_config is NOT called. The original invoke_config lacks these fields. This causes FromInput DI resolution to fail for all nodes that run AFTER an Operator interrupt. Discovered in piarch UC pipeline: write-si merge works (before interrupt), write-overview/mss/brainstorm merges fail (after resume). Fix: store original input in config on first call, re-inject on resume. See runner.py lines 63-68.
close_reason: Fixed: run() stashes input in config, re-injects on resume. Test: test_from_input_resolves_after_resume.

neograph-otg0 shipped di.py with DIBinding but integration is partial:

2. ParamResolution is still dict[str, tuple[str, Any]] — should become dict[str, DIBinding] or list[DIBinding]
3. _classify_di_params still produces (kind_string, payload) tuples — should produce DIBinding objects
4. lint.py still has 4x duplicated check pattern — should use _check_binding(node_label, binding, config, issues) helper per architecture-v2.md

title: Cost callback: expose per-call token usage to host applications
description: NeoGraph extracts usage_metadata (input_tokens, output_tokens) from LangChain responses in invoke_structured and invoke_with_tools, but only logs them to structlog. Host applications (piarch) have their own CostTracker but cannot capture NeoGraph-initiated LLM calls. Needed: cost_callback parameter on configure_llm() that NeoGraph calls after each LLM invoke with (tier, model, input_tokens, output_tokens). Evidence: piarch BR-RW-036 run shows total_cost=0 because 809/813 LLM calls go through NeoGraph framework, bypassing piarch CostTracker.

title: Tighten Construct.input/output annotation from Any to type[BaseModel] | None
description:
MED-11 from code review 040426_2207 (PP-11).

src/neograph/construct.py:69-70 declares:
  input: Any = None   # type[BaseModel] | None
  output: Any = None  # type[BaseModel] | None

The comment states the real type. Pydantic v2 with arbitrary_types_allowed=True accepts type[BaseModel] | None directly (verified: pydantic accepts a BaseModel subclass as a type[BaseModel] value). Leave Node.input as Any because of its dict[str, type] case — that's a legitimate union.

Fix construct.py only:
  input: type[BaseModel] | None = None
  output: type[BaseModel] | None = None

Gives better IDE autocomplete and a more honest field contract.

Source: .claude/code-review/040426_2207/synthesis.md MED-11
close_reason: Wave 3 refactors. MED-02 extracted split_each_path helper in modifiers.py; both construct.py static walker and compiler.py runtime walker consume it. MED-06 adopted msg=...; raise idiom for 4 ConstructError sites. MED-09 rewrote _source_location using sys._getframe (dropped inspect import). MED-10 hardened _resolve_field_annotation to return _MISSING for unresolved ForwardRef/str leaks. MED-11 tightened Construct.input/output to type[BaseModel] | None. MED-07 added explanatory comment above _format_no_producer_error documenting the intentional multi-line divergence. 102/102 passing; examples 04/10 continue to run (ForwardRef resolution intact).

title: Loop + dict-form inputs: first iteration passes empty list instead of None for self-reference key
description:
## Bug

When a Node with Loop modifier has dict-form inputs (e.g. inputs={'refine': Draft, 'seed': Config}), the first iteration receives an empty list [] for the self-reference key instead of None.

## Root cause

In factory.py:_extract_input, the Loop re-entry check (line 506-545) only fires when own_val is a truthy list. On first iteration, own_val is [] (empty append-list from state initialization), which is falsy, so the check is skipped. Execution falls through to the fan-in dict path (line 556+), which reads the raw state field 'refine' and gets the empty list []. The unwrap check (line 566) doesn't fire because empty lists are falsy. So [] is passed to the user function as the value for the self-reference key.

## Impact

User code expecting Draft | None for the self-reference key on the first iteration gets [] instead. This causes AttributeError if the code tries input_data['refine'].text or similar attribute access. The user must add defensive checks for empty lists, which contradicts the typed contract.

## Reproduction

Node with inputs={'refine': Draft, 'seed': Config}, outputs=Draft, Loop modifier. On first iteration, input_data['refine'] is [] instead of None.

## Fix direction

In the fan-in dict path of _extract_input, when a Loop node reads its own append-list field and gets an empty list, it should return None for that key instead of the raw empty list. Add: if isinstance(value, list) and not value and node.has_modifier(Loop) and state_key == own_field: value = None
close_reason: Fixed: empty list [] treated as None on first iteration

title: Oracle merge_fn not firing after generators complete in construct_from_functions pipeline
description:
In piarch's RW pipeline, decompose uses Oracle(n=3, merge_fn='group_claims'). Three generators run and complete (confirmed in logs — 3x node_start/node_complete for decompose). But the merge barrier node (merge_decompose) never fires. The pipeline skips directly from the last decompose completion to flatten-groups, which receives empty/null input and the pipeline ends with no claims.

Log evidence (logs/16c91187.jsonl):
- Events 16-24: 3x decompose start + complete with LLM calls (47s, 76s, 100s)
- Event 25: flatten-groups starts IMMEDIATELY (no merge event in between)
- Event 26: flatten-groups completes in 0.0s (empty result)
- Event 27: pipeline completes at 108s (should be ~25 minutes for full pipeline)

The graph compiles correctly — compile logs show 'merge_decompose' as a node. But at runtime, the Oracle barrier doesn't collect the 3 generator results.

This worked in the previous neograph version (before the construct_from_functions Construct-acceptance fix in neograph-dqe). The dqe fix may have broken Oracle barrier assembly.

Pipeline: construct_from_functions with @node + sub-construct.map(). The Oracle is on decompose, NOT inside the sub-construct. The sub-construct is verify_claim (explore→score).
notes:
## Reproduction

Minimal repro — pure neograph, no piarch dependencies:

```python
from pydantic import BaseModel
from neograph import (
    node, construct_from_functions, compile, run,
    configure_llm, register_scripted, merge_fn,
    ToolInteraction,
)

class Input(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    text: str

class Claims(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    items: list[str]

class Merged(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    final: list[str]

class SubIn(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    claim: str

class SubOut(BaseModel, frozen=True):
    result: str

# Fake LLM
class FakeLLM:
    def with_structured_output(self, model, **kw):
        self._m = model
        return self
    def invoke(self, msgs, **kw):
        return self._m(items=['claim-1', 'claim-2'])

configure_llm(
    llm_factory=lambda tier, **kw: FakeLLM(),
    prompt_compiler=lambda t, d, **kw: [{'role': 'user', 'content': 'test'}],
)

# Oracle node — 3 generators + merge
@merge_fn
def merge_claims(variants: list[Claims]) -> Merged:
    all_items = [i for v in variants for i in v.items]
    return Merged(final=list(set(all_items)))

register_scripted('merge_claims', merge_claims)  # if needed

@node(outputs=Merged, model='fast', prompt='decompose', ensemble_n=3, merge_fn='merge_claims')
def decompose() -> Merged: ...

@node(outputs=list[str])
def flatten(decompose: Merged) -> list[str]:
    print(f'flatten received: {decompose}')  # <-- will show None or empty
    return decompose.final if decompose else []

# Sub-construct (to trigger the dqe code path)
@node(outputs=SubOut, model='fast', prompt='verify')
def explore(item: SubIn) -> SubOut: ...

@node(outputs=SubOut, model='fast', prompt='score')
def score(explore: SubOut) -> SubOut: ...

sub = construct_from_functions('sub', [explore, score], input=SubIn, output=SubOut)

# Parent: @node + sub-construct in one construct_from_functions call
pipeline = construct_from_functions('test', [
    decompose, flatten, sub.map('flatten', key='claim'),
])

graph = compile(pipeline)
result = run(graph, input={'node_id': 'test'})
print(f'Result keys: {sorted(result.keys())}')
print(f'flatten output: {result.get("flatten")}')
```

Expected: merge_claims runs after 3 decompose generators, flatten receives Merged with items.
Actual: flatten receives None/empty, pipeline completes in seconds.

The bug is triggered by having BOTH an Oracle node AND a sub-construct in the same construct_from_functions call. Remove the sub-construct and Oracle works fine.
## Reproduce Attempt
- Wrote 2 regression tests covering both failure modes (Oracle before sub-construct, Oracle after sub-construct)
- BOTH PASS — bug not reproducible with current code (commit 655f8eb, the dqe fix)
- The Oracle barrier assembly is not affected by the sub_by_field dict or topo sort changes
- Possible consumer-side issue: stale install, topology detail not captured, or LLM/prompt setup
- Tests committed as regression guards regardless
## Trace Similar
- Checked all Oracle-related compiler code in compiler.py (_add_node_to_graph Oracle path)
- The dqe changes are purely in decorators.py (_build_construct_from_decorated)
- The compiler receives the final ordered list and processes Node vs Construct independently
- No path from the dqe changes to Oracle barrier wiring — they're in different layers
- The all_known adjacency dict adds sub-construct keys but doesn't modify how Node keys are processed
## Architect Review
- [LOW] Tests cover both orderings (Oracle before/after sub-construct)
- [LOW] Bug not reproducible — likely consumer environment issue
- Recommendation: close as cannot-reproduce, keep regression guards

Overall: ALL_LOW
## Root cause found

The compiled graph only has edges for the first 3 nodes:
```
__start__ → build-catalog → register-artifacts → read-node → __end__
```

ALL other nodes (decompose, merge_decompose, flatten-groups, classify, cluster, etc.) exist in the graph but have ZERO edges connecting them. The Oracle conditional edges were never compiled.

The Mermaid output confirms: only static edges up to read-node, then __end__. The 15 remaining nodes are orphaned.

This is NOT an Oracle-specific bug. It's a compiler edge-wiring bug — the compiler stops adding edges after the first few nodes when construct_from_functions contains a mix of @node items + Construct.map() items.

## Reproduce more specifically

Get the Mermaid from: compile(pipeline).get_graph().draw_mermaid()

Expected: 18 nodes with edges forming the full pipeline topology.
Actual: 3 nodes connected, 15 nodes orphaned, __end__ after read-node.
## Root Cause Analysis (corrected)
- The pipeline DOES work end-to-end at runtime
- Oracle fires: 3 generators → merge → merged result flows to downstream nodes
- The Mermaid rendering (graph.get_graph().draw_mermaid()) omits conditional edges — this is a LangGraph visualization limitation, NOT a neograph edge-wiring bug
- Oracle uses add_conditional_edges which Mermaid renders as dotted lines or omits entirely depending on LangGraph version
- The consumer's logs showing 'flatten-groups starts IMMEDIATELY (no merge event)' needs further investigation on their specific topology (piarch's 18-node pipeline), but our repro shows the pattern works correctly
- Regression guards committed — 2 tests covering both orderings, both pass
close_reason: Not a neograph bug — Oracle+sub-construct works at runtime. Mermaid rendering omits conditional edges (LangGraph visualization issue). 2 regression guards in place.

title: DI type validation: isinstance check in DIBinding.resolve()
description:
After retrieving a value from config, validate it matches the declared inner_type before returning.

For scalar DI (FROM_INPUT, FROM_CONFIG):
  val = _get_configurable(config, self.name)
  if val is not None and not _isinstance_safe(val, self.inner_type):
      raise ExecutionError(f"FromInput param '{self.name}' expects {self.inner_type.__name__}, got {type(val).__name__}")

_isinstance_safe must handle: basic types, Optional/Union (unwrap None), generic origins (list, dict — check base type), BaseModel subclasses.

For bundled DI (FROM_INPUT_MODEL, FROM_CONFIG_MODEL): Pydantic already validates during construction, but per-field type check before construction would give better errors.

title: KILLER: Construct.forward() subclass mode for branching logic
description:
Source: DSPy Module.forward, PyTorch nn.Module
Effort: Large (v2 project)
Breaks API: No (new class-based mode)

KILLER IDEA #2. Kills Operator(when='string_key') string dispatch entirely. This is where LangGraph's core pain lives.

Current:
  validate = Node(...) | Operator(when='has_failures')
  # 'has_failures' is a string key into _condition_registry — no type checking

Proposed:
  class Analysis(Construct):
      decompose = Node.produce(output=Claims, ...)
      classify  = Node.produce(output=Classified, ...)
      report    = Node.produce(output=Report, ...)

      def forward(self, topic: str) -> Report:
          claims = self.decompose(topic)
          classified = self.classify(claims)

          # CONTROL FLOW IS JUST PYTHON
          if classified.confidence < 0.7:
              claims = self.decompose(topic, temperature=1.0)
              classified = self.classify(claims)

          if any(c['severity'] == 'critical' for c in classified.issues):
              neograph.interrupt({'reason': 'critical', 'items': classified.issues})

          return self.report(classified)

NeoGraph traces forward() once at compile time (like torch.fx) to build the compiled StateGraph. At runtime, executes like the declarative version.

Wins:
- Conditionals are Python 'if' — no Operator(when='...')
- Loops are Python 'for'
- try/except works
- Type checker sees everything (classified.confidence is a real attribute)
- Testability: instantiate Analysis(), call .forward(topic) directly with fakes

Prior art: torch.fx, jax.make_jaxpr, dspy tracing.

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #6

## Resolved design decisions (2026-04-04 spike)

Spike doc: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md`
Prototype: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/spike.py` (passes — traced forward() produces nodes list equivalent to declarative Construct)

**Tracing strategy: Symbolic Proxy (torch.fx-style).** At compile time, `ForwardConstruct.__init__` discovers `Node` class attributes via `cls.__mro__` walk, builds a `_ForwardSelf` shim whose `__getattr__` returns recording callables, and invokes `forward()` once with a stand-in `Proxy` input. Each node call records an entry; the ordered entries become the `nodes` list passed to `Construct.__init__`, which feeds the existing `compile()` pipeline unchanged.

**Why not AST walking:** brittle on REPL/notebook-defined classes, forces re-implementation of Python name resolution, diverges the IR for control flow. Proxies get name resolution, helper-call pass-through, and straight-line-in-same-IR for free. See design doc for full rationale.

**Why not hybrid:** doubles surface area before proving the simpler version. Proxies can reach conditional support via `__bool__` interception (torch.fx precedent).

**Coexistence with declarative Construct:** `ForwardConstruct` is a subclass of `Construct`. Both styles share one compile pipeline. Users pick per-pipeline.

**MVP scope:** straight-line only. Branches, loops, try/except each get their own subtask with their own design discussion.

## Subtasks

1. neograph-di0 — ForwardConstruct base class + class-attribute node discovery (P2, blocked-by: none)
2. neograph-3us — symbolic-proxy tracer for straight-line forward() (P2, blocked-by: neograph-di0)
3. neograph-vxv — compile() integration for traced ForwardConstruct (P2, blocked-by: neograph-3us)
4. neograph-w5z — Python `if` conditional branching via Proxy.__bool__ (P2, blocked-by: neograph-vxv) — this is the subtask that kills `Operator(when='...')` string dispatch
5. neograph-m8n — Python `for` loop support via Proxy.__iter__ (P3, blocked-by: neograph-w5z)
6. neograph-xi0 — try/except support in forward() tracing (P3, blocked-by: neograph-w5z)

This epic stays open until subtasks 1–4 land (MVP + killer conditional feature). Subtasks 5–6 may defer.
close_reason: ALL 6 SUBTASKS CLOSED. ForwardConstruct epic complete: base class + node discovery (di0), symbolic proxy tracer (3us), compile() integration with zero compiler changes (vxv), Python if branching via Proxy.__bool__ + re-trace (w5z), for-loop via __iter__ → Each (m8n), try/except v1 documented limitation (xi0). Users can write forward() with if/for/try and compile to LangGraph. 195 tests.
