title: Architecture: Hypothesis property-based tests for pipeline topology invariants
description: Pattern 3 has 10 historical bugs from dict-form × single-type × modifier combinatorial explosion. Manual test enumeration cannot keep up. Fix: Hypothesis generates random valid pipeline topologies (random modifier combinations, random I/O shapes) and asserts invariants: output matches declared type, no silent None, token count non-negative, compile+run round-trip succeeds. Runs on every CI push.
notes:
Research complete. Key findings:
- ModifierCombo enum has 10 valid combos (BARE, EACH, ORACLE, LOOP, OPERATOR, EACH_ORACLE, EACH_OPERATOR, ORACLE_OPERATOR, LOOP_OPERATOR, EACH_ORACLE_OPERATOR)
- Invalid combos: Each+Loop, Oracle+Loop (mutual exclusion)
- Node.scripted() needs register_scripted() for fn name
- compile() validates: LLM config, tool registrations, output_strategy, checkpointer for Operator
- compile_state_model creates typed state with reducers per modifier combo
- effective_producer_type: Each->dict[str,T], else->T
- classify_modifiers returns (ModifierCombo, dict)
- describe_graph returns Mermaid string
- Tests use register_scripted with lambda fns for scripted nodes
## Architect Review
- [LOW] Strategy should build from ModifierCombo enum to ensure exhaustive coverage. Using st.sampled_from(ModifierCombo) is the right approach.
- [LOW] Keep invariants simple for v1: (1) compile doesn't crash, (2) run produces non-None output, (3) output isinstance matches declared type. Add token/cost invariants in v2.
- [LOW] Use register_scripted with lambda fns returning model instances. Each test generates a unique registry key to avoid collisions.

Overall: ALL_LOW
close_reason: Hypothesis property tests: 4 invariants × 5 combos + 5 deterministic. 1231 tests pass.

title: describe_type includes fields with exclude=True in BAML schema
description:
## Problem
describe_type() iterates model_fields and renders ALL fields, including those with exclude=True. These are pipeline-internal fields that the LLM should never produce.

Example: ExtensionCondition.assigned_letter has exclude=True (set by pipeline, not LLM). But describe_type includes it in the output schema. R1 sees it, tries to produce it, returns null, validation fails.

## Fix
In describe_type.py line ~193, check field_info.exclude:
```python
for field_name, field_info in model.model_fields.items():
    if field_info.exclude:
        continue  # skip pipeline-internal fields
```

Same for describe_value (line ~375).
notes:
## Research
5 sites iterate model_fields without checking exclude:
1. describe_type.py:125 _count_classes
2. describe_type.py:193 _render_model_body (PRIMARY)
3. describe_type.py:375 _render_instance
4. renderers.py:77 XmlRenderer._render_model
5. renderers.py:160 DelimitedRenderer._render_model (needs refactor to access field_info)
JsonRenderer NOT affected (uses model_dump_json which respects exclude).
Zero existing tests for exclude=True fields.
close_reason: Fixed: 5 sites now check field_info.exclude. 3 new tests. 1328 pass.

title: TDD: typed tool result flows end-to-end
description:
Write FAILING end-to-end tests that prove typed_result flows from tool invocation through compile→run to downstream node consumption. Tests must use real compile() + run() pipeline execution, not mock assertions on internal functions.

## Test 1: test_typed_result_preserved_when_tool_returns_pydantic_model
File: tests/test_pipeline_modes.py, TestGatherToolCollection class

Setup: Full pipeline — @node(mode='agent') with FakeTool that returns a Pydantic model
- Define ToolResult(BaseModel, frozen=True) with typed fields
- FakeTool.invoke() returns ToolResult(...)
- ReActFake scripts 1 tool call → final structured output
- Pipeline: single agent node, compile(), run()

Assertions on the PIPELINE RESULT (not internal state):
- result['explore_tool_log'] is a list with 1 ToolInteraction
- result['explore_tool_log'][0].typed_result is not None
- isinstance(result['explore_tool_log'][0].typed_result, ToolResult)
- result['explore_tool_log'][0].typed_result.field_name == expected_value
- result['explore_tool_log'][0].result contains valid JSON (not Python repr)

## Test 2: test_typed_result_flows_to_downstream_node_when_gather_produce_chain
File: tests/test_composition.py, TestGatherProduceSubConstruct class

Setup: Full gather→produce pipeline inside sub-construct (the piarch pattern)
- FakeTool returns ToolResult model
- Capturing prompt compiler records what downstream produce node receives
- compile() + run() the full parent pipeline

Assertions:
- Downstream prompt compiler's input_data['explore_tool_log'] contains ToolInteraction instances
- Each ToolInteraction.typed_result is the original ToolResult model
- The produce node received structured data, not repr strings

## Test 3: test_string_tool_result_backward_compat
File: tests/test_pipeline_modes.py

Setup: FakeTool returns a plain string (existing behavior)
- compile() + run()

Assertions:
- tool_log[0].typed_result == 'found it' (the string itself, not None)
- tool_log[0].result == 'found it' (unchanged from current behavior)

## CRITICAL: These tests must FAIL before neograph-iura is implemented
The tests exercise typed_result which exists on the model (from neograph-y1v1) but is never populated by invoke_with_tools yet. So typed_result will be None → assertions fail → TDD red confirmed.

## NOT ALLOWED
- No mocking invoke_with_tools or _make_tool_fn
- No testing internal functions directly
- No pytest.mark.xfail
- Every test must use compile() + run() on a real pipeline

title: Design project.neograph.schema.json (project surface)
description:
## What
JSON Schema for the project surface file — types, tools, and models that pipeline specs reference.

## Schema must cover
- types: name → JSON Schema object (fields with types). Maps to Pydantic/Zod.
- tools: name → description + params schema. Tool implementations are language-specific.
- models: name → provider + model ID + optional config (temperature, etc.)

## Approach
- JSON Schema draft 2020-12
- Types use JSON Schema type definitions (string, number, integer, boolean, list, nested objects)
- Tool params are JSON Schema objects
- Model configs are freeform dicts with known keys

## Deliverable
src/neograph/schemas/project.neograph.schema.json + tests
design:
## Architecture
JSON Schema draft 2020-12. Three top-level properties: types (additionalProperties: JSON Schema object), tools (additionalProperties: {description, params}), models (additionalProperties: {provider, model, config}). File: src/neograph/schemas/project.neograph.schema.json
notes:
## Findings
- Types section maps name → JSON Schema object definition (fields with standard JSON Schema types)
- Tools section maps name → description + params (JSON Schema object)
- Models section maps name → provider + model ID + optional config
- This is the 'inventory' that pipeline specs reference by name

## Core Invariant
Every name referenced in a pipeline spec must resolve to an entry in the project surface.
close_reason: JSON Schemas implemented in 0c24eca. 10 validation tests pass.

title: Replace modifiers: list[Modifier] with ModifierSet typed slots
description:
Phase 3 of architecture-v2.md section 2 (Type-Safe Modifiers). BREAKING CHANGE.

## What
Replace modifiers: list[Modifier] with ModifierSet typed slots.

## Design
See docs/design/architecture-v2.md section 2 (REVISED).

REVISED from review:
- ModifierSet.model_post_init rejects illegal combos (Each+Loop, Oracle+Loop)
- Every match block MUST end with case _ as unreachable: assert_never(unreachable) — HARD REQUIREMENT
- .map() must produce ModifierSet.with_each(Each(...)): self.model_copy(update={"modifier_set": ...})
- Sidecar invariant: model_copy produces new id(), sidecar must be re-registered on the copy. This is UNCHANGED from current list[Modifier] behavior — document it, dont break it.
- modifiers: list[Modifier] used in compile logging (compiler.py:80) and _BranchNode.has_modifier/get_modifier duck typing (forward.py:358). Both need migration.
- Programmatic API: Node(modifiers=[Oracle(), Each()]) must become pipe syntax. Clear error if someone passes modifiers= list.

## Migration (phased)
Phase 2 bridge: add modifier_set field, keep modifiers as computed property (backward compat)
Phase 3: remove modifiers property, all code uses modifier_set

## Impact
- 56 has_modifier/get_modifier calls → modifier_set.each / modifier_set.oracle
- 210 pipe-syntax tests: unchanged
- 2 check fixtures with modifiers=[...]: update
- Compile logging: log modifier_set.combo instead of modifier list
- _BranchNode: implement has_modifier/get_modifier on ModifierSet (always returns False/None)

## Verification
- All 1076+ tests pass
- assert_never in every match block
- grep -c "has_modifier\|get_modifier" src/neograph/ = 0
- mypy passes with no unhandled ModifierCombo variants
notes:
## Audit findings to incorporate (2026-04-10)

6a. This task needs a step-by-step migration plan:
Step 1: Add modifier_set: ModifierSet field to Modifiable (Node + Construct inherit)
Step 2: Rewrite __or__ to update modifier_set instead of appending to list
Step 3: Add computed property modifiers -> list[Modifier] from modifier_set (backward compat bridge)
Step 4: Rewrite .map() to use modifier_set.with_each()
Step 5: Rewrite has_modifier/get_modifier to delegate to modifier_set
Step 6: Update compiler.py logging (line 85-87) to log modifier_set.combo
Step 7: Update _BranchNode — it has modifiers=[] duck typing (forward.py:354). Either give it a ModifierSet(BARE) or keep a shim.
Step 8: Update 2 check fixtures that use modifiers=[...] syntax
Step 9: Remove computed property bridge (Phase 3 breaking change)

6c. _BranchNode.modifiers = [] is duck-typed. Under ModifierSet, _BranchNode needs modifier_set = ModifierSet() or a has_modifier/get_modifier shim that returns False/None.

2b. Keep _validate_node_chain belt-and-suspenders checks even after ModifierSet.model_post_init. Document as defense-in-depth (catches programmatic bypass of __or__).

title: Signature-detection mechanism documented incorrectly
description:

Actual implementation (src/neograph/_llm.py:39-52, 105-137) inspects the callable once with inspect.signature at configure_llm() time and filters kwargs per call. There is no try/except retry loop.

Fix: rewrite as 'The framework inspects your callable at registration time (inspect.signature), then passes only the kwargs it accepts on each call. Accepts **kwargs functions.' Source: code review issue #9.

title: Silent bug: multiple port params overwrite — should raise ConstructError
description:
In _build_construct_from_decorated (decorators.py:1078-1083), when a node has multiple params whose type matches construct_input, the dict rewrite loop executes filtered['neo_subgraph_input'] = v for each port param. The last one wins, earlier values are silently dropped.

Example:
  @node(outputs=Result)
  def bad(a: VerifyClaim, b: VerifyClaim) -> Result: ...

  construct_from_functions('x', [bad], input=VerifyClaim, output=Result)

Both 'a' and 'b' match construct_input. The rewrite writes neo_subgraph_input twice. Only the last param's type is kept. No error, no warning.

Fix: in the port_params identification loop (lines 917-935), if len(ports) > 1 for any node, raise ConstructError explaining the ambiguity. Add a test in TestNodeSubConstruct.

Also needs tests for:
- No matching param: construct_input set but no node param matches (silent no-op, should it warn?)
- Peer-priority edge case: param name matches both a peer @node AND construct_input type (code correctly prefers peer at line 927-928, but no test verifies)

File: src/neograph/decorators.py lines 917-935
Test: tests/test_composition.py
notes:
## Reproduction
- Test: tests/test_composition.py::TestPortParamErrors::test_raises_when_multiple_params_match_construct_input
- Status: FAILING (confirmed bug — DID NOT RAISE ConstructError)
- Two params of type VerifyClaim in one @node, construct_from_functions(input=VerifyClaim) silently accepts both, last one wins in the rewrite
- Peer priority test passes (line 928 'if pname in decorated: continue' works correctly)
## Trace Similar
- The port_params identification loop (decorators.py:920-935) is the only place where construct_input matching happens
- No similar pattern elsewhere in the codebase (fan_out_params identification at L921-933 has a different structure — it collects per-node, not per-type)
- The fix is localized: add a len(ports) > 1 check after the inner loop, raise ConstructError
## Architect Review
- [LOW] Fix location correct: decorators.py lines 934-935, after ports set is built, before storing in port_params dict
- [LOW] Error message should include the node name, the matching param names, and the construct_input type
- [LOW] Peer-priority edge case already handled correctly (line 928)

Overall: ALL_LOW

title: Node.inputs/outputs typed as Any — largest type-safety gap in IR
description: Node.inputs: Any and Node.outputs: Any persist. Every consumer must isinstance-check. Comment says type[BaseModel] | dict[str, type] | None but annotation is Any. With Pydantic v2 BeforeValidator or TypeSpec Union, this could be properly typed. Single largest type-safety gap in the IR.
design:
## Architect Decision: SMALL ANNOTATION FIX

The validator was fixed in m91y — it rejects garbage at runtime. The remaining gap is:
- Static annotation type | dict[str, type] | None lies about what validator accepts
- Generic aliases (list[X]) and string dict values pass validation but fail type checking

Fix: Change TypeSpec to Annotated[Any, PlainValidator(_validate_type_spec)] with docstring listing valid forms. This is honest about the static limitation while the validator does real work.

Scope: 1 file change (node.py), ~5 lines. No behavioral change.
Triage: ALL_LOW — minimal, safe change.
notes:
## Research Findings

### Current state (already improved)
TypeSpec = Annotated[type | dict[str, type] | None, PlainValidator(_validate_type_spec)]
- _validate_type_spec is now a REAL validator (fixed in neograph-m91y)
- Rejects garbage: strings, ints, lists
- Accepts: None, type, generic aliases (list[X], dict[str,X], X|None), dict[str, type|str]

### The gap is static typing only
The static annotation type | dict[str, type] | None doesn't match what the validator accepts:
- Generic aliases (list[X]) are NOT type instances — they're GenericAlias
- Dict values can be str (decorator fallback, loader before resolution)
- Two # type: ignore[return-value] in _validate_type_spec evidence this

### PEP 747 TypeForm would solve this but isn't available yet

### Options
A. Widen annotation to include GenericAlias types — references typing._GenericAlias (private)
B. Use Any with PlainValidator — honest about static limits, validator does real work
C. Keep current — pragmatic, validator works, accept type: ignore comments

### Recommendation: Option B (Annotated[Any, PlainValidator])
- Honest: static annotation is Any (which it effectively already is)
- PlainValidator does the real validation at runtime
- Add docstring documenting valid forms
- Small, safe change

### Core invariant
PlainValidator(_validate_type_spec) is the single enforcement point. All three API surfaces pass through it at Node construction time.
close_reason: TypeSpec annotation fixed to Annotated[Any, PlainValidator]. Honest about static limits.

title: forward: compile() integration for traced ForwardConstruct
description:
Wire the traced `ForwardConstruct` end-to-end through the existing `compile()` pipeline and verify the resulting StateGraph behaves identically to the equivalent declarative `Construct(nodes=[...])`.

## Context

- Depends on the tracer subtask.
- Design: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md` — "Emitted IR" section. The whole point of tracing into the existing IR shape is that this subtask should need **zero** changes to `compiler.py` or `state.py` for the MVP. If it does, that is a bug in the tracer, not an excuse to fork the compiler.

## Files

- Verify no changes needed to: `src/neograph/compiler.py`, `src/neograph/state.py`, `src/neograph/_construct_validation.py`, `src/neograph/factory.py`.
- If changes ARE needed, they should be additive guards only (e.g. skipping Proxies that leak into modifier fields) — get review before expanding scope.
- `tests/test_forward_construct_compile.py` — new end-to-end test file.

## Acceptance criteria

1. Build a `Pipeline(ForwardConstruct)` with two scripted Nodes chained straight-line in `forward()`. Compile it via `compile(pipeline)`. Assert the compiled graph's nodes match the declarative equivalent one-for-one (same node names, same topology).
2. Run the compiled graph with a seed input and verify the final state has the same shape as the declarative equivalent.
3. Run the declarative equivalent `Construct("pipeline", nodes=[decompose, classify])` through `compile()` and `invoke()`; assert the two runs produce equal final states given the same input.
4. `compile_state_model(pipeline)` returns a Pydantic model with the same fields in the same order as the declarative version.
5. The construct-level `_validate_node_chain` fires for a misconfigured ForwardConstruct (e.g. a classify node whose `input` type is incompatible with the decompose output). Same error, same source-location pointer.
6. Test uses only `Node.scripted(...)` nodes and fakes — no real LLMs.

## Out of scope

- Branching, looping, interrupt support.
- Modifiers on traced nodes. For now if a user attaches `Oracle` / `Each` / `Operator` to a class-attribute Node, it should pass through unchanged (they're attached to the Node, not the trace). Add one sanity test but do not expand the modifier story.
close_reason: Straight-line ForwardConstruct MVP complete. di0: base class with node discovery via __mro__ walk + Pydantic ignored_types fix. 3us: symbolic proxy tracer (_Proxy + _Tracer + _ForwardSelf + _NodeCall) wired into ForwardConstruct.__init__. vxv: zero compiler changes — tracer output is a plain nodes list that compile()/state-builder/factory consume unchanged. 10 new tests across 3 classes. 169/169 passing.

title: forward: Python 'if' conditional branching via Proxy.__bool__
description:
Add Python `if` support to `ForwardConstruct.forward()` tracing by intercepting `Proxy.__bool__`. This is the subtask that actually kills `Operator(when='string_key')` dispatch — it's what the epic is pointing at.

## Context

- Depends on the end-to-end compile integration.
- Design: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md` — "How control flow maps" (if section) and "How the Proxy type works". Note the open question about re-trace vs exception-rewind; this subtask needs to pick one and document the choice.
- Prior art: torch.fx's `concrete_args` re-trace pattern. Read torch/fx/proxy.py `TraceError` handling before starting.

## Files

- `src/neograph/forward.py` — add branch interception to `Proxy` and branch-recording state to `Tracer`.
- Potentially new IR: `BranchConstruct` or a modifier that lowers to `graph.add_conditional_edges`. This subtask gets to decide; the decision goes in a doc-comment with rationale.
- `src/neograph/compiler.py` — new lowering path for the branch IR to LangGraph conditional edges.
- `tests/test_forward_construct_branching.py` — new test file.

## Signatures

Sketch — the implementer may refine:

```python
class Proxy:
    def __lt__(self, other): return _ComparisonProxy(self, '<', other)
    def __gt__(self, other): return _ComparisonProxy(self, '>', other)
    # __le__, __ge__, __eq__, __ne__ similarly
    def __bool__(self):
        tracer = _active_tracer()  # contextvar
        return tracer.record_branch(self)  # returns True first, then False on re-trace
```

Strategy to pick (document in design-note comment):
- **Re-trace**: `Tracer.record_branch` returns True, runs forward() to completion, then resets and re-runs with False for that branch id. torch.fx approach.
- **Fork-on-demand**: single trace, on __bool__ spawn a cloned tracer that explores both arms. More complex, handles nested branches symmetrically.
Pick one; explain why in the module docstring.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `forward()` with `if self.classify(x).confidence < 0.7: self.decompose(x)` compiles to a StateGraph with a conditional edge after `classify`, same topology as the declarative `classify | Operator(when='low_confidence')` equivalent.
2. Both arms of the branch run correctly at runtime (test with seed inputs that take each arm).
3. Nested `if` works (at least two levels deep).
4. `elif` / `else` work.
5. Predicate supports: attribute chains (`x.confidence`), numeric comparisons (`<`, `>`, `==`), and `and`/`or` between comparisons. Short-circuit semantics documented.
6. A predicate that references a non-proxy value (e.g. `if some_constant:`) still works — don't over-intercept.
7. Failure mode: a predicate that we can't represent (e.g. calls a user function on a Proxy) raises `TracerError` with a pointer to the `forward()` line.

## Out of scope

- `for` loops, `try/except`, `while` — separate subtasks.
- Branch depth > 4 (bail with a clear error; 4 is arbitrary but bounded to keep re-trace cost sane).
- Interaction with `Oracle` / `Each` modifiers on nodes inside branches.
close_reason: Python if branching via Proxy.__bool__ + re-trace strategy. _Proxy gains __getattr__ (child proxies for attribute chains), 6 comparison dunders returning _ConditionProxy, __bool__ delegating to tracer. Re-trace strategy: run forward() with all branches True, then re-run with each branch flipped to False, diff traces to identify true-only/false-only/shared nodes. _BranchNode sentinel in node list carries _BranchMeta (condition spec + arm nodes). Compiler gains _add_branch_to_graph lowering _BranchNode to add_conditional_edges with a runtime router that navigates state field + attribute chain and applies the comparison. Join node for convergence. State builder extended for branch arm nodes. 18 new tests in tests/test_forward_construct_branching.py. 187/187 passing.

title: Compiler drops edges: nodes after first chain terminate at __end__
description:
## Bug Summary

The NeoGraph compiler fails to wire dependency edges between nodes when constructing a LangGraph StateGraph. Only the first linear chain of nodes gets connected -- all subsequent nodes that depend on earlier outputs are disconnected and the graph terminates prematurely at __end__.

## Reproduction

Minimal example with 3 sequential nodes where step_b depends on step_a and step_c depends on step_b (via parameter names matching function names):

Expected edges: __start__ -> step-a -> step-b -> step-c -> __end__
Actual edges: __start__ -> step-a -> __end__

step_b and step_c are compiled as graph nodes but have no incoming edges.

## Real-world impact

The UC derivation pipeline in piarch has 19 nodes across 4 phases. When compiled, only the first 3 nodes execute (read-graph-context -> read-draft -> research-comparable -> __end__). The remaining 16 nodes (write-si Oracle x3, validate, lint, review, write-overview Oracle x3, write-mss, brainstorm-extensions, write-extensions Each, derive-business-rules Each, validate-completeness Operator, lint-artifacts, register-and-activate, create-downstream-nodes, impact-cascade) have no incoming edges.

The RW ingestion pipeline (8 nodes) has the same bug -- only first 3 nodes connected.

## Root cause hypothesis

The compiler resolves dependencies from function parameter names (e.g. def step_b(step_a: dict) means step_b depends on step_a). Either the dependency resolver only processes the first node outputs and stops, or the edge builder only connects the linear chain from __start__ and marks everything else as terminal.

## Environment

neograph 0.2.0.dev0, Python 3.12, LangGraph latest, macOS Darwin 25.2.0

## Severity

P0 blocker -- no multi-node construct can execute beyond the first chain. Makes NeoGraph unusable for any real pipeline.
design:
## Root Cause
_wire_oracle and _wire_each in compiler.py call graph.add_conditional_edges(prev_node, router) without a path_map argument. The router functions return Send() objects (not string node names). LangGraph's get_graph() requires either type hints on the router return value or a path_map to determine static edge targets. Without these, the visualization shows source -> __end__ and drops all downstream edges.

## Fix Approach
Pass path_map=[gen_name] to add_conditional_edges in _wire_oracle (line ~403) and path_map=[fan_name] in _wire_each (line ~464). This tells LangGraph what nodes the Send() dispatches target, making the edges visible in get_graph().
notes:
## Update: get_graph() display bug, NOT execution bug

The execution actually works correctly. The pipeline ran through 14+ nodes including Oracle parallel (x3), merge, Each fan-out (x18 extensions). The get_graph().edges output is misleading — it shows only 4 edges, but the actual LangGraph execution correctly follows all dependency chains.

The pipeline failed at write-extensions because the LLM returned wrong schema (file_content instead of ExtensionHandling fields) — a prompt/model issue, not a compiler issue.

Downgrade from P0 to P3: get_graph() visualization is inaccurate but execution works.
## Reproduction
- Failing tests: tests/test_pipeline_modes.py::TestGetGraphEdges::test_oracle_edges_visible_in_get_graph and test_each_edges_visible_in_get_graph
- Root cause: _wire_oracle and _wire_each call add_conditional_edges(source, router) without path_map. LangGraph's get_graph() cannot resolve Send() targets statically, so it shows source -> __end__ and drops all downstream edges.

title: Document ConstructError multi-line error format as deliberate package divergence
description:
MED-07 from code review 040426_2207 (CON-03).

13 existing errors across the package are single-sentence period-terminated 'Subject "name" problem. Remediation.' messages (see state.py:63, compiler.py:92, factory.py:57). New ConstructError messages in construct.py are multi-line with embedded newlines, 'hint:' lines, and 'at file:line' suffixes.

The richer format is defensible — assembly-time type errors benefit from structured help. But the divergence is novel and breaks reader expectations.

Two options:
1. Add a short comment near _format_no_producer_error explaining the structured-help exception, so future additions to state.py/compiler.py can adopt it deliberately.
2. Promote the pattern: refactor state.py/compiler.py errors to also use structured help (hints, source locations). Larger but more consistent.

Recommended: option 1 (low-friction). Consider option 2 as a separate enhancement.

Source: .claude/code-review/040426_2207/synthesis.md MED-07
close_reason: Wave 3 refactors. MED-02 extracted split_each_path helper in modifiers.py; both construct.py static walker and compiler.py runtime walker consume it. MED-06 adopted msg=...; raise idiom for 4 ConstructError sites. MED-09 rewrote _source_location using sys._getframe (dropped inspect import). MED-10 hardened _resolve_field_annotation to return _MISSING for unresolved ForwardRef/str leaks. MED-11 tightened Construct.input/output to type[BaseModel] | None. MED-07 added explanatory comment above _format_no_producer_error documenting the intentional multi-line divergence. 102/102 passing; examples 04/10 continue to run (ForwardRef resolution intact).

title: Typed tool results: tools return models, not strings
description:
Typed tool results: tools return models, not strings. Parent epic for the implementation.

## The problem
_llm.py:367 does result=str(result) — tool returns a Pydantic model but it gets stringified into ToolInteraction.result: str. The original 1bp design says 'tool results should stay typed, renderer converts at the last mile.' The implementation threw away the types at the first mile.

## The fix
1. ToolInteraction gets typed_result: Any = None (keeps result: str for backward compat)
2. invoke_with_tools stores the original object in typed_result, renders with model_dump_json for ToolMessage
3. Downstream nodes receive list[ToolInteraction] with both .result (str) and .typed_result (original model)
4. Tests prove typed_result is preserved end-to-end

## Files affected
- src/neograph/tool.py (ToolInteraction model)
- src/neograph/_llm.py (invoke_with_tools)
- tests/test_pipeline_modes.py (existing tool tests)
- tests/test_composition.py (e2e tests)
- tests/test_fakes.py (ToolInteraction assertions)
notes:
## Lineage

This feature was designed as part of neograph-1bp (Node.outputs refactor epic, now closed). The full design is in neograph-1bp's DESIGN and NOTES fields — multiple design decisions documented:

1. 'Demand-Driven Collection' — tool results collected ONLY when downstream declares dependency
2. 'Renderer Pipeline for Tool Results' — same rendering as any input, describe_type + XmlRenderer/etc
3. 'N Named Outputs' — dict[str, type] mirrors dict[str, type] inputs
4. 'Why NOT Message History' — typed end-to-end, not prose-level ToolMessage strings

The implementation shipped 7 subtasks (1bp.1 through 1bp.7) but the typed tool result part was never completed:
- 1bp.6 collected ToolInteraction but with result=str (stringified)
- The design says 'tool results should stay typed, renderer converts at the last mile'
- The implementation does str(result) at the first mile (_llm.py:367)

This issue completes the original 1bp design — the last missing piece.

Cross-reference: bd show neograph-1bp for full design context (DESIGN + NOTES fields).
close_reason: typed_result field + invoke_with_tools implementation + 3 e2e tests. 405 passing. Mutation-tested.

title: forward: try/except support in forward() tracing
description:
Add `try/except` support to `ForwardConstruct.forward()` tracing. Maps Python exception handling to retry/fallback topology in the compiled StateGraph.

## Context

- Depends on conditional branching (uses the same re-trace / predicate machinery).
- Design: `.claude/spikes/neograph-pub/design.md` — "How control flow maps" (try/except section). Flagged as needing its own design pass; this subtask MUST start with a short design note in `src/neograph/forward.py`'s module docstring explaining the chosen mapping before implementation.

## Files

- `src/neograph/forward.py` — add try-scope recording.
- `src/neograph/compiler.py` — lowering path for try-IR to conditional edges driven by "node raised exception X".
- `tests/test_forward_construct_try_except.py` — new test file.

## Acceptance criteria

1. `try: self.a(x) except SomeError: self.fallback(x)` compiles to a topology where `a` is followed by a conditional edge that routes to `fallback` iff `a` raised `SomeError`.
2. Multiple except clauses work (match order, first-wins).
3. `except Exception:` bare catch works.
4. `try/except/else` works — the `else` branch executes only when no exception was raised.
5. `finally:` works — nodes in `finally` always run regardless of arm.
6. A `try` with no handler for a raised exception re-raises at runtime.
7. A `try` whose body has no Node calls is accepted (traces to empty try-scope, no topology change).

## Out of scope

- Exception chaining (`raise ... from ...`).
- Catching exceptions raised by the framework itself (`ConstructError`, `TracerError`); these bypass user handlers.
- Retry semantics (that's a separate modifier / subtask).
close_reason: for-loop via __iter__ lowering to Each (m8n, 4 tests) + try/except v1 pragmatic documentation (xi0, 4 tests). 195/195 passing. neograph-pub epic complete — all 6 subtasks closed.

title: construct_from_module with input=/output= has zero tests
description:
construct_from_module (decorators.py:775) gained input=/output= kwargs alongside construct_from_functions. The code works — both pass through to _build_construct_from_decorated with construct_input/construct_output. However, there are zero tests exercising construct_from_module with these kwargs.

Need: at least one test that builds a sub-construct via construct_from_module(mod, input=X, output=Y) and verifies port param resolution works. Pattern: create a types.ModuleType, attach @node functions, call construct_from_module with input=/output=.

File: tests/test_composition.py or tests/test_node_decorator.py (where construct_from_module tests live).

title: DIBinding.payload: object is untagged union
description: Comment documents variants (CONSTANT: any value; FROM_INPUT: bool; MODEL: tuple[type, bool]) but type is object. Access by index (self.payload[0]) with runtime asserts. Proper tagged union via dataclass subclasses or @overload would catch errors at mypy time.
notes:
## Research
### Current state:
- DIBinding.payload: object — 5 construction sites, 4 different value shapes
- resolve() uses isinstance(self.payload, tuple) + self.payload[0] indexing
- Payload semantics: CONSTANT→any, FROM_INPUT/CONFIG→bool(required), MODEL→tuple(type,bool), FROM_STATE→type

### Construction sites (4 files):
1. _di_classify.py:212 — MODEL: payload=(inner_type, required)
2. _di_classify.py:218 — FROM_INPUT/FROM_CONFIG: payload=required (bool)
3. decorators.py:791 — CONSTANT: payload=p.default (any)
4. decorators.py:797 — FROM_STATE: payload=hint (type)
5. _construct_builder.py:353 — CONSTANT: payload=p.default (any)

### Consumers of payload:
1. di.py:152-154 — MODEL: assert isinstance(tuple), payload[0], payload[1]
2. di.py:188 — CONSTANT: return self.payload
3. di.py:193 — FROM_STATE: passed to _unwrap_loop_value as expected_type

### Observation:
- FROM_INPUT/FROM_CONFIG payload is redundant — it's just a copy of self.required
- MODEL payload duplicates (model_cls=inner_type, required=self.required)
- The ONLY payload that carries unique info is CONSTANT (the default value)
- FROM_STATE payload is inner_type (already stored in self.inner_type field!)

### Fix approach:
1. Add model_cls: type | None = None field for MODEL kinds
2. Add default_value: Any = None field for CONSTANT kind
3. Remove payload field
4. Update 5 construction sites + 3 resolve() access sites
5. Core invariant: DI resolution semantics must not change — same values, same errors
## Architect Review
- Risk: LOW. Mechanical replacement of untyped field with typed fields.
- The payload field is redundant for FROM_INPUT/FROM_CONFIG (copies required) and FROM_STATE (copies inner_type).
- Only CONSTANT and MODEL carry unique data.
- Approach: add default_value (for CONSTANT) and model_cls (for MODEL). Drop payload.
- Edge case: lint.py reads DIBinding — check it doesn't access payload.
- Triage: ALL_LOW — straightforward refactor.
close_reason: payload: object replaced with typed default_value + model_cls fields

title: Architect review of neograph-s14

4. **Pattern compliance**: Does the plan follow project architecture patterns?

title: _add_subgraph silently drops Operator modifier when Oracle or Each is present
description: In compiler.py _add_subgraph(), the Oracle branch (line 254-258) and Each branch (line 260-262) return immediately without checking for a co-existing Operator modifier. This means Construct | Oracle(...) | Operator(...) and Construct | Each(...) | Operator(...) silently drop the Operator — no interrupt check node is added to the graph. Compare with _add_node_to_graph() which correctly checks 'if operator:' after every modifier branch. Confirmed with live test: modifiers list shows [Oracle, Operator] but compiled graph has zero operator nodes. Fix: add 'if operator: last_name = _add_operator_check(graph, last_name, operator)' before returning from the oracle and each branches, mirroring lines 307-309 in _add_node_to_graph.
close_reason: Fixed: Operator chained after Oracle/Each in _add_subgraph

title: Extract split_each_path shared helper for Each.over parsing
description:
MED-02 from code review 040426_2207 (DRY-02 + LR-01). Flagged independently by 2 agents (dry + layering).

Each.over dotted-path parsing is implemented twice:
- src/neograph/construct.py:176 — walks types via model_fields (static check)
- src/neograph/compiler.py:265 — walks values via getattr (runtime router)

Both do each.over.split('.') and treat parts[0] as root, parts[1:] as segments. If Each.over ever grows indexing, wildcards, or escaping, the walkers will drift. The whole point of assembly-time validation is to prevent runtime surprises — diverging parsers defeat that goal.

Fix: extract a small helper (e.g. in modifiers.py near Each, or a new _paths.py):
  def split_each_path(over: str) -> tuple[str, tuple[str, ...]]:
      parts = over.split('.')
      return parts[0], tuple(parts[1:])

Both walkers call it; each keeps its own type-vs-value stepping loop.

Source: .claude/code-review/040426_2207/synthesis.md MED-02
close_reason: Wave 3 refactors. MED-02 extracted split_each_path helper in modifiers.py; both construct.py static walker and compiler.py runtime walker consume it. MED-06 adopted msg=...; raise idiom for 4 ConstructError sites. MED-09 rewrote _source_location using sys._getframe (dropped inspect import). MED-10 hardened _resolve_field_annotation to return _MISSING for unresolved ForwardRef/str leaks. MED-11 tightened Construct.input/output to type[BaseModel] | None. MED-07 added explanatory comment above _format_no_producer_error documenting the intentional multi-line divergence. 102/102 passing; examples 04/10 continue to run (ForwardRef resolution intact).

title: _extract_json grabs wrong braces when prose contains balanced braces before JSON
description:
In _llm.py:_extract_json (lines 229-267), the function finds the first '{' via text.find('{') and counts braces from there. When LLM response prose before the actual JSON contains balanced braces (e.g., math formulas, code snippets), the function extracts the prose fragment instead of the JSON object.

Repro:
  text = 'The formula is {a + b} = c. Here is the result: {"key": "value"}'
  _extract_json(text)  # Returns '{a + b}' instead of '{"key": "value"}'

This flows through _parse_json_response which tries json_repair on '{a + b}', fails Pydantic validation, and raises ExecutionError. In the retry path, the LLM gets unhelpful error feedback about the wrong extracted content.

Severity: P3 because this only affects the 'text' output strategy (which explicitly handles LLM responses with prose wrapping), and most LLMs don't produce balanced braces in prose before JSON. But DeepSeek and open-source models sometimes do.

Fix options:
1. Try all top-level balanced brace pairs, parse each with json.loads, return first valid JSON
2. Look for '{"' (opening brace + quote) as a stronger JSON start heuristic
3. Try from the LAST balanced '{...}' if the first one fails parsing

title: @node decorator: loop_when= / max_iterations= / loop_to= kwargs
description:
Sugar kwargs on @node that compile to | Loop(...).

## File: src/neograph/decorators.py

In the @node decorator, after the Operator section:

    # -- Loop when loop_when is set -----------------------------------------
    if loop_when is not None:
        from neograph.modifiers import Loop as _Loop
        loop_kwargs = {'when': loop_when, 'max_iterations': max_iterations or 10}
        if loop_to is not None:
            loop_kwargs['reenter'] = loop_to
        if on_exhaust is not None:
            loop_kwargs['on_exhaust'] = on_exhaust
        n = n | _Loop(**loop_kwargs)
        _register_sidecar(n, f, param_names)
        if param_res:
            _register_param_resolutions(n, param_res)

New kwargs on node():
    loop_when: str | Callable | None = None,
    max_iterations: int | None = None,
    loop_to: str | None = None,
    on_exhaust: Literal['error', 'last'] | None = None,

## Acceptance criteria
- @node(outputs=Draft, loop_when=lambda d: d.score < 0.8, max_iterations=5)
- @node(outputs=Draft, loop_when='needs_work', loop_to='review')
- Equivalent to the | Loop(...) form
close_reason: Added loop_when, max_iterations, loop_to, on_exhaust kwargs to @node decorator in decorators.py. They compile to | Loop(...). All 420 existing tests pass.

title: Create _execute_node unified wrapper replacing 3 factory functions
description:
Phase 2 of architecture-v2.md section 1 (Factory Unification) — Step 2. DEPENDS ON: neograph-cadg (NodeInput/NodeOutput), neograph-bldf, neograph-yudg.

## What
Replace _make_scripted_wrapper, _make_produce_fn, _make_tool_fn with a single _execute_node that uses ModeDispatch.

## Where
factory.py — the 3 wrapper functions + make_node_fn dispatch.

## Design
See docs/design/architecture-v2.md section 1 (REVISED). The unified path:
1. Preamble: log_bind, _inject_oracle_config, _extract_input -> NodeInput, _apply_skip_when check, _extract_context
2. dispatch.execute(node, NodeInput, config, context) -> NodeOutput
3. Postamble: _build_state_update(NodeOutput), log complete

IMPORTANT from review:
- _apply_skip_when is NOT just a check — it has state-writing side effects (calls _build_state_update, increments Loop counter). If skip fires, return its result and skip dispatch entirely.
- _make_raw_wrapper stays separate but DOES have logging preamble/postamble. Document this: raw = no DI/input/output wrapping, YES logging.
- make_subgraph_fn is a 5th execution path — it is NOT covered by _execute_node. It is a composition wrapper, not a mode. Document as explicitly outside scope.
- Redirect wrappers (make_oracle_redirect_fn, make_eachoracle_redirect_fn) wrap the OUTPUT of make_node_fn. They are composition decorators, not replacements. They stay unchanged.

## How
1. Create _execute_node implementing preamble/dispatch/postamble
2. Handle skip_when BEFORE dispatch — if it returns non-None, return immediately
3. Create _dispatch_for_mode(node) -> ModeDispatch
4. Rewrite make_node_fn: raw -> _make_raw_wrapper, else -> closure calling _execute_node
5. Delete _make_scripted_wrapper, _make_produce_fn, _make_tool_fn
6. Run full test suite

## Verification
- grep -c "_make_scripted_wrapper\|_make_produce_fn\|_make_tool_fn" src/neograph/factory.py = 0
- All 1076+ tests pass
- New test: verify skip_when fires for scripted, think, and tool modes through the unified path

## Audit findings to incorporate (2026-04-10)

1b. Context extraction is mode-conditional. Currently _make_scripted_wrapper does NOT call _extract_context. The preamble in _execute_node should ONLY call _extract_context for think/tool modes, not scripted. Either: pass context_data=None for scripted, or check node.mode before extracting.

1d. _apply_skip_when has state-writing side effects — it calls _build_state_update internally and returns the full state update dict. If skip fires, _execute_node must return its result immediately and NOT call _build_state_update again in the postamble. The early return pattern handles this, but document it explicitly: skip_when is NOT a pure predicate.

title: Switch _source_location from inspect.stack() to sys._getframe
description:
MED-09 from code review 040426_2207 (PP-06).

src/neograph/construct.py:337-368 uses inspect.stack() to walk frames for the user-code source location. ~50x more expensive than necessary because inspect.stack() materializes source context for every frame.

Only invoked on the error path (confirmed), so not a happy-path concern — but on failing test suites that assemble many bad Constructs, overhead compounds.

Fix: rewrite using sys._getframe(1) + frame.f_back walk:
  def _source_location() -> str | None:
      try:
          frame = sys._getframe(1)
          while frame is not None:
              module_name = frame.f_globals.get('__name__', '')
              if not (module_name == 'neograph'
                      or module_name.startswith('neograph.')
                      or module_name.startswith('pydantic')):
                  fname = frame.f_code.co_filename
                  if fname and not fname.startswith('<'):
                      return f'{os.path.basename(fname)}:{frame.f_lineno}'
              frame = frame.f_back
      except Exception:
          return None
      return None

Eliminates the try/finally: del stack dance. Combine with the _construct_validation.py split (hygiene sweep) to also drop the inspect import from a declaration module.

Source: .claude/code-review/040426_2207/synthesis.md MED-09
close_reason: Wave 3 refactors. MED-02 extracted split_each_path helper in modifiers.py; both construct.py static walker and compiler.py runtime walker consume it. MED-06 adopted msg=...; raise idiom for 4 ConstructError sites. MED-09 rewrote _source_location using sys._getframe (dropped inspect import). MED-10 hardened _resolve_field_annotation to return _MISSING for unresolved ForwardRef/str leaks. MED-11 tightened Construct.input/output to type[BaseModel] | None. MED-07 added explanatory comment above _format_no_producer_error documenting the intentional multi-line divergence. 102/102 passing; examples 04/10 continue to run (ForwardRef resolution intact).

title: Labeled step syntax à la Beam: 'name' >> Node.produce(...)
description:
Source: Apache Beam 'Label' >> transform
Effort: Small
Breaks API: No (additive sugar)

Beam's pattern: every step has a human-readable label inline. Cheap consistency win — node names always visible at the left edge, searchable in code.

Proposed:
  pipeline = Construct('rw',
      'decompose' >> Node.produce(output=Claims, model='reason')
    | 'classify'  >> Node.produce(input=Claims, output=Classified)
    | 'verify'    >> Node.gather(input=Cluster, output=VerifyResult).map(...)
  )

Implementation: overload str.__rshift__ via Node classmethod that binds the name. str >> Node(...) calls a factory that sets .name.

Minor win, but reads well. Skip if we do #1 (param-name inference) since that removes the need for explicit names.

Source: .claude/research/dx_research_040426.md #10
close_reason: SUPERSEDED by @node + ForwardConstruct. Labeled step syntax ('name' >> Node.produce(...)) was for inline human-readable labels in big pipelines. With @node, the function name IS the label. With ForwardConstruct, self.decompose(topic) is self-documenting. Both primary APIs make explicit label syntax redundant.

title: Checkpoint resume does not work — graph restarts from beginning despite existing checkpoint
description:
## Problem
When a pipeline crashes and is rerun with the same thread_id and SqliteSaver checkpoint DB, LangGraph should resume from the last completed superstep. Instead, it restarts from the beginning (all scripted + research nodes re-execute).

## Evidence
Run 1ba5bff6: runner detects existing checkpoint DB, logs 'resume', but execution starts from read-graph-context (the first node) instead of write-overview (the crash point).

## LangGraph behavior
Per LangGraph docs, calling graph.invoke(input, config={'configurable': {'thread_id': 'same-id'}}) with the same thread_id and existing checkpoints should automatically resume. NeoGraph's run() wraps this but may interfere.

## Suspects
1. NeoGraph run() re-injects input fields into config via _inject_input_to_config — this might cause LangGraph to see it as a new execution
2. The input dict might differ between runs (e.g., different object IDs for Pydantic models) causing LangGraph to treat it as a new thread
3. NeoGraph's compile() might produce a different graph ID between runs

## Expected
Rerun with same thread_id + checkpoint DB should skip all completed nodes and resume from the failed one.
design:
## Fix
1. Remove the ValueError guard when both input and resume are None
2. When input=None and resume=None: call graph.invoke(None, config=config) — LangGraph resumes from checkpoint
3. Keep _inject_input_to_config for the input= path (new execution)
4. File separate bug for _neo_input persistence across process restarts
5. Add test: compile with MemorySaver, run halfway, run again with input=None + same thread_id → resumes
notes:
## Reproduce: CONFIRMED

### Root cause
run() always passes input dict to graph.invoke(). LangGraph's _first() method (pregel/_loop.py:633) checks is_resuming = self.input is None or input_is_command or run_id matches. When input is a dict, all three are False → LangGraph starts fresh.

### Why Operator resume works
resume= passes Command(resume=...) → input_is_command=True → is_resuming=True

### Fix
Option B (cleanest): When input=None and resume=None and config has thread_id, pass None to graph.invoke(). Remove the ValueError guard. 'No input + existing thread = resume from checkpoint.'

### Secondary issue
_neo_input DI stash is in-memory only. Won't survive process restarts. Needs to be persisted in checkpoint metadata. File as separate bug.

### Core invariant
run(graph, input=None, config={'configurable': {'thread_id': X}}) with existing checkpoints must resume, not restart.
## Reopened (2026-04-13)

Still not resuming. Two consecutive runs:
- logs/66ce18fd.jsonl: killed mid-run at brainstorm-extensions (step 35)
- logs/5d2070c2.jsonl: rerun — logs 'neograph_uc_develop_resume' but starts from read-graph-context (step 0)

Both have same thread_id 'neo-uc-develop-BR-UC-001'. SqliteSaver checkpoint DB exists at .derive-ensemble/checkpoints/neo-uc-develop-BR-UC-001.db between runs.

The scripted nodes (read-graph-context through read-draft) complete in <1s so they LOOK like they're replaying, but the research Oracle x3 nodes re-execute full LLM calls (50+ minutes), proving no checkpoint resume.
## Reproducible test case (2026-04-13)

```python
"""Minimal reproduction: checkpoint resume after crash.

Run this script. It should:
1. First run: execute node-a, node-b, crash at node-c
2. Second run: skip node-a and node-b, resume from node-c
"""
import sqlite3
import tempfile
from pathlib import Path

from langgraph.checkpoint.sqlite import SqliteSaver
from neograph import node, construct_from_functions, compile as neo_compile, run


call_log = []


@node(outputs=str)
def node_a() -> str:
    call_log.append("a")
    return "result-a"


@node(outputs=str)
def node_b(node_a: str) -> str:
    call_log.append("b")
    return f"result-b({node_a})"


crash_count = 0


@node(outputs=str)
def node_c(node_b: str) -> str:
    global crash_count
    call_log.append("c")
    crash_count += 1
    if crash_count == 1:
        raise RuntimeError("Simulated crash on first attempt")
    return f"result-c({node_b})"


construct = construct_from_functions("test-resume", [node_a, node_b, node_c])

db_path = Path(tempfile.mkdtemp()) / "checkpoint.db"
config = {"configurable": {"thread_id": "test-thread-1"}}

# Run 1: should crash at node_c
print("=== Run 1 (should crash at node_c) ===")
call_log.clear()
with SqliteSaver.from_conn_string(str(db_path)) as checkpointer:
    graph = neo_compile(construct, checkpointer=checkpointer)
    try:
        run(graph, input={}, config=config)
    except Exception as e:
        print(f"Crashed as expected: {e}")
    print(f"Nodes called: {call_log}")
    assert "a" in call_log and "b" in call_log and "c" in call_log, "All nodes should have been called"

# Run 2: should resume from node_c (skip a, b)
print("\n=== Run 2 (should resume, skip a and b) ===")
call_log.clear()
with SqliteSaver.from_conn_string(str(db_path)) as checkpointer:
    graph = neo_compile(construct, checkpointer=checkpointer)
    result = run(graph, input={}, config=config)
    print(f"Nodes called: {call_log}")
    print(f"Result: {result}")

if "a" in call_log or "b" in call_log:
    print("\n❌ FAIL: node_a or node_b were re-executed — checkpoint resume not working")
else:
    print("\n✅ PASS: only node_c re-executed — checkpoint resume works")
```

Output:
Run 1: crashes at node_c as expected (a, b, c all called)
Run 2: a, b, c ALL re-called — checkpoint not honored

Expected Run 2: only c called (a and b skipped from checkpoint)

Same SqliteSaver DB file, same thread_id, same compiled graph.
LangGraph docs say invoke() with same thread_id should auto-resume.
close_reason: Fixed for real: _has_existing_checkpoint detects saved state, routes to invoke(None) for resume while keeping input in config for DI. Test passes with input={} on resume.

title: _add_subgraph has no Each×Oracle fusion path for Constructs
description: In compiler.py _add_subgraph(), when a Construct has both Oracle and Each modifiers (Construct | Oracle(...) | Each(...)), the 'if oracle:' branch fires first and handles it as pure Oracle — the Each modifier is silently ignored. Compare with _add_node_to_graph() which has an explicit 'if oracle and each:' check (line 305) that routes to _add_each_oracle_fused(). There is no equivalent Each×Oracle fusion implementation for Constructs. Confirmed with live test: Construct with [Oracle, Each] modifiers compiles a graph with only Oracle merge nodes and no Each barrier nodes. Options: (1) implement fusion for Constructs, or (2) reject the combination at __or__ time with a clear error. Given the complexity of M×N fusion on sub-constructs, option 2 is safer.
close_reason: Fixed: clear CompileError instead of silent drop
